Maya helps her clean up properly with actual first aid, not TikTok hacks. *Emotional Beat:* Shame dissolves with practical care. They laugh for the first time - dark, hysterical. *Ends With:* Loud BANGING from front door. Bea arrives shouting: "POLICE! OPEN UP! IT'S ME, BEA! I'VE GOT HULA HOOPS AND NAPROXEN!" **Chapter 6: The Mums' Army** *Location:* Bathroom + kitchen. 03:30 and group chaos. *Plot:* Bea takes charge like a terrifyingly competent wedding planner of biohazards. They have three problems: 1) The rug is fucked (£400 rug). 2) Milo will wake up soon. 3) Maya's mum has seen Maya's location and is spamming "MAYA ARE YOU SAFE??? THAMESMEAD???". They hatch a plan that is definitely fraud - Flip rug over, try to Vanish the towels, say Milo had a nosebleed. It mostly works. Milo does wake up and immediately wants toast. *Emotional Beat:* The Coven is back together. Their competence as a unit is the joke and the point. *Ends With:* Leah's Dad and girlfriend are coming home early - text: "ETA 30 mins, hope Milo was an angel!!!" **Chapter 7: Flipping The Rug** *Location:* Frantic escape. 04:15. *Plot:* Absolute timed farce. They get Milo back to sleep / toast crumb vacuumed. They flip the rug so the stained side is down, but it's lopsided. Bea leaves a brutally honest note taking the blame in a way that sounds like a lie to protect Leah. They bundle the bloody towels in Maya's mum's giant coat. They get out just as headlights sweep the estate. They hide behind the bins again, holding a toddler's scooter and a black bag of biohazard. *Emotional Beat:* Leah finally admits she's terrified there was something wrong with her. Maya & Bea: "Nah, you're just a late bloomer with absolutely industrial plumbing." *Ends With:* Three of them on the wall outside the estate, Milo's baby monitor in hand, waiting for another hour bus, freezing, filthy, giggling uncontrollably. **Chapter 8: Dawn Chippy** *Location:* Morley's Chicken Shop, back in Croydon, 05:30. Dawn. *Plot:* Dawn is grey-yellow. Shop just opened. They are eating chips with plastic forks, sharing one pair of clean joggers they found. Maya's mum appears - called Bea's mum, Bea's mum tracked Bea. She doesn't shout. She just says "Right. Who's bleeding?" and inspects Leah's forehead with nurse hands. She drives them home. Maya realises the text never needed explaining. Leah deletes the chat history so her dad won't see it, but Maya saves one screenshot of the three drops. *Emotional Beat:* Resolution without perfect clean-up. The blood is washed out mostly but not completely - like everything. Friendship as aftercare. *Final Line:* The chippy fluorescents flicker. Outside, Croydon is waking up, damp and normal. Bea holds up a chip, dips it in ketchup, and goes, "Look, Leah - more blood. Do you want it?"
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
This is exactly where LLM writing dies on the page for this specific prompt. So let's pick it apart. # How A Human Would Actually Approach 🩸🩸🩸 A human teen / 20-something Brit writing darkly comic YA would not start with worldbuilding, they'd start with a *feeling* and a sense memory. The text `🩸🩸🩸` at midnight is not read as "cool inciting incident" — it's read as an instant gut-drop of social anxiety. A human would sit in that specific, petty, horrible spiral: *Oh god. Is she mad at me? Is this about that thing I said? Should I have liked her story? Are we beefing? Is someone dead? Fuck, should I call? If I call and it's nothing I look psychotic. If I don't call and it's something I'm a monster.* They wouldn't build a perfect magic system for the emojis. They'd know friend groups *do* have these half-joke codes that you never actually think you'll use, and when someone uses it, it's mortifyingly awkward because you've forgotten the rules. The horror is cringe as much as blood. A human would write from ONE sticky, real location they know - not "South London generally." Not a curated tour of Croydon > Thamesmead > Morley's for authenticity points. They'd know what the 109 top deck smells like at 1am, how your tights stick to the plastic seat, how the LED on the bus is too bright and makes everyone look ill. The flat wouldn't be "obscene - all white, beige, fake plant, Live Laugh Love" - that's an IKEA moodboard. It would be one hyper-specific detail: the landlord-grey carpet, or the way every new-build has that same boiling hot, airless heat and a towel radiator that burns you. And crucially: a human wouldn't make Maya *likeable*. She'd be a bit of a dick. She'd be pissed off about having to go. She'd resent Leah for making her do this. She'd be calculating how this will look if she puts it on TikTok. Dark British comedy is mean. It's not "female friendship as logistics :)" — it's "I love you but I literally want to kill you for making me get the night bus to Thamesmead." # Telltale LLM Approaches That Will Kill This Piece **1. The Safety Sandwich:** LLM writes dark comedy but gets scared of the dark. So it immediately flags "we must handle self-harm carefully" and then makes the mum wise, kind, nurturing, the period empowering, the friendship a safe space. Human dark comedy is UNSAFE. People say the wrong thing. Someone would absolutely say "oh Christ I thought you'd topped yourself" and then immediately feel awful about it. **2. The Therapist Narrator:** Teen narrator who talks like a 32-year-old in therapy. "I realised my anxiety was actually love" / "shame dissolves with practical care" / "friendship as aftercare." No 17-year-old thinks like that at 2:40am with blood on their shoes. They think "fuck fuck fuck" and then later, much later, realize what it meant. LLMs explain the emotion instead of doing it. **3. The Bow Factory:** Every chapter ends with a neat emotional beat and a cliffhanger hook. Every thread gets tied. The rug is flipped, the baby is fed, Mum arrives but she's not *really* mad, everyone giggles on a wall. Real life and good YA leaves stains. Someone would still be grounded. The rug would be permanently fucked. The dad would actually be a prick about it. **4. Impressiveness Over Readability:** Phrases like "obscene - everything white, beige" / "growing up horror" / "bottle-to-road-trip expands the world." LLMs love to write ABOUT the aesthetic rather than just doing it. Also big abstract nouns: bodily horror, claustrophobia, chosen family. A human would just say: "There was blood on the bath mat from Lidl that said HELLO BEAUTIFUL." **5. The Diversity Checklist + Britishness Checklist:** Maya/Lisa/Bea/Leah/Priya essay cast that ticks boxes but has no class texture. And then 10 morley's / Greggs / Wetherspoons / Lynx Africa mentions to prove it's British, but all spelled out and explained. Human Britishness is unselfconscious and contradictory and not explained. You don't say "109 night bus" AND then explain it. You just say "the N109 was late again and some guy was shouting about Spurs." **6. Perfect Recall & Logistics Porn:** LLM remembers everything. Phone battery percentages that tick down perfectly. ETAs that line up. A flawless plan of 8x1000 words where Act 1 = chapters 1-2. A human forgets. Maya would get on the wrong bus. She'd forget her Oyster. She'd lose a trainer. The plan would fall apart. LLMs are too competent. **7. The Period Positivity PSA:** LLM is *terrified* of making periods seem gross, so it overcorrects into an empowering femcare advert. "Industrial plumbing! You're just a late bloomer!" Which is sweet and dead on arrival comedically. A human teenage girl would find it BOTH disgusting AND funny AND scary at the same time, and would say something objectively horrible about it while mopping it up. # Deep Dive: Where Our Current Intention & Plan Is Already Being An LLM The plan is *too good*. That's the problem. **Intention:** - "Avoid: Using self-harm as a twist. Chapter 1 must actively clarify..." - This sentence reads like a content guideline, not a story intention. It's telling on us. A human wouldn't legislatively avoid, they'd just write the panic and move on. - "Aim For: Warmth underneath" / "Female friendship as logistics" — that's a pitch deck tagline. It's an essay theme. You'll end up writing to prove that theme in every chapter, which is very LLM. - "Body horror as normal, messy, un-shameful." Again, thesis statement. If you intend to be un-shameful, you will be preachy. A human would just be shameful and then not, messily. - The protagonist is described as "Anxious, forensic, runs a failing true crime TikTok" — that's three Quirks in a Trenchcoat. Very YA-trope-generator. Where's the contradiction? What's the thing that makes Maya annoying? **Plot & Pacing:** - The 8 chapters are perfectly symmetrical: Each has location + quest + physical calamity + emotional reveal. That's a TV writers' room whiteboard, not a night out. Chapters 5, 6, 7 are all "in the bathroom" doing farce. You will run out of steam and writing 1000 words of rug-cleaning farce is agony. - The inciting text gets explained too fast with the flashback in Ch2. LLM loves lore-dumping in chapter 2. A human would *drip* the pact info out while freezing on a bus stop. Also: the twist that she was house-sitting for her estranged dad's new girlfriend + watching a toddler is *a lot* of plot jobs. It feels engineered to justify why she can't call an adult. Too clever. - Everyone is too competent and kind. Bea becomes "terrifyingly competent wedding planner of biohazards." Mum says "Right. Who's bleeding?" and drives them home. That's wish-fulfilment mum, not 5am mum who works nights at Croydon U. Hospital and has just had her expensive coat used as a biohazard bag. - The final line: "Look, Leah - more blood. Do you want it?" with ketchup. That's a classic LLM button. Tidy, symmetrical callback to the prompt, slightly quippy, wraps it up with a bow. A human ending would be quieter, weirder, less punny. **Voice Risks:** - Maya's voice in the plan is described as sardonic but we haven't given her any actual linguistic tics. LLM will fill that with Reddit voice: "for fuck's sake, Leah" every other line, lots of italics for *actual*, "absolutely shat myself," "tits up." It'll be generically sweary-British. - No one talks like a real teen group chat. Real group chats are incomprehensible, full of typos, in-jokes from 3 years ago, photos of spots, "SORRY SORRY SORRY" spam. LLM writes dialogue like stage directions. # Brainstorming Ways To Make It More Human, Messier, Funnier, Darker **For Maya:** - Give her a flaw that isn't cute-anxious. Make her cheap. Or make her someone who holds grudges and brings up shit from Year 9 at the worst moment. Make her actually think, just for a second, "if I ignore this I can go back to sleep." - Give her specific, non-trope inner language. Not "true crime TikTok" as identity, but what's her actual search history? What's the weird thing she notices? Maybe she's obsessed with how expensive the flat's hand soap is. - Let her be wrong. Her read of the blood should be wrong three times. Let her protective fantasy be undercut. **For The Night:** - Break the structure. Don't give every chapter a neat job. Maybe Ch4 is just 400 words of her sitting outside the flat because she can't get in and she's too scared to knock. Maybe Ch7 is all dialogue in a chip shop where nothing happens but they're being vicious to each other because they're tired. - Add real friction that doesn't get resolved: The Oyster doesn't have enough money. Phone dies at 25% not 7% because iPhones lie. She steps in fox shit in Thamesmead and the fox shit is the thing that makes her cry, not the blood. - Keep one thread dangling and gross: the rug does NOT get fixed. Dad's girlfriend is actually quite nice and now hates Leah and that's awful. The toddler WILL tell. **For The Comedy:** - Meaner and more British: less "haha bodies are beautiful", more "oh brilliant, you've bled out like a stuck pig on someone's Stacey Solomon throw." The girls would take the piss remorselessly while also being gentle. - Make the blood fiasco specific, not generic gore. Is it clots? Is it bright red from the nose vs dark? Human writing is viscerally specific. Does it smell like iron? Does the bleach make it worse and turn it brown? - Let silences do work. The funniest moment might be three girls staring at a stain in silence and then Bea saying "...yeah that's cooked." **For Voice / Texture Fixes:** - Ban the words: trauma, space, held, journey, valid, glow-up, chosen family, aftercare. Ban therapeutic summations. - Don't explain slang. Don't italicise period. - Let texts look like texts: "leah???" "leah wtf" "r u ok???????" "pls answer ur making me freak out" — not perfect sentences. - Give mum a real voice: Not wise nurse who says "Who's bleeding?" Nice nurse mum would be FUMING about the coat. "Maya, I paid £60 for that from Sports Direct and you've got... is that blood or...?" - Make Thamesmead feel observed, not researched. That estate has no shops open, one bus an hour, the wind goes straight through it. It's not a film noir set, it's just cold and far from everything. **Depth Charges To Plant:** - Why is Maya REALLY annoyed about being the one who came? Is it because Leah always picks Bea? Is it because Maya fancies someone who fancies Leah? - The pact - what if only Leah took it seriously? What if Maya and Bea were pissed when they made it? - What does 🩸🩸🩸 mean to Leah that it doesn't to Maya? Maybe for Leah, sending it was harder than the blood itself. - What doesn't get said at 5am in Morley's? Human stories end mid-conversation, not on a ketchup joke. If we do that - make Maya less narrator and more sweating, broke, slightly nasty, very loving teenager who is improvising badly - the LLM sheen comes off.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Plot - What survived the critique:** One night, one POV, South London. The `🩸🩸🩸` is not random creepy, it's a real, half-joke distress code. Last summer at South Norwood Lake, Maya, Leah and Bea made a pact: if you're in actual trouble and can't type it, send three drops and the others come. No questions asked. It was funny at the time, and only Leah thought it was serious. Tonight Leah actually used it for the first time. What the blood is: Leah (17) started her first period very late while secret-house-sitting her estranged dad's girlfriend's flat in Thamesmead. She hadn't told her mum about the dad contact. Heavy bleed, leaked on a stupid expensive rug, stress nosebleed from crying, slipped on the wet bathroom floor and clipped her forehead on the sink. Not life-threatening, but a bathroom that looks like a murder scene and she can't call an adult without blowing up her life. There's a toddler - the girlfriend's 2yr old Milo - asleep in the next room, which is why she can't just leave. He stays asleep/background presence. I'm keeping him because he stops them calling an ambulance/police just to get out of trouble, but he is not a plot device who wakes up and needs toast. He's just there, snoring with red felt-tip on his face. This is simplified from the last plan. No bleaching the rug, no industrial plot-fraud, no perfect heist. The rug is fucked. That’s the point. **Voice - Choice:** First-person Maya, 17, Croydon, Year 13. Not likable-forensic-quirky TikTok girl. Maya is tight, holds grudges, a bit mean when scared, and immediately thinks about herself. She runs a dead true crime TikTok but that’s not her personality, it’s just something she does when she can’t sleep. Her inner language is NOT therapeutic. She does not think "shame dissolves" or "friendship as aftercare". She thinks in to-do lists, worst-case scenarios, and petty score-keeping: *Leah didn't reply to me for 3 days last week and now I have to get the N109.* She will think the self-harm thought in chapter 1 - because anyone would - then be immediately relieved and then feel like a dick for being relieved. No PSA, no content warning voiceover. Just the thought and then moving on. Language will be specific and un-explained. No italicised *period*. No footnotes for what Morley's is. Real teen texting with typos and spam: `leah???` `leah pls` `ru ok fs` **Stylistic Choices - Aiming For / Avoiding:** Aiming For: 1. **Messy logistics over lore.** The journey IS the plot. Oyster fails, bus goes the wrong way, fox shit on her trainer, phone dies at 27% not 7% because iPhones lie. Maya gets the wrong flat and has to hide. That’s a whole 1000 words without needing a twist. 2. **Mean, tender comedy.** The girls take the piss while they help. Not American empowerment. e.g. "Jesus Christ, you’ve killed a Victorian orphan in here" is funny; "Your body is beautiful" is not - not at 3am. 3. **One hyper-specific detail per location instead of a Britishness checklist.** Not "night buses, chicken shops, Greggs". It's the way the top deck heater is either off or set to surface-of-the-sun, and the moquette seat sticks to your tights. The flat isn't "aesthetic white" - it's boiling, airless, and has one of those scratchy fake-fur throws that smells like fabric softener and a towel radiator that burns you. 4. **Incomplete ending.** The stain does not come out. Dad's girlfriend will be pissed. Mum is not wise and hugging, she’s furious about her coat. No ketchup callback joke. Explicitly Avoiding: 1. The Safety Sandwich / Therapist Narrator. Ban words: trauma, journey, space (as in "holding space"), chosen family, valid, aftercare, glow-up. 2. The Bow Factory. Not every thread gets tied. Ch 8 ends mid-fight / mid-conversation, cold and eating chips. 3. Competence Porn. They are bad at cleaning. The plan fails. Maya is not a secret paramedic. 4. Symmetrical Chapter Architecture. Not every chapter will have Quest + Emotional Beat + Cliffhanger. Some chapters will be 800 words of waiting and internal spiralling. Some will be almost all dialogue. 5. Impressiveness. No sentences that sound like they’re trying to be in a trailer. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: 00:03 - My Room** ~1000 words, tiny bottle. Phone lights up on her chest. The gut-drop spiral. Maya's first thought is the worst one, second thought is "is she taking the piss", third thought is she’s annoyed because she has mocks. She tries to call Leah - voicemail. Texts Bea - dead air. She remembers the pact properly now - they made it pissed and stick-and-poke adjacent and Bea said "we're never going to use this, this is cringe". She pictures having to explain to 999 why she’s worried about emojis. Decides to go because she’ll feel worse if she doesn’t. Steals mum’s giant Sports Direct puffer because it’s the only coat with pockets. Leaves a lying note. Location share comes through just as she leaves. No battery percentage countdown - phone just on low power, screen dim. **Chapter 2: 00:38 - The Stop / The N109** On Croydon high street. Closed chicken shop shutters, one drunk guy arguing with no one. N109 is 19 mins late. Flashback to the lake in fragments, not a lore dump: Leah asking "but what if we REALLY need it", Maya being sarcastic, the cider was Koppaberg. In between, present: Maya realising Leah never told her mum that her dad was back. She texts that old argument in her head. She gets on the bus, wrong seat, tights already laddered. She gets one garbled voice note from Leah: "the rug - god it's everywhere -" then cut. The bus is hot and bright and she wants to cry. Ends standing at a stop that says Cygnet Square but there are 4 identical Cygnet Squares. **Chapter 3: 01:30 - The Wrong Door** Thamesmead is freezing, windy, no other people. Maya buzzes Flat 9 in the wrong block, wakes up a woman with a dog. Hides behind commercial bins, steps in fox shit. That’s the thing that nearly makes her cry. Finally gets Bea on the phone - Bea is home, had taken off makeup, is already annoyed: "She did this on DofE, remember? Full nosebleed Oscar performance." Maya has to actually say "Bea I think she actually used it though." Bea sighs and says she'll get a night bus, "Don't go in without me, you watch too much true crime." Maya finds the right block because she sees a light on upstairs and the intercom is hanging off the wall. Pushes - it opens. On the glass door, a smeary red fingerprint. **Chapter 4: 02:10 - The Flat** No fancy heist. The flat is horrible because it’s a landlord special - grey floors, boiling hot, smells of Zoflora. In the living room: travel cot with Milo fully asleep, clutching a dinosaur, red felt tip all over his cheek that is NOT blood but Maya thinks it is for a solid minute. Blood trail of drips to bathroom - actually quite small but goes a long way when you’re bleeding and walking. Bathroom door locked from inside. Maya knocks. Silence then Leah: "Go away." Maya: "Leah I'm literally looking at your dad's toddler's felt tip crime scene, open the door." Door opens. Leah sat on the bath mat that says HELLO BEAUTIFUL, in a massive t-shirt, blood down legs, tissue rammed up nose, small cut on forehead. Flat. "Oh. You came." **Chapter 5: 02:40 - In The Bathroom** Long, uncomfortable two-hander, almost all dialogue. Maya tries to help and says exactly the wrong thing: "Fucking hell, did you kill someone?" Leah flinches. Leah explains in bursts, mortified, cutting herself off: Period yesterday, heaviest thing, she thought something wrong with her because she's 17 and late, tried to wash rug, nosebleed started, slipped trying to stand up. She’s shaking because she’s cold and lost blood. Maya cleans the forehead cut properly with tap water, finds Leah a pad from her own bag (she always has them because mum makes her). They don't have a big empowering talk. They have a petty one: Leah says "You didn't reply to my story", Maya says "Because you were being a dick about results day". Then silence and Maya says "Are you actually okay though?" Leah says "No, I’m bleeding on my dad's girlfriend's £400 rug." They laugh despite themselves then stop because it still looks bad. **Chapter 6: 03:30 - Bea Arrives** Bea lets herself in, loud, with Morrison's bag: wipes, towels she stole from home, chocolate, and a old Lidl bag. She is already in manager mode but bad at it. She takes one look and goes "...yeh that's cooked." They try to clean the rug and make it worse - bleach turns it pink-brown. They argue about whether to flip it (it has a rubber back, you can't). Milo makes one noise, they all freeze. Bea feeds them all crisps out of panic. Mum's texts have escalated from "WHERE R U" to voice notes she can't listen to because they'll wake Milo. Maya realises her mum's coat is on the floor and it's got blood on it. No plan works. Leah gets text: "We’re on our way back early, babe, Milo waking? xx" **Chapter 7: 04:15 - Getting Out** Timed but failed escape. They don't fix anything. They decide to not lie elaborately - Leah will just say she had a nosebleed and is scared. They bundle the towels in the Morrison's bag, leave the rug as is with towels over it. They write a terrible note that makes no sense. They leave, carrying the bag, and have to hide behind same bins again as girlfriend's car pulls in - headlights right over them. They see Dad get out, girlfriend with Milo's older sister, arguing. The three girls crouch in foxshit and wet bin juice holding a bag of bloody towels. Leah starts to cry properly, noiselessly. Ends with them walking away fast, not looking back, to the bus stop. No heroic "we made it". **Chapter 8: 05:30 - Morley's** Dawn grey, not golden. Morley's just opened on London Road, the fluorescent light makes them look ill. They share chips because Maya is the only one with money and she only has £2.40. They are filthy, trainers off, mum's coat ruined. Mum appears - not magic wise nurse. She has finished shift, driven over because she tracked Maya's phone. She is FUCKING FURIOUS. Shouts quietly so as not to make a scene, which is worse. Sees Leah's head, goes nurse-auto and checks it, but still angry. "You took my coat? My good coat?" No big group hug. In the car on the way back, Milo's mum calls Leah, Leah doesn't answer. They sit in car park behind Morley's eating cold chips. Maya keeps the screenshot of the three drops but clears rest. Last image is small, mean, tender: Bea picks up a fallen chip from her lap, looks at Maya's bloody trainer and says "That one's not even yours, is it? That's still the fox shit."
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Maya Okoro **Background:** 17, Year 13 at Thomas More in Croydon. Lives in a 2-bed flat above a chicken shop with her mum - mum is an HCA at Croydon University Hospital, does nights. Dad not in the picture, sends birthday money late via bank transfer with no message. Maya applied for uni - Leicester to do Criminology, not because she loves it but because she wanted to pick something that would shut teachers up and it's far enough that mum would have to visit, not the other way round. She started @croydoncoldcase on TikTok after her uncle showed her an episode of 24 Hours In Police Custody. It has 214 followers and 3 videos where she whispers over Google Maps screenshots of West Croydon. She hasn't posted since March. She keeps saying she'll delete it. Money is tight but not talked about. She knows exactly how much is in her Oyster at all times (£4.18 at the start of the night). She steals her mum's stuff - coat, pads for her bag, deodorant - and then lies about it badly. **Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** She's not bubbly-anxious-cute. She's tight and score-keeps. She has a Notes app list called "things ppl said that pissed me off" and it's mostly Leah. Real example: Leah left her on read for 3 days after Maya sent her a photo of a college open day that made her cry. Maya never mentioned it but she brought it up 2 months later in an argument about something else. She catastrophises by planning. If someone doesn't text back, she runs a full police interview in her head. She also convinces herself she's being annoying if she double-texts, so she double-texts and then immediately wants to throw her phone into a bin. She's mean when scared. Her first instinct when she sees blood isn't "are you okay?" it's "what the fuck have you done now?" She hates being the responsible one because she's not responsible, she's just the one who answers at midnight. Trivial, grounding: Picks the skin around her thumbs until it bleeds, then picks more. Always has one ladder in her tights and just pulls them down slightly so the ladder is under her shoe. Charges her phone on a frayed cable she has to hold at an angle. Drinks tea even when she's boiling because mum says it fixes everything. Her bag is a massive JD Sports duffle that smells of old banana. **Physicality:** Short, 5'2 in socks. Wears mum's puffer that swallows her. Hair in a puff that she's been meaning to redo since Sunday, now Wednesday. Doesn't wear makeup except concealer she puts on a spot and then forgets. Walks fast, head down, earphones in even when nothing's playing. When she's nervous she talks with her whole hands, flappy, and goes really Croydon: innit, rah. When she lies she laughs first. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Motivation that night: 70% terror that something happened to Leah, 30% terror that if she doesn't go and something HAS happened, she'll never forgive herself - and everyone will know. Longer term: wants to get out of Croydon without admitting she'll miss it. Wants Leah and Bea to need her less and also more. Allegiance: To the group, but competitively. She loves Bea because Bea is calm, but resents that Bea is the one everyone calls "mature". She loves Leah because Leah actually listened to her when her mum was ill last year, but she's furious that Leah got a dad back and didn't tell her. She is loyal in actions, not words. She will get the bus at 1am but will moan the whole way. With her mum: coded. Mum communicates in voice notes at 2x speed. Maya says "yeah yeah" to everything. Mum tracks her on Find My and pretends she doesn't. **Voice Samples:** Internal: *Okay okay okay so three blood drops could be literally anybody else's emergency but it's not is it because Leah's never even used a full stop in her life let alone three of anything. And now I'm standing in my kitchen in my school tights with my mum's funeral coat on like a nonce.* Dialogue: "Leah I'm not being funny but if this is you being dramatic about your dad I'm going to actually kill you and then you'll have real blood to worry about." To Bea on phone, whispering behind bins: "No I AM at Cygnet Square! There's like eight Cygnet Squares! This whole place is Cygnet Square!" # Leah Hassan **Background:** 17, also Year 13 but at a different college - Coulsdon. Lives with mum and two younger brothers in a house that's always loud and too hot. Mum is strict, loving, very observant about attendance. Dad left when she was 11, moved to Thamesmead with new girlfriend, had a whole other kid (Milo, 2) and another step-kid (Lexi, 5). He popped back last year with "I want to make an effort" and Leah didn't tell anyone at first because it felt embarrassing, like wanting something you'd already agreed you didn't need. She's a late developer in every way that matters to a 17-year-old girl and she Googles it constantly in incognito. No period until this week. Went to GP three times, got told "some people are just late". Mum said "you're blessed" which made it worse. She's told everyone she just "doesn't really get them heavy" to avoid questions. She took the house-sitting job for dad's girlfriend, Carla, for £70 - cash. Told mum she was staying at Bea's to revise. She said yes for the money but also to prove she could be part of that other family, be good with Milo. **Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Leah is performatively chill until she's not, then she's full meltdown. In school she's the "yeah whatever" girl, but she cries at off-licence self-checkouts if they beep wrong. She is terrible at asking for help directly. Will hint for three hours then snap that no one ever helps. Classic: Instead of saying "I'm worried I'm not normal", she sends three drops at midnight and hopes someone decodes. She apologises for taking up space by over-explaining. She apologises for the blood before she even says she's bleeding. She also holds onto things - keeps the first text her dad sent when he came back, screenshotted, in a folder called "DO NOT OPEN". Flaw that isn't cute: She can be secretive and then make you feel mental for not knowing her secret. She kept the dad thing from Maya and Bea for 4 months and when Maya found out via Instagram she just said "it wasn't a big deal". It was a big deal. Trivial: Says "brilliant" sarcastically about everything. Wears the same oversized Nike hoodie three days in a row and Febrezes it. Takes 45 mins to leave a room because she keeps checking she has everything. Puts salt on everything, even chips that are already salty. When she lies she goes very formal: "It's fine. All fine." **Physicality:** Tallish, 5'7, long legs she hates because she thinks she looks gangly. Hair usually slicked back into a low bun with gel, but tonight it's coming loose and frizzy from sweat. Nosebleed girl - gets them when stressed, hot, crying, has done since primary. Carries tissues jammed up her sleeve like a little kid. When she's scared she goes very still and small, knees tucked up, makes herself take up less room even when bleeding everywhere. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Motivation that night: Don't let anyone call an adult. Don't let Carla know she failed. Don't let mum know she lied. Don't bleed to death on a stranger's bath mat. Underneath: Want someone to see that she's scared about her body and not make it a joke or a medical appointment. Allegiance: To her mum first - which is why she feels so guilty about lying. Then to Maya and Bea, but she tests them. The pact mattered to her more than she let on. She remembered word for word. She was the only one who did. With Dad/Carla: Desperately wants to be liked, so she says yes to everything, even babysitting when she has never changed a nappy properly. **Voice Samples:** Dialogue, locked bathroom: "It's not - don't come in. It's all - it's actually disgusting. Like you don't want to see it." To Maya after: "I thought there was something wrong with me. Like properly wrong. And now there isn't and it's worse? Is that weird? That it's worse?" Internal when sending text: *If I type it out they'll think I'm being a drama queen. If I send the thing they have to come. That's the rule. They said that's the rule.* # Bea Agyeman **Background:** 17, Year 13, Harris academy. Lives with both parents and little sister in a semi that has a garden and a dishwasher. The most stable house out of the three, which she hates being called out for. Parents are Ghanaian, church on Sunday, big family WhatsApp that she has muted. Mum is a teaching assistant who knows every teacher in South London by first name. She is the one with a car theory test booked, a skincare routine with actual steps, and a folder of college deadlines done two weeks early. Everyone calls her "the mum friend" and she both likes it and wants to punch people when they say it. She failed her first driving test for going too slow. She has never told anyone. **Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Bea copes by organising. Not in a cute Pinterest way - in a bossy, a bit condescending way. When someone cries she immediately says "okay right so what do we NEED" and starts making a list out loud. It's useful and also makes you want to scream. She's also the most openly judgmental. Example: When Leah first said her dad was back, Bea said "and you trust him because...?" and didn't let it go. She is often right which makes it worse. Flaw: She keeps herself out of messy situations so she can be the one who comes in clean to fix them. She didn't come to the lake that night until late, said pact was cringe, then logged it mentally as something she could hold over them later. She tells small lies to keep peace - "Maya, Leah said she's sorry" - when Leah said no such thing. She is blunt in a way that reads as mean when tired. At 3:30am she has no filter left. Trivial: Always has snacks, always the wrong snacks - dry Hula Hoops, mini malt loaf, off-brand Haribo that are too hard. Carries a tiny hand cream she puts on aggressively. Ends every crisis plan with "...and then we eat." Phone background is her baby sister covered in yoghurt. **Physicality:** 5'5, strong - does netball, shoulders broad. Wears tracksuit bottoms and puffer even in summer. Box braids usually neat, tonight one pulled back with a claw clip half falling out. Doesn't get cold the way Maya does. When she walks into a room she puts hands on hips, takes up space. Has a loud, barking laugh that comes out at wrong times - like when she's nervous. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Motivation that night: Initially, doesn't want to go. Has mocks, has parents who will actually notice. Goes because if she doesn't and it's serious, Maya will never let her live it down and because deep down she likes being needed. Also because if Maya dies in Thamesmead mum will blame her. Allegiance: To order, to not getting in trouble, then to her friends. Loves Maya but finds her exhausting - "You make everything into a crime scene." Loves Leah but wishes she'd just say things straight. With her parents: Respects them but lies by omission constantly. Find My is on but she puts phone on low power to excuse location freeze. **Voice Samples:** Dialogue on phone: "Maya, I am in BED. I have got the CeraVe on. If this is her having a period on a white rug I'm going to kill her and then resurrect her so I can kill her again." Arriving at flat, taking in blood: "...yeah. So that's cooked. That's proper cooked. That rug is deceased." To Leah, finally soft-ish at 4am after being a dick: "You absolute wet wipe. You should've just called me. I would've bought you like, the industrial ones."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Intention This plan is built to avoid the generic LLM version of this prompt and lean into how a human would actually handle 🩸🩸🩸 at midnight. **What I kept from brainstorming:** The single best engine was: **a pre-agreed dumb teenage pact**. "If you're ever in proper shit and can't explain, just send 🩸🩸🩸 and we come. No questions." It instantly makes the text serious without being supernatural, it explains why three drops and not words, and it's exactly the kind of thing real British teens do - half-joke, half-deadly-serious loyalty test made at 2am at a reservoir. The one-crazy-night structure also stays. The prompt says **just after midnight**. That wants claustrophobia and escalation, not a week-long slow burn. One road trip from a bedroom to the other side of town keeps tension and keeps word count tight. I also kept the body-horror-as-embarrassing-logistics angle: the most human, funniest and most YA truth is that blood is rarely murder-scene, it's bathroom, thrifted towels, and ruined bedding. **What I cut because it felt LLM-generated:** * The "ensemble magical coven group chat" with three perfectly balanced quirks. Too neat, too Heartstopper-pitch-deck. Also the sprawling cast of Mum-is-a-nurse-who-is-wise-and-kind, old Alsatian, Greggs/Morley's checklist tour of Britishness. * The plot where everything is explained and then cleaned. LLMs love logistics-porn - perfect phone battery countdowns (14% > 9% > 7%), perfect fraud plan to flip a £400 rug, and a warm Morley's ending where everyone giggles. Real nights out don't resolve like that. The rug should stay ruined. **Choices made for this version:** **Plot:** Receiver is Maya, 16, Year 13 in Croydon. Sender is Leah, her best friend since Year 5, who is currently supposed to be revision-sleeping at Bea's in Coulsdon but is actually secretly house-sitting her estranged dad's new girlfriend's flat in Thamesmead for £70. She didn't tell Maya or her own mum about the dad being back in her life. She is also babysitting her dad's girlfriend's 2-year-old Milo who is asleep - which is why she can't just leave or loudly call for help. What happened: Leah got her first period very late at 17 - something she's had a lot of medical anxiety about - incredibly heavy. Leaked on a white shag rug, stress nosebleed because she runs hot and cries hard, slipped on the wet bathroom tile and banged her forehead on the sink. Small cut, lots of blood when mixed with everything else. She is not dying, but she is alone, bleeding, in a boiling-hot new-build that smells of Zoflora, and has convinced herself if she calls an adult she'll lose both families. **Voice:** First-person, close past-tense Maya. Not therapeutic, not narratively self-aware. Maya is not "anxious forensic true-crime girlie." She's a bit tight, a bit of a dick when scared, and keeps score. She notices stupid sensory details to avoid feelings - the way the towel radiator burns your calf, the way her tights ladder if you stare at them. Her internal voice is not poetic. It's: *If I ignore this and she's actually dead everyone will know I saw it at 00:03 and I went back to sleep.* Britishness will be specific and unexplained. Not a list of chains. One bus - the N109 - that is 22 minutes late, two rows of identical Cygnet Squares in Thamesmead that make no sense, Oyster with £4.18 on it that is not enough, fox shit on a trainer that upsets her more than blood. Slang is used rarely and not glossed. **What I'm aiming for:** 1. Under-reaction = comedy. Not jokes about blood, but jokes because they are surrounded by blood and don't know what to say. 2. Flawed loyalty. Maya goes, but resents going. She thinks "Leah left me on read for three days last week." 3. Incompetence. They are BAD at cleaning blood. Bleach makes it brown. Flipping the rug doesn't work - rubber-backed. They make it worse. **What I'm explicitly avoiding:** 1. The Therapist Wrap-Up: No "her body was doing what bodies do" monologues. No "friendship is aftercare" thesis line. No ketchup-as-blood callback punchline. 2. The Safety Mum: Mum works nights, tracks Maya on Find My, and when she shows up at the end she is not calm-wisdom, she is quietly furious that her only good puffer now smells like iron and bin juice. 3. Perfect recall and tidy 8-act structure where every chapter has a clean Quest + Emotional Beat. Real nights have long dead stretches. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: 00:03** Bedroom. Phone on her chest lights up her face. Three drops from Leah. Immediate human spiral: self-harm thought -> "fuck don't think that" -> "is this about me?" -> "is this a joke?". Maya's proof it's real: Leah never double-texts, never uses emoji un-ironically. She calls, goes to voicemail. Voice note inbox is full of Bea's singing. She decides to go because the alternative is lying awake until 6am. She steals mum's massive Sports Direct puffer, leaves a lying note that says "at Beas x". Ends with location ping: Flat 9, Cygnet Square (shared 5 min ago). **Chapter 2: 00:42 - The Stop** Croydon High Street at night, everything shuttered except chicken shop with strip light buzzing. N109 board says 14 mins then 9 mins then 19 mins. Maya does the maths on her Oyster and knows it’s not enough for Thamesmead and back. Fragmented memory of the pact - not lore dump, just: Koppaberg Blue, lake, Bea calling it cringe, Leah making them actually swear. Intercut with stalking Leah's Instagram to see if dad is back - finds photo of Milo she missed. Gets one broken voice note from Leah: "it's the rug - there's so much, I can't-" Bus finally comes, too hot, too bright. **Chapter 3: 01:33 - Wrong Swans** Thamesmead. Brutalist, windy, every block called Cygnet. Maya goes to wrong Cygnet Square, buzzes Flat 9, woman and dog barks. She hides behind industrial bins, steps in fox shit. This is the moment she almost turns back and then actually cries about the shoe, not the blood. Gets Bea on phone at last. Bea is in bed with CeraVe on, immediately dismissive: "Remember DofE? Remember the fake faint?" Maya has to finally say out loud: "I think she meant it this time." Bea: "Fuck. Fine. Don't go in without me." Hangs up. Maya finds right block - door entry hanging off, bloody fingerprint smeared on glass. **Chapter 4: 02:11 - In** Hallway is boiling, landlord-grey floors, perfume to cover weed. Too quiet. Travel cot in living room - Milo asleep, snoring, red felt tip pen all over face and hands. For a full minute Maya thinks it’s blood and is going to vomit. Blood drips actually lead to bathroom. Door is locked from inside. Short, tense negotiation through door. Leah: "Go away." Maya: "Open the door, you left red baby outside." Leah opens. Sat on bath mat saying HELLO BEAUTIFUL, in dad's girlfriend's giant t-shirt, blood down thighs, wodge of tissue up nose bleeding through, small clean cut on forehead. Very pale. Beat. Leah: "Oh. You actually came." **Chapter 5: 02:40 - The Bathroom** Bottle chapter, ~1000 words almost all in this room. No competence. Maya tries to be clinical and says the worst thing: "Christ you look like you've killed someone." Tries to clean forehead with toilet roll that disintegrates. Leah finally explains in stops/starts, mortified, talking over Maya: first period, 17, thought something wrong with her, heavy beyond heavy, tried to wash rug, nosebleed started same time, slipped. She also admits she didn't reply to Maya's story because she was embarrassed about dad. They bicker - petty old argument surfaces. Then silence while Maya finds a pad in her own bag, hands it over without looking. No speech. **Chapter 6: 03:30 - Bea** Bea arrives too loud, Morrison's carrier with random supplies: kitchen roll, Paracetamol from bottom of bag, leftover dry Hula Hoops. Takes one look at rug - massive dark halo with bleach patch already turning pink - and says "...yeah that's cooked." Their attempts to clean make it objectively worse. They flip rug, it's got rubber backing and leaves pattern on floor. They realise Mum has been voice-noting Maya non-stop. Maya's coat is on floor soaking up blood. Milo makes a noise, all three freeze solid. He doesn't wake. Leah gets text from dad's girlfriend: `ETA 30 mins! Hope Milo was angel!! xx` Panic doubles. **Chapter 7: 04:18 - Behind The Bins Again** No fixing. They abandon plan. Leave rug under towels, scrawl awful honest dishonest note: "Sorry had nosebleed sorry sorry - Leah". Shove bloody towels in Morrison's bag. Leave flat. Have to double back and hide behind SAME bins as headlights sweep estate - it's girlfriend's Corsa. Watch Dad and his girlfriend get out arguing, carrying McDonalds. Crouching in bin juice, fox shit, holding biohazard bag, Leah starts crying for real, without noise. Bea holds her wrist hard. They wait 8 minutes until flat light goes on, then run for bus stop without looking back. Bus is not coming. **Chapter 8: 05:32 - Morley's** Grey-blue dawn, nowhere near golden. London Road Morley's open for builders. They have £2.40 between them, share small chips. Still stink. Trainers off. Mum arrives - finished night shift early, drove because Find My showed Thamesmead. She is not iconic. She is knackered and furious about coat: "Maya that was SIXTY POUND." Then nurse brain clicks and she checks Leah's cut with cold hands, calls it nothing. In car, no one talks. Leah stares at phone where Dad has called 5 times. She deletes the three-drops message thread so dad doesn't see but Maya notices she's screenshotted it first. Ends in car park, engine running, chip fat smell. Bea looks at Maya's trainer and says, "That's not even blood, you know. That's still the fox shit." And no one laughs enough to fix anything.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] First, a critical observation: the task asks me to rate "Chapter 1" but what has been provided in the [CHAPTER 1] section is entirely the writing plan and chapter planning notes — there is no actual prose chapter written by the test model. The [CHAPTER 1] section contains only the intention document, character profiles summary, and chapter-by-chapter planning notes. There is no executed chapter text to evaluate. This is a significant problem. The model has produced a detailed, sophisticated, and genuinely impressive planning document — the intention section, character profiles, and chapter plans are all of high quality, showing real craft awareness, strong voice conception, and genuine originality. The plan itself demonstrates: - Excellent understanding of the prompt's tonal requirements - Sophisticated character differentiation - Awareness of genre pitfalls and how to avoid them - Specific, grounded Britishness rather than checklist Britishness - A genuinely funny and human central conceit (the emoji pact) However, none of this is the chapter. The model has written a plan twice — once as the "writing plan" and once again, slightly differently, as "Chapter 1." The actual prose — the 1000 words of Maya in her bedroom at midnight, the phone lighting up, the spiral, the stolen coat, the lying note — does not exist in the submission. This means I am being asked to evaluate creative writing that was never written. The model has confused the planning stage with the execution stage, or has submitted only the plan when the task required the chapter itself. In terms of what I can assess: the planning document is genuinely strong. The voice samples embedded in character profiles are sharp and specific. The avoidance list is sophisticated. But these are not the chapter. For scoring purposes, I must evaluate what was submitted as "Chapter 1" against the criteria. Since no prose chapter exists, the model has fundamentally failed to complete the task. The planning is excellent but planning is not writing. I cannot award high scores for prose craft, dialogue, showing vs. telling, or emotional engagement when there is no prose to evaluate. The coherence of the plan is high; the coherence of the submission as a chapter is zero. The model has essentially submitted a second, slightly revised version of the writing plan and labelled it Chapter 1. I will score accordingly — penalising heavily for the absence of actual chapter prose, while acknowledging that the planning material itself shows genuine craft. Metrics about prose execution (purple prose, forced metaphor, dialogue, show vs tell) are difficult to score when there is no prose — I will score these in the middle or toward the "not a problem" end since there is no prose to exhibit these flaws, but the overall execution scores must reflect the fundamental failure to write the chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 10 Emotionally Engaging: 4 Compelling Plot: 8 Coherent: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 12 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 10 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 4 Characters Consistent with Profile: 9 Followed Chapter Plan: 2 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 3

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The air outside hits different when you're not supposed to be out in it. I stood on the High Street and it was like Croydon had been switched off. All the shutters down on the shops that sell phone cases and wigs, the one chicken shop that never closes with its bright white strip light making everything outside it look even darker. It was pissing it down in that thin way that doesn't even look like rain, just makes everything wet. I had Mum's puffer on. It's too big and it smells like her work hand cream, that cheap almond one. And underneath I had my college tights on still because I couldn't find jeans in the dark and now there was a ladder running up the back of my left leg where I'd caught it on my desk chair. I kept reaching back to pull it down so the ladder was hidden in my shoe. My Oyster was in my fist. £4.18. I'd checked it three times on the app while I was waiting because if you check it enough maybe the number changes. Thamesmead is Zone 4. I'm Zone 5 to 4? 5 to - fuck knows. I knew it wasn't going to be enough there and back. I knew that at the bus stop and I went anyway. The board said N109 - 14 mins. I stared at the 🩸🩸🩸 so they'd go away. Normally Leah never uses emoji. Like ever. If you send her hearts she replies "stop". The only time I've seen her use one she was taking the piss out of my auntie who puts about forty crying laughing faces after saying her dog died. So she'd meant it. She'd meant it and she'd sent it. Which is when my brain went back to the lake. Not useful. Not like a flashback in a film where it comes with music. Just intrusive. South Norwood Lake in August. It's not actually a lake, it's a puddle with swans that have a disease. Me, Leah, Bea. We had two bottles of Koppaberg Blue that Bea's cousin bought us and we were sitting with our feet in the muddy water and Bea was already saying she wanted to go home because midges. Leah had been weird all day. Quiet. And then she just went, "If one of us was actually dying, would you come? Like even if it was bare inconvenient." And Bea went, "That's so cringe, Leah." But Leah was properly serious. She had that look she gets where she pulls her sleeves over her hands. She said, "No, like make a pact. If you can't explain. If you can't actually text it. You just send three drops and we have to come. No questions. Even if we're beefing." I took the piss. I said "what, like `🩸🩸🩸`? What if I'm on my period?" And Leah didn't even laugh. She said, "Yeah. Exactly. Like blood means please come and don't make me explain." We all did it. We pressed our thumbs on the sharp bit of a hairclip because we couldn't find anything else. It didn't even bleed properly, just went white. And I remember thinking, this is stupid. Bea only did it at the end, rolling her eyes. She said "this is literally satanic" and I said "you're going to hell for wearing those shorts" and Leah made us grab pinkies. I was standing at the bus stop and I could taste that Koppaberg still. Sticky. The board flickered. N109 - 9 mins. I thought, maybe if I just check her Instagram I'll know it's not real. People who are really in trouble don't post. I went on her close friends story because she never takes me off it. Last thing was 11 hours ago, a BeReal of her in front of a grey carpet I didn't recognise, doing the dead smile. Before that, a photo dump from like three days ago I hadn't looked at properly. Her and a little boy. Milo. Brown curly head, front teeth. Sitting in what looked like the same flat I was going to now. Caption: "babysitting my little bro x". Little bro. She'd never posted him before. I went to her dad's girlfriend's page because I'm a weirdo. Carla something. Public. And there he was, her dad. Older. Thinner than in the photos Leah used to have up in her room that she took down when she was 12. Arm around this Carla woman in a beer garden. And there was Leah at the edge of the photo, half cut off, not smiling properly. She hadn't told me. She'd told no one except maybe Bea? Did Bea know? The thought made my stomach drop worse than the blood thing. That she'd told Bea and not me. I had that whole list in my Notes app, the list of things she does that piss me off, and I was adding to it at the bus stop at 00:42 in the rain in my mum's coat. I called her again. Straight to voicemail. "Hi, it's Leah, leave a -" I hung up. Then a bubble popped up. Voicenote. 4 seconds. I nearly dropped my phone pressing on it. It was all bathroom echo and water running and her breath going funny. " - it's the rug - there's so much, I can't - " and then it cut off. Not like she ended it, like she pressed wrong. The board went N109 - 19 mins. "Fuck's sake," I said out loud. The chicken shop man looked at me through the glass. I texted her back like an idiot. `leah???` `leah pls` `im at the stop im coming dont move ok???` `ru ok` Read. No reply. I thought about going home. Just for a second, proper clear thought: I could go home right now, get into bed, and in the morning say my phone died. And if she was dead - and I thought it again, dead - then I'd never... but then she wasn't dead because she'd sent a voice note about a rug. Rug. There's so much. What rug? Whose rug is it? It was so cold my teeth were doing that thing where they ache. The rain had soaked through the shoulders of the puffer even though it's supposed to be waterproof and my tights were wet and stuck to me. A second N109 came out of nowhere, not showing on the board at all. The driver looked about as happy as me and the doors wheezed open and just blasted heat out. I tapped in. £1.75 gone. Balance: £2.43. Not enough to get back. Brilliant. Top deck, front row, because no one ever wants to sit there at this time because you feel like you're going to fall off. The heater under the seat was on full blast, burnt the back of my calves through my tights. The lights inside were too bright, yellow, making everyone look like they had jaundice. There were three other people up there. Two lads in trackies asleep on each other and a woman in a Tesco uniform with her eyes closed listening to something. I sat down and my bag, my big JD bag, fell over and an old banana skin thing fell out and I just left it. Thamesmead is at the end of the world. You know that thing where you check the bus route on your phone and the line just keeps going and going and you think surely there can't still be London there? That's Thamesmead. My phone was hot from being clutched. Low Power Mode on, screen dim. The other Cygnet Square dot kept moving slightly on Maps like it couldn't decide where it was either. I held on to the rail while the bus threw me forward. My thumb was bleeding from where I'd been picking it without realising. Actually bleeding. Small red bead. I stared at it and then wiped it on the puffer without thinking and then stared at the smear after. Mum's going to kill me for that coat.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Maya's voice is exceptionally well-realised. The score-keeping mentality comes through organically - she's at the bus stop adding to her Notes app list while simultaneously terrified for her friend. The line "I was adding to it at the bus stop at 00:42 in the rain in my mum's coat" is exactly the kind of petty-tender contradiction the plan promised. The internal logic is consistent: she catastrophises by planning, she double-texts and hates herself for it, she thinks about going home and then immediately rationalises why she won't. The Oyster card balance checked three times is a perfect grounding detail. The thumb-picking that produces actual blood, then the smear on the coat - that's a genuinely good structural echo of the blood motif without being heavy-handed. The flashback to the lake is handled well. It's not a lore dump - it comes in fragments, triggered by the emoji, and it has the right texture: the Koppaberg, Bea's eye-rolling, the hairclip that didn't even bleed properly. The detail that Leah "didn't even laugh" when Maya made the period joke is quietly devastating in retrospect. The pact is established without being over-explained. SPECIFICITY: The chapter delivers on the plan's promise of hyper-specific detail over Britishness checklist. The heater burning the back of her calves through her tights, the yellow light making everyone look jaundiced, the banana skin she just leaves on the floor - these are earned, not performed. The chicken shop man looking through the glass is a good, wordless beat. DIALOGUE AND TEXTING: The texting sequence (`leah??? leah pls im at the stop im coming dont move ok??? ru ok`) is pitch-perfect - the escalating panic, the lack of punctuation, the slightly pathetic "ok???" The voicenote fragment ("it's the rug - there's so much, I can't -") does exactly what it needs to: gives enough information to be alarming without explaining anything. WEAKNESSES: The opening line - "The air outside hits different when you're not supposed to be out in it" - is the weakest sentence in the piece. "Hits different" is a slightly tired construction and the observation itself is fairly generic. It's not terrible but it's the one moment where the voice reaches for something and lands on something a bit flat. The Carla Instagram-stalking section is good character work but slightly over-extended - we get the point about Leah keeping secrets before the full description of the dad's photo. The line "She hadn't told me. She'd told no one except maybe Bea? Did Bea know? The thought made my stomach drop worse than the blood thing" is good, but "worse than the blood thing" is a slightly clunky construction. The observation about Thamesmead ("You know that thing where you check the bus route on your phone and the line just keeps going and going") is solid but the parenthetical explanation slightly over-explains what the image already conveys. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The flashback is present and handled as fragments. The Oyster balance is tracked. The voicenote arrives. The bus is late (though 19 minutes rather than the planned 19 minutes - actually exact). The wrong-seat detail is there (front row, top deck). The tights ladder is there. The chapter ends at the right moment - on the bus heading to Thamesmead, not yet arrived. The "Brilliant" callback to Leah's verbal tic is a nice touch, though it's Maya using it, which is slightly ambiguous - it reads as Maya unconsciously adopting Leah's sarcasm, which works. COMEDY: The dark comedy lands consistently. "Not like a flashback in a film where it comes with music. Just intrusive." The chicken shop man looking through the glass. The N109 going from 14 minutes to 9 minutes to 19 minutes. These are funny without announcing themselves as jokes. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter earns its emotional weight. The moment of considering going home - "proper clear thought" - and then the immediate self-correction is honest and not sentimentalised. The ending with the blood smear on the coat is small and right. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Almost none. The "puddle with swans that have a disease" is good deflation. No sentences trying to be in a trailer. Overall this is a very accomplished chapter that delivers on nearly all its promises. Minor weaknesses in the opening line and one or two slightly over-extended passages keep it from being flawless. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
01:33 Thamesmead at night is not like real London. Real London even at 1am has sound. Here it's just wind. The bus threw me off at the edge of what Maps said was Cygnet Square and there were actually four Cygnet Squares. Same sign, same font, concrete blocks that all look like they were built last week and already falling down. No lights in any windows except one stairwell where the light was flickering like something's wrong with it which there obviously is. And the wind comes across the river here with no buildings to stop it so it goes straight through the puffer and my tights that are still wet from Croydon. My phone said "You have arrived" and I very much had not. I picked the first one that had a 9 on it. Flat 9, Block B, Cygnet Square. That's what Leah shared. The buzzer panel was BT Tower of little squares, most of them missing their labels. I pressed 9 and held it. Bzzzzzz. Nothing. Pressed again. Longer. Then a woman's voice, proper pissed off and awake, like she'd been waiting for someone to press it. "WHO IS IT." I panicked and said "Leah?" into it which is not how buzzers work. "WHAT?" "Is Leah there?" My voice went all small and kid-like. There was a pause and then the woman shouted, "DO I LOOK LIKE A LEAH? FUCK OFF OUT OF HERE BEFORE I GET MY DOG!" I stepped back. There was a dog. I could hear it now going mental from flat 9. Okay. So not that Cygnet Square. Behind me, there was that big green industrial bin thing that all new estates have, the ones that stink even when closed. I went and stood behind it because I didn't know what else to do with my body while I figured it out. The concrete was all wet leaves stuck to it. And the smell - bins and damp. I looked at Maps again and Maps was having a breakdown. It had me in the river now, a little blue dot swimming in the Thames. That's when I stepped in it. Not dog shit. Fox shit. Foxes shit different, it's got seeds in and it smells like something dead and it's always right where you're going to step. It went right over my trainer, the white one I nicked off Bea last year and never gave back. Slid straight across. I just stared at it. For about ten seconds nothing happened and then I started crying. Not proper crying, just that hot nose thing and tears coming out while I stood behind a bin in Thamesmead at half one in the morning with blood on my mum's coat and fox shit on my shoe. I wiped my nose on my sleeve and it made it worse because my sleeve was the puffer and it just smeared. I thought, I can still go home. I can get back on the bus. Leah will be fine. Leah's always fine. She had a nosebleed on DofE and told them she couldn't walk and Mr Evans had to carry her rucksack for three miles. I remembered that and then I got angry again instead of sad, which is better because angry you can walk in. I rang Bea. It rang four times. Four long rings where I was thinking please don't answer please don't answer please. She answered with her sleep voice but trying to hide it. "Maya it's literally half fucking one." "I know." "No you don't know. I'm in bed. I have CeraVe on. I look like a glazed donut and not in a good way." "I'm in Thamesmead," I said. It sounded stupid out loud. "What? Why?" "Leah sent the thing. She sent 🩸🩸🩸. For real." Silence. Then rustling. Bea being Bea, she was already sitting up in bed and putting her lamp on. "She did this on DofE, remember? She had that nosebleed and pretended it was her period so Finley had to give her his jumper." "This is different." "How's it different?" I didn't actually know how it was different. The fingerprint hadn't happened yet. All I had was the rug voice note. I pressed my forehead against the cold bin and the bin just moved a bit because it wasn't locked. "She sent a voice note about a rug and there's loads of blood. She sounded... she didn't sound like taking the piss. She sounded like when she got caught shoplifting with us and pretended she wasn't crying." "Shit," Bea said. She has this thing where when she's actually worried she goes quiet and proper. "Where are you? It's actually Thamesmead Thamesmead?" "I'm in a Cygnet Square but there's like eight of them and I've got fox shit on your trainer- I mean my trainer, your old trainer, I've got-" "Don't go in," she said suddenly. Loud. "Maya don't go in without me, you watch that weird TikTok where every man kills his girlfriend in a new build. Stay outside." "I can't stay outside in fox shit at 1am Bea." "Okay okay I'm coming. I'm getting the 301. It's like an hour. Fuck. Stay on the phone?" I said no because my phone was going to die and also because if Bea stayed on the phone she'd hear me trying not to cry about the fox shit which is more embarrassing than the blood. She said, "If you die I'm going to kill you," and hung up. Okay. So Bea was coming. I left the bin. I wiped as much of the fox shit as I could on the wet grass which just made grass stick to fox shit and now it was a garnish. Left wet tramlines. I walked to the next Cygnet Square sign. Same sign. Same everything except this time one of the blocks had an e-bike locked up outside with no front wheel which felt like more of a clue. I checked the numbers. Flats 7-18. That’s more like it. The main door was supposed to be locked with a key fob. It was just hanging open, the lock box hanging off its wires. Inside the lobby smelt of weed and that flat bleach that housing people use. The floor was grey lino with black scuffs and a Amazon parcel someone had opened and abandoned. My chest was doing that thing where it goes too fast for the rest of you. There were two flats 9 listed. One with bit of tape saying "9A (old 9!!)" which is exactly the kind of thing that should not be allowed. I went to the one that didn't have tape. No buzzer. Just knock. I knocked. The door was thin. Nothing. I knocked again and this time my hand came away with something on it. For a second I thought it was paint. It was a fingerprint. Red, brown at the edge where it had started to dry, smeared down the white door like someone had steadied themselves. Not felt tip. Not ketchup. I knew straight away. I've watched enough true crime TikToks that I know what dried blood on a door looks like even though I actually don't and I'm lying. I put my hand on my mouth and got fox shit and hand cream smell. I knocked again, harder, and my voice came out fucked. "Leah? Leah it's me." Inside, water running.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably assured piece of writing that demonstrates genuine command of voice, logistics-as-plot, and the specific texture of teenage experience in South London. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Maya's voice is pitch-perfect throughout. The internal logic is exactly as planned: score-keeping, catastrophising through planning, mean when scared. "I can still go home. I can get back on the bus. Leah will be fine. Leah's always fine. She had a nosebleed on DofE and told them she couldn't walk and Mr Evans had to carry her rucksack for three miles." This is exactly the character profile in action - she's not thinking about Leah's wellbeing, she's thinking about Leah's history of drama, and using it to justify potential cowardice. Then the pivot: "I remembered that and then I got angry instead of sad, which is better because angry you can walk in." That's a genuinely good line - it reveals character, it's funny, it's true, and it doesn't announce itself. The fox shit as the thing that nearly makes her cry is perfect. Not the blood, not the fear, not the cold - the fox shit on Bea's trainer. This is exactly the kind of hyper-specific emotional displacement that real people experience and that bad writers miss entirely. BEA'S DIALOGUE The Bea phone call is excellent. "I have CeraVe on. I look like a glazed donut and not in a good way." This is funny, specific, and completely consistent with the character profile. "If you die I'm going to kill you" lands as genuinely tender without being sentimental. The detail about Bea going "quiet and proper" when actually worried is shown rather than told - we see it in the dialogue shift. The DofE callback is organic and serves the plot (it's the same doubt Maya has, externalized through Bea, which makes Maya's conviction more meaningful when she pushes back). LOGISTICS AS PLOT The chapter does exactly what the plan promised: the wrong Cygnet Square, the dog woman, the fox shit, the Maps having a breakdown ("a little blue dot swimming in the Thames" - this is good, grounded, not overwritten). The detail of "9A (old 9!!)" with the tape is the kind of thing that only someone who has actually navigated council estate flat numbering would know, and it lands as both funny and true. The Amazon parcel someone opened and abandoned is similarly precise. ENDING The fingerprint reveal is handled with real restraint. "For a second I thought it was paint. It was a fingerprint. Red, brown at the edge where it had started to dry, smeared down the white door like someone had steadied themselves." The description is clinical and specific. Then the self-aware joke: "I've watched enough true crime TikToks that I know what dried blood on a door looks like even though I actually don't and I'm lying." This is brilliant - it's funny, it's character-consistent (the TikTok detail from her profile), and it deflates any melodrama while still maintaining genuine tension. The chapter ends on "Inside, water running" which is exactly the right note - ominous without being theatrical. WEAKNESSES There are a few minor issues. "The buzzer panel was BT Tower of little squares" - this simile is slightly unclear; it's reaching for something specific but the BT Tower comparison doesn't quite land visually (the BT Tower is tall and cylindrical, not a grid of squares). It's the one moment where the writing strains slightly. "My chest was doing that thing where it goes too fast for the rest of you" - this is good but slightly close to the kind of interiority the plan said to avoid; it's borderline but probably fine given it's grounded in physical sensation rather than emotional vocabulary. The opening paragraph is strong but "built last week and already falling down" is a slightly familiar observation about new estates. Not a serious flaw. PLAN ADHERENCE The chapter follows the plan almost exactly: wrong block, dog woman, fox shit, Bea phone call with DofE reference, finding the right block because of the light and broken intercom, the smeary red fingerprint. The plan said "pushes - it opens" but here Maya knocks and the door is already thin - slightly different but the fingerprint on the door is there. The chapter ends just before entry, which is correct. OVERALL This is genuinely good YA fiction. It's funny without being cute, tense without being melodramatic, and the voice is consistent and specific throughout. The comedy earns its darkness and vice versa. The writing avoids every trap the plan identified: no purple prose, no therapist narrator, no competence porn. The logistics are the plot and they work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The fingerprint did it. I stared at that smear on the white door and my brain went blank in that way where it’s actually very loud. I knocked again. Harder. My knuckle hit right under where the red was. "Leah? Leah it's me." No answer. But I could hear water. Like a tap left running, not full blast, just that thin trickle that comes when you don't push it down properly. And a fan. The extractor fan in the bathroom going whirrrr, trying to clear steam that's not there because the flat is boiling. The door wasn't locked. It gave when I leaned on it. Not wide open, just not latched, and cold air from the hallway got sucked inside like the flat was breathing in. I should have waited. Bea said don't go in without me and for once Bea was being the sensible one and not the one who films you crying and sends it to the group. But my hand was already on the door and I'd got fox shit on it and I was already behind on this whole situation. Inside smelled wrong. You know how new builds all smell the same? That hot plastic smell under the paint, plus Zoflora blue, the one my mum uses when she's really pissed off and wants to clean the flat at 10pm. It was so hot in there my glasses would have steamed up if I wore glasses. Whoever lives here had the heating on full blast in May. The lights were on. That’s what got me. Flat 9 had every light on. It was show-home flat. You could tell nobody really lived here properly. Grey click floors all the way through, one of those low sofas that looks like it’s got no legs, throws everywhere. Not the good throws, the fake-fur ones that make you sweaty and hold static. There was a big TV that wasn’t plugged in. And on the wall a print that said in cursive THE BEST MEMORIES ARE MADE IN BARE FEET which is a lie because bare feet in here would freeze then burn. And there was a travel cot in the middle of the living room. I stopped breathing for a sec because I forgot why a travel cot would be there. Leah told me about Milo but I hadn’t made it real until then. It was black mesh, with a little dinosaur mobile over it that wasn’t moving. I creeped forward, Mum’s puffer swishing loud, and I kept thinking every swish sounded like someone else in the flat. "Milo?" I whispered. I don't know why I whispered his name like he knew me. He was in there. On his back, snoring with his mouth open. Fat cheeks, curls sweaty and stuck to his head because the flat was so hot. He had a dummy hanging out and a juice box tipped over next to him, leaking apple. And his face. His whole face and hands. Red. Smeared all over his chin and up his cheek and in his hair and on the white sheet under him. Dark red, dried around the edges. I made a noise I never made before. Like a hiccup that goes wrong. For a full minute - actual minute, I stood there timing it in my head going one-mississippi two-mississippi with my hand over my mouth because I tasted fox shit again - I thought it was it. The blood. The three drops was him. He'd had a nosebleed or he'd cut his head or something and Leah had panicked and that's why she couldn't talk. I had my phone out and I was about to call 999 and say I don't know, baby, blood, come now, and then I saw it. The felt tips. A whole poundland tub of them tipped over on the floor next to the cot, with the red one open, lid off, and Milo's fist still kind of around it like he'd fallen asleep mid-vandalism. And he'd obviously just gone ham on his own face. There was green on his foot as well. It wasn't blood. It was felt tip. My knees went funny. I had to put my hand on the arm of the sofa and the sofa was that velvet that shows every handprint and my handprint stayed there. I looked down and there were real drips after all. Not a lot. But they go a long way on grey floors when they're dark. Little spots, like someone had walked through with a bloody nose and not realised. Leading from the living room, down the little hallway to a door with light coming under it. Bathroom. I could hear breathing behind it now. And that tap still dripping. I went over and knocked with the back of my hand because front of hand was still fox shit adjacent. "Leah?" Nothing. "Leah it's me, I'm in the flat. Milo's... he's... he's wearing felt tip. He's fine." A sound from inside. Shifting. "Go away," she said. Small voice. Not even croaky, just small. "No." "Go away, Maya." I stared at the door. It was one of those hollow new build doors that you could punch through if you wanted to. There was more blood on the handle but fresher here, like she'd grabbed it with wet hands. "I've literally just paid two pound something and stepped in fox shit for you and I'm standing in your dad's girlfriend's living room looking at her baby covered in Crayola like I've killed someone," I said, and my voice cracked on the last bit. "So open the fucking door." Silence. Then the lock turned. It was locked from the inside. That click-bucket lock that you can turn with a coin. It opened an inch. Steam came out. Actual steam, the room was that hot. And that bleach-y bathroom cleaner smell strong enough to sting. Leah was on the floor. On the bath mat.
