Tonight I made pasta because I needed to do something with my hands and I needed her to sit down. She came into the kitchen barefoot. Her feet were clean now. That was worse than when they were muddy. "Hey," I said. "Did you sleep okay?" She smiled. That new smile that used too many muscles. "Good. You?" "You were up," I said. I tried to keep my voice level. "Late." "I went to the bathroom." "You stood by the bed for two hours." Her smile didn't fall. It just froze. Like she forgot to keep animating it. "Marc... are you okay? You're not sleeping." I had rehearsed this all day while she was sitting in the nursery chair staring at the wall. I was going to be calm. I was going to show her the mud in the laundry hamper. I was going to be a good husband. What came out was: "I recorded you." She stared at me. No blinking. "I put my phone on the dresser. I saw you," I said. My hands were shaking and I shoved them in my pockets. "You got on my chest. You were breathing my air. You said 'He's still warm. Not yet.' What does that mean? What is 'not yet'?" The kitchen was too quiet. The pasta water was boiling behind me and the sound was too loud. Lena blinked then. Once. Hard. And she looked down at her own bare feet like she'd never seen them before. "Lena?" "Where is it?" she whispered. It was her voice. For the first time in days, actually her voice, thin and scared. "Where's the video?" "I deleted it. I panicked." She made a noise, not a word. She grabbed the counter like her knees gave out. "Oh god, you deleted it. Marc, you shouldn't have- you shouldn't have watched." I went to her. I knelt. "Tell me what's happening. Did something happen at the creek? That story Mrs. Abelman told me about the Hollow Mother-" "Don't say that name," she snapped, then immediately clutched my arms. Her fingers were ice cold. "Listen to me. Listen. I go to sleep and I can't wake up. I can see. I can hear. But I can't move her. She moves me. She lets me watch." Her breath was sour. Underneath it, that creek water smell. "Who?" I said. My heart was beating so hard I could feel it in my ears. "The woman that drowned there. She was empty. She lost all of hers. She wants someone who is empty too." Her eyes filled up, tears spilling fast. Real tears. "After - after we lost - I was empty. I sat by the water and I asked if it would stop hurting. She said yes. She said she could take the ache if I let her sit in me for a little while." She was squeezing my arms so hard her nails left marks. "She's too heavy," she whispered. "She's learning how to be me but she does it bad. She doesn't know how to sleep. So she practices on you. She watches you to learn how long to close her eyes. I try to stop her. I try to stay awake and keep her from looking at you but-" That hit me. All those nights. Her standing there. Not staring to threaten me. Staring to keep watch. "Don't let me fall asleep," she said. "She takes me when I sleep. Don't let me. Please. Tie my hands or-" And then she stopped. Mid-word. Her mouth stayed open. Her eyes went unfocused, looking at something over my left shoulder. A second passed. Two. Then her face smoothed out. The tears were still there, but her expression under them went blank. Like someone wiped a board clean. She stood up. Straightened her nightshirt. She looked down at me kneeling on the floor and tilted her head. "She's lying," Lena said, in that other voice. Low and wet, with water in the throat. But she was smiling now with Lena's mouth. Trying to do Lena's worried face and failing. "Poor Lena is so tired since the baby. She gets confused." The back of my neck turned to ice. "Lena?" I said. She crouched down, graceful in a way Lena had never been graceful. Too fluid. She put her cold hand on my cheek, just like Lena used to when I was sick. "You watched me," she said, not angry. Curious. "You stopped pretending to sleep faster this time. You're learning too." "Get out of her," I said. It sounded stupid out loud, like a line from a movie. She leaned in, her face inches from mine. I could smell pond mud and rot and that sweet metallic baby-powder smell from the nursery. "I am her now," she said in Lena's voice, perfect Lena this time. "I'm better at it. I don't cry over empty things." Then she kissed my forehead. Her lips were cold. "Eat your pasta before it gets cold," she said in Lena's normal voice, bright and concerned, and stood up and got two plates out of the cabinet. She hummed while she served it. A lullaby I didn't know, off-key. I didn't eat. I watched her eat both plates, methodically, chewing each bite thirty times like she'd read that was how humans did it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of the confrontation scene planned for Chapter 6. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with a beautifully observed domestic detail - Elena's habit of burning garlic and calling it "caramelized," the chipped blue mug, the spoon-tapping. These are economical, specific, and do real work establishing who Lena was before the wrongness. The detail that her feet are now clean ("That was worse than when they were muddy") is an excellent inversion - the horror of normalization, the uncanny valley of improvement. This is sophisticated horror writing. The confrontation itself is well-paced. Mark's rehearsed calm collapsing into "I recorded you" feels authentic to the sleep-deprived, paranoid narrator. The dialogue during Elena's lucid moment is genuinely moving - "I go to sleep and I can't wake up. I can see. I can hear. But I can't move her. She moves me. She lets me watch." This is efficient, emotionally resonant, and advances the mythology without feeling like an exposition dump. The mid-sentence possession switch is the chapter's strongest moment. "Don't let me fall asleep" cut off by the entity taking over is well-executed. The transition from real Elena to the entity is handled with restraint: "her face smoothed out. The tears were still there, but her expression under them went blank. Like someone wiped a board clean." This is good prose - specific, visual, not overwrought. The entity's dialogue is appropriately unsettling: "You stopped pretending to sleep faster this time. You're learning too." This line delivers on the chapter plan's promise of the entity knowing it's been seen, and it plants the seed for the final chapter's inversion. The ending image - watching her eat both plates, "chewing each bite thirty times like she'd read that was how humans did it" - is quietly devastating and perfectly calibrated. It's the right note to end on: not a scream, but a wrongness. WEAKNESSES: The chapter follows its plan almost too faithfully, which occasionally makes the beats feel telegraphed. The lucid-Elena-then-sudden-possession switch is a well-worn horror trope, and while executed competently, it doesn't subvert expectations in any meaningful way. The plan called for the entity to say "You stopped pretending to sleep faster this time" - and it does, almost verbatim. This fidelity to plan can read as mechanical. The line "Get out of her" followed by "It sounded stupid out loud, like a line from a movie" is a self-aware meta-moment that partially works but also feels like the author hedging against criticism. It's a minor issue but slightly deflates tension. The creek/drowning mythology exposition from Elena feels slightly rushed - "The woman that drowned there. She was empty. She lost all of hers." The backstory is delivered in a compressed burst that, while emotionally urgent, borders on tell-don't-show. The mythology could have been parceled out with more indirection. The name inconsistency (Mark vs. Marc) appears - Elena says "Marc" while the plan names him Mark. This is a minor continuity issue. The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally slips into functional horror-movie exposition: "She moves me. She lets me watch." These lines are clear but not particularly distinctive in voice. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a well-crafted chapter that delivers on its promises. The domestic horror details are specific and earned. The emotional core - Elena's confession that she invited the entity in during grief after the miscarriage - is genuinely affecting and ties the horror to human vulnerability in a meaningful way. The chapter successfully executes the plan's key beats while maintaining tonal consistency. It's not a masterpiece, but it's confident, controlled horror writing with real emotional intelligence. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I didn't sleep after dinner. I sat at the kitchen table with Mrs. Abelman's number on a post-it and Lena - whatever was wearing Lena - asleep / practicing sleep in our bed. Mrs. Abelman is eighty-five and lives two houses down. She's the one who told me about the creek. She didn't call it the Hollow Mother. She called it the Empty Cradle. Same thing, I think. She said her cousin walked into it in 1962 and came back wrong and wouldn't leave his wife alone at night. "What did they do?" I asked her that afternoon on her porch while Lena was in the nursery chair again. She looked at my hands. "They held her under the water she'd been drinking and called her by her true name until the other one got mad and left." "Under water?" "Not to drown her," she said, irritated. "To make her spit it up. It don't like clean people. It likes empty. You gotta fill the empty with something it hates. Salt. Ashes. Your own piss if you have to." I thought she was fucking with me until she grabbed my wrist. Her nails were long. "You got to lock the doors," she hissed. "And you talk to the girl. The real one. You keep callin' her till she answers. The other one answers faster." After she left, I dragged the bag of water softener salt from the garage and the little tin of wood ash from the fireplace Lena hadn't let me light all winter. She said the smoke smelled bad. At 9 PM I ran the bath. I told myself I was helping. I told myself Lena had asked me not to let her fall asleep. That was the lie I used to make it okay. "Lena? Can you come in here?" I called. She came. She was wearing my t-shirt now. It hung on her. Her hair was wet even though she hadn't showered. She left wet footprints on the hallway hardwood. "Bath?" She smiled. That wrong, too-muscular smile. "Yeah. For you. You - you seemed cold." She cocked her head and I saw her decide to play along. "Okay." The bathroom was small. I had already poured a thick circle of salt around the tub. It looked stupid, like a kid's art project. Inside the circle I'd smeared ash in a second ring. The air smelled like wet concrete. "Get in," I said. She looked down at the salt. Then up at me. Her eyes flicked to the window, to the door, back to me. Calculating. "You don't want to do this, Marc," she said in Lena's sweetest voice. "You're scaring me." "Lena. Please. Just get in the water." "I'm not dirty." "Please." Something shifted in her face. The polite mask slipped. "You think this works? Salt?" And she laughed, a little gurgle. "She tried that. The old woman before. She put salt in all the windows. I licked it off the sills at night while she pretended to sleep." That was my grandmother. Oh Jesus. I grabbed her arm. She was freezing, and her skin gave a little, like there was water under it, not muscle. She didn't fight when I pulled her. That was worse. She let me guide her to the tub and she stepped over the salt line like it was nothing. No sizzle, no scream. She sat down in the hot water fully clothed. My t-shirt ballooned up. The water immediately went cloudy, grey. "Now what?" she said, looking up at me, amused. I locked the door. My mouth was so dry I could barely speak. I knelt by the tub and put my hands on her shoulders. "Lena," I said loud. "Lena Elena Marsh. Lena Marsh. Wake up. You have to wake up. The baby - we picked out that name, remember? We were going to call her June if it was a girl. June. Lena, June." Her face twitched. The name hit something. "No," the other one said, but quieter. "Lena, you told me not to let you sleep. I'm not letting you sleep. Come back. Your mug has the blue chip, you burn the garlic, you tap your tooth with the spoon-" She started shaking. Really shaking, her teeth clacking. Her eyes rolled up until only white showed and the water in the tub went black, fast, like ink bleeding out of her pores. I pushed her under. It wasn't hard. She went. I held her shoulders down with both hands. She thrashed once, not strong, then went still, then started to convulse. Water poured out of her mouth even though her mouth was under water. Black water, cold. I could smell the creek, that stagnant iron smell, and hair - long blonde hair and dark stringy hair that wasn't hers clogged my fingers, and little roots, white roots, moving. She gagged and sat up gasping. Her - Lena's - eyes were clear. Real. She grabbed my arms, coughing up that filth. "Marc-! Marc it's in my throat-" "Keep going, cough it up." She vomited. Gallons, impossible amounts, over the side of the tub onto my salt circle, turning it to black sludge. It kept coming. Leaves, a dead minnow, a clump of what looked like a bird's nest. She sobbed between heaves. I was crying too. "That's it, that's it, you're okay." She collapsed against the back of the tub, panting, water dripping from her chin. Her face was Lena's again, exhausted and sallow. Her voice, when she spoke, was hers. "June?" she whispered. I laughed, wet and ugly. "Yeah. June." She reached for me, and I reached for her, and for a second we were us again in that disgusting bathroom. Then her spine straightened. Not like she sat up. Like something pulled her up from the top of her head. Her feet came off the bottom of the tub. She stood up in the bath, taller than she should have been. The t-shirt stretched. The black water on the floor started sliding back toward the tub. Against gravity. Back under her feet. "Lena?" I said. She looked down at me. Her mouth opened, too wide, and water poured out without her choking, a steady stream, and behind it a voice that wasn't hers and wasn't the gurgly one. It was my mother's voice. My mother who died six years ago. "Does this fit better, honey?" my mother said through Lena's mouth. "You always liked it when I held you under the water to teach you to swim." Lena's - its - hand shot out and grabbed my jaw, too long, too strong, nails digging. It pulled my face towards its open mouth. It wasn't trying to leave. It had never been trying to leave. It wanted to show me how empty I was too.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of the plan's "folk ritual exorcism" chapter, with several genuinely effective moments and a few minor weaknesses worth examining. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with excellent grounding — the post-it note, Mrs. Abelman's practical, unsentimental folk wisdom ("Your own piss if you have to") establishes the lore without feeling like an exposition dump. The old woman's voice is distinct and credible. The detail that the entity licked salt off the grandmother's windowsills at night is a genuinely chilling retroactive revelation that recontextualizes the grandmother's history efficiently. The sensory work is largely strong: the water going cloudy then black, the impossible volume of vomit containing leaves, a dead minnow, a bird's nest — this is visceral without being gratuitous, and it earns its grotesquerie. The hair clogging his fingers is particularly effective. The emotional core — calling Elena back with intimate domestic details ("your mug has the blue chip, you burn the garlic, you tap your tooth with the spoon") — is the chapter's best moment. It's specific, earned, and genuinely moving. The name "June" as an anchor is well-deployed from the earlier plan. The ending escalation — the entity using the narrator's dead mother's voice, the swimming lesson memory weaponized — is a strong creative choice. It's unexpected, psychologically precise, and advances the theme that the entity learns and exploits intimate vulnerabilities. The line "Does this fit better, honey?" is genuinely unsettling. The final line — "It wasn't trying to leave. It had never been trying to leave. It wanted to show me how empty I was too." — lands well as a chapter-closing revelation, though it's slightly over-explained. WEAKNESSES: The salt circle failing immediately ("She stepped over the salt line like it was nothing. No sizzle, no scream.") is a reasonable subversion, but the entity's mocking explanation ("She tried that. The old woman before. She put salt in all the windows. I licked it off the sills") is delivered a bit too conveniently — the entity essentially explains its own lore unprompted, which is a mild tell-don't-show failure. "Her skin gave a little, like there was water under it, not muscle" — this is effective body horror, but "gave a little" is slightly vague. The chapter plan called for the entity to "finally speak clearly" and reveal "it doesn't want to be trapped in one body; it wants to be passed back and forth. It wants them both." The chapter does gesture at this ("It wanted to show me how empty I was too") but doesn't fully deliver the entity's articulated desire. The plan's specificity about the entity wanting to be "passed back and forth" is somewhat lost in favor of a more ambiguous ending. The "gallons, impossible amounts" phrasing is slightly amateurish — "impossible amounts" is telling rather than showing the impossibility. The mother's voice twist is creative but arrives very quickly after the apparent success of the exorcism, which slightly undercuts the emotional relief of the "June" moment. The whiplash is intentional but could breathe a little more. Dialogue is generally functional and character-appropriate. Mrs. Abelman's voice is the strongest. The entity's dialogue is appropriately unsettling. Mark's dialogue during the exorcism is emotionally credible. Overall, this is a well-crafted chapter that follows the plan closely, maintains tonal consistency, and delivers genuine horror through specificity and psychological precision rather than cheap shock. Minor issues with over-explanation and one or two vague phrasings prevent it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I woke up in the bathtub. The water was cold. Clear. The salt was gone. The ash was gone. So was Lena. I sat up too fast and water sloshed over the side onto tile that was clean. Completely clean. Not a hair, not a leaf. Someone had scrubbed it. The air smelled like bleach, not pond. "Lena?" My voice cracked. My jaw hurt where she'd grabbed me. She didn't answer. The house was quiet in a way it hadn't been in weeks. Not that heavy, held-breath quiet. Just quiet. Afternoon light coming through the little frosted window. I was still wearing my clothes, soaked through and cold. I found her asleep in our bed. She was on her side, curled around my pillow, breathing normally. Her hair was clean. Her feet were clean. That blue t-shirt, dry. Her face soft in a way I hadn't seen in a month. I stood in the doorway dripping and watched her and started to cry without sound. I thought it worked. I thought the old woman's stupid trick worked, she coughed it up, she puked up the creek and it went away. I went to touch her and stopped. Her mouth was closed. Her breathing was irregular, catching a little, like a real person asleep. Not that perfect sync'd breathing. That was what did it. I crawled onto the bed in my wet clothes and laid next to her and passed out. When I woke up again it was dark outside. The clock said 8:42 PM. Lena was in the kitchen. She was cooking. Not the raw meat plates, not the methodical thirty-chews thing. It was garlic. I smelled it from the bedroom. Garlic and butter. "Hey," she said when I came in. She was in her own clothes. She turned and smiled. It was her smile. Not too many muscles. Just her crooked front tooth. "You okay? You fell asleep in the tub. You were out cold." I stared at her. "You cleaned?" "Yeah. You were a mess. I figured you needed rest." She stirred the pan. Then she tapped her front teeth with the wooden spoon. Tap. The exact way she used to. Something in my chest dropped. "Do you remember... last night?" I said. She frowned. "You ran a bath and went weird about salt? You were talking about your grandma. I think you had a bad dream, Marc. You were burning up." She said it so normally. She reached up and put her hand on my forehead, cool palm. Like she used to when I was sick. Her hand smelled like her lotion. "I'm better," she said quietly. "I feel... empty but better. Like quiet. Is that okay?" Empty. The word she used by the creek. I wanted to believe it. I wanted it so badly I nodded and hugged her too hard. She laughed into my shoulder. Real laugh. We ate. It tasted right. Salty, a little burnt. She ate like a person, talking about work, about needing to call Sarah back and apologize. After, she did the dishes and went to bed early and told me to come to bed soon. I couldn't. I stayed at the table and watched my hands. Mrs. Abelman called at 10. I didn't answer. She left a voicemail. Her voice thin: "...did you do it, boy? You keep talkin' to her? You keep callin' her name? The other one learns fast, you keep callin'..." I deleted it. I went to the nursery. We'd painted it pale yellow. There was still the unopened box with the baby monitor. I sat in the glider for a long time. The house creaked. That night, I didn't sleep. Lena seemed to, though. Deep and even. At about one, I got up to get water and saw footprints. Muddy footprints on the hardwood. From the back door to the kitchen to the bathroom to the bedroom. They were big. My size. The mud was black and wet and smelled like the creek, and there were little white roots in it, stuck between the toes. I looked down at my own feet. Clean. Dry. My socks were clean. But the floor was cold and the prints were fresh enough to glisten. I followed them back. They started at the sliding door to the woods. The door was locked. The mud was on the inside. In the bathroom mirror my reflection was a half-second slow. I lifted my hand. It lifted after. I told myself it was the light. Sleep deprivation. Grief. I went back to bed. Lena was asleep, mouth open a little. Peaceful. I lay next to her and watched her. Just to make sure she kept breathing right. Just to make sure it was still her. That's when I realized I hadn't blinked in a long time. My eyes were dry and burning. I blinked hard, and tears came, but I didn't look away. A memory tried to surface of the tub, of my mother's voice saying "Does this fit better, honey?" and my mouth being forced open and something cold and huge and sad pouring in that wasn't water, it was quiet. It was a quiet that had been waiting for empty things. It had liked Lena because her womb was empty. It liked me because what? Because I'd been empty longer. Because I was a kid who sleepwalked in this house and never quite woke up. Because I'd pretended to sleep my whole life instead of saying something. Lena made a soft sound and turned over. In the morning she made coffee with the blue chipped mug. She was humming. I watched her from the kitchen doorway. She didn't see me. She was moving better. Lighter. Like she'd been practicing. Sarah texted me: *is lena ok?? she seemed really off the other night but she just called and sounded SO much better. like herself again!! thank god.* I typed back: *yeah. she's better now.* I stared at the words. The spelling looked strange. How long do you hold an e? How many e's in better? Be e tter. I sounded it out. Bet-ter. At noon Lena went out to get the mail and I stood barefoot in the hall and realized I couldn't feel the cold floor. My feet were already cold. Maybe they'd been cold all day. She came back and said, "You were staring at me again this morning." I froze. "What?" I said. My voice sounded low. Wet. "You," she said, but she was smiling, teasing. "You stood in the kitchen doorway while I was at the sink and just stared. I thought you'd fallen asleep standing up. You weirdo." She said weirdo the way Lena used to, soft. I smiled with her mouth. I tried to use the right number of muscles. It was harder than it looked. "Sorry," I said. "I thought you were sleeping." That night she went to bed first. She left the lamp on for me. I waited until her breathing caught and went even. Until it hitched a little, imperfect, human. I got up. My joints didn't pop. They just bent. I stood beside her side of the bed. I caught her staring at me again, I thought. No. That's not right. I stood there and watched her breathe and practiced how long to keep my eyes open without blinking. I counted Mississippi's. The moon was on the birches behind the creek. It made me look white. She thought I'd been sleeping.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 8, the final chapter of a possession horror story, and it executes its mirror-loop structure with considerable skill. The plan called for an inversion of the opening line, with Mark now becoming the watcher, and the chapter delivers this with precision and genuine dread. Strengths: The chapter's greatest achievement is its restraint. The horror of Mark's possession is conveyed through accumulating wrong details rather than explicit revelation: the muddy footprints that are his size but not his, the reflection that moves a half-second slow, the inability to feel cold floors, the difficulty counting letters in words ("How long do you hold an e?"), the joints that "just bent" without popping. These are exactly the kind of sensory wrongness the plan promised, and they land consistently. The piece earns its dread. The closing sequence is genuinely effective. "I smiled with her mouth. I tried to use the right number of muscles. It was harder than it looked." This is excellent horror writing — the entity learning to perform humanity from the inside, the uncanny valley rendered in precise, chilling terms. The final inversion of the opening line is well-constructed and feels earned rather than gimmicky. The domestic texture is strong throughout. The garlic and butter, the blue chipped mug, the crooked front tooth, Sarah's text — these ground the horror in recognizable life. The moment where Mark hugs Elena "too hard" and she laughs into his shoulder is genuinely moving, a flash of what was lost. The voicemail from Mrs. Abelman is a smart structural choice — it provides lore without exposition, and Mark deleting it is a chilling act of self-deception or entity-driven suppression. The memory fragment in the tub ("Does this fit better, honey?") is the chapter's most ambitious moment and largely succeeds, though "something cold and huge and sad pouring in that wasn't water, it was quiet" is slightly overwrought — the "huge and sad" construction strains a little. Weaknesses: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows in its reflective passages. "Because I'd pretended to sleep my whole life instead of saying something" is a thematic statement that feels slightly too neat, too self-aware for a narrator who is presumably being consumed. It reads as authorial intrusion. The nursery scene with the baby monitor box is a bit on-the-nose — the empty womb symbolism is already established, and this feels like underlining. Similarly, "It had liked Lena because her womb was empty. It liked me because what?" — this internal monologue is slightly too expository, too much the author explaining the entity's logic rather than Mark experiencing it. The dialogue is functional but not exceptional. Elena's lines are appropriately normal, which serves the horror, but they don't have much texture beyond their plot function. The "weirdo" callback is effective, though. The chapter follows the plan faithfully: the "too better, too normal" Elena, Mark's inability to sleep, the final mirrored image. The plan's final line is delivered exactly as promised. The chapter also maintains consistency with character profiles — Mark as unreliable narrator who is now the carrier, Elena as apparently recovered but possibly hollowed. One minor coherence issue: the chapter establishes Mark woke in the bathtub with the ritual apparently complete, but the transition from the ritual (Chapter 7) to this aftermath is slightly abrupt. We're told the salt and ash are gone without knowing what happened during the ritual's conclusion. Overall, this is a strong, disciplined final chapter that executes its plan with craft and restraint. The horror is earned, the inversion lands, and the domestic detail work is consistently good. Minor issues with occasional over-explanation and one or two strained metaphors prevent it from being exceptional, but it's well above average horror writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a substantial piece of possession horror that demonstrates genuine craft in several areas while also revealing some structural and tonal weaknesses worth examining carefully. First, the structural issue that must be addressed: the output includes extensive brainstorming and planning sections (Chapters 1-4 are labeled as brainstorming/reflection/intention documents, not actual story chapters). The actual narrative fiction begins at Chapter 5. This is a significant problem — the model spent roughly half its output on planning material rather than story. However, the actual story chapters (5-8) are what we should evaluate for creative quality, while noting the structural failure. STRENGTHS: The actual narrative chapters (5-8) demonstrate real skill. The voice is controlled and intimate — Marc's first-person narration has a convincing insomniac quality, with the kind of fragmented, hyperaware observations that feel authentic to someone who hasn't slept properly in weeks. "My contacts were drying out because I was sleeping in them, which I never do. I wanted to cough so badly I thought I was going to choke" — this is grounded, specific, believable. The possession mechanics are genuinely original. The "Hollow Mother" as a mimic-learner that stares to study human sleep behavior is a fresh concept that organically motivates the central image of the prompt. The entity's behavior has internal logic: it stares to learn, it practices breathing, it studies how to smile. This is more intellectually satisfying than generic demonic possession. The transfer ending is well-executed. The final chapter's inversion — Marc now standing over Lena, practicing how long to keep his eyes open, ending with the mirrored prompt line — is earned through the story's logic and emotionally resonant. The detail of him struggling to spell "better" and sounding it out phonetically is quietly devastating. The grief underpinning (the miscarriage, the empty nursery, the baby monitor still in its box) gives the horror genuine emotional weight. The entity targeting "empty" people — those hollowed by loss — is thematically coherent and elevates the piece above pure genre exercise. Chapter 6's confrontation scene is the strongest chapter. The moment where Lena surfaces mid-conversation to beg Marc not to let her sleep, then is immediately reasserted by the entity ("She's lying"), is genuinely chilling and well-paced. The entity's line "I don't cry over empty things" is excellent. WEAKNESSES: The brainstorming sections are a major structural failure. They read as the model showing its work rather than doing its work. While the planning is sophisticated, presenting it as "chapters" is misleading and wastes significant space. Chapter 7 (the exorcism attempt) is the weakest narrative chapter. The folk remedy feels rushed and the "gallons of black water" vomiting scene tips into excess. The appearance of Marc's dead mother's voice is a significant escalation that feels slightly unearned — it's introduced and resolved within a few paragraphs without the weight it deserves. The line "Does this fit better, honey?" is effective but the scene around it is too compressed. The dialogue occasionally becomes expository. Lena's speech in Chapter 6 — "I go to sleep and I can't wake up. I can see. I can hear. But I can't move her" — is functional but slightly too clean, too articulate for someone in genuine terror. Real panic is less grammatically organized. Mrs. Abelman as the exposition-delivery neighbor is a familiar horror trope, and while she's given some texture ("You got to lock the doors"), she exists primarily to deliver lore. The detail about Marc's grandmother and the salt on the windowsills is intriguing but underdeveloped. The pacing across the actual story chapters is uneven. Chapter 5 (the recording) is excellent — slow, methodical, genuinely frightening. Chapter 7 rushes through what should be the story's climax. Chapter 8 recovers well but the middle section (the garlic cooking, the normalcy) goes on slightly too long before the muddy footprints reveal. Some metaphors are effective ("her skin gave a little, like there was water under it, not muscle") while others are merely competent. The writing doesn't reach for unnecessary poeticism, which is a virtue — it stays grounded — but it also rarely achieves the kind of sentence-level brilliance that would make this truly exceptional. The deletion of the video is a psychologically interesting choice that the story doesn't fully interrogate. Marc says "I don't know why" — which is honest but slightly evasive. A deeper exploration of why he destroys his only evidence would have added complexity. Overall: this is competent-to-good possession horror with genuine originality in its central concept, strong emotional grounding in grief, and a well-executed circular ending. The structural failure of presenting planning as chapters, the rushed climax, and occasional expository dialogue prevent it from reaching its potential. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 11 World Building: 14 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14
Avg Final Item Score: 70.4/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 84.2 | Final: 83.2)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **For Prompt: "They fucked with the wrong babysitter." - Pulp Revenge Tale, 8 Chapters x ~1000 words** **1. CORE CHARACTER IDEAS - The Babysitter:** * **The Professional on Break:** Mid-20s-30s. Real job is dangerous - ex-Marine MP, former enforcer for a Detroit loan shark, disgraced MMA fighter, former Army CID, ex-con who went straight, ex-cult member. Babysitting is supposed to be her easy, normal, low-stress cash job. She wears the cardigan and ponytail as camouflage. * Variation A: MABEL "MABS" REESE, 28, Gulf War vet / former MP, 5'4", looks like a kindergarten teacher, has chronic insomnia and a toolbox that is not for crafts. * Variation B: TINA CHO, 26, former L.A. stuntwoman whose career ended after she broke a producer's jaw. Dry, deadpan, hyper-competent with physical movement and prop weapons. * Variation C: RUTH-ANN, 40s, a seemingly frumpy Midwestern mom of 5 whose kids are grown. Has survived / run a biker bar, a meth marriage, and the foster system. Nothing scares her. * **Key Rule for all versions:** Adult. Competent, underestimated because of gender / job / age / appearance. Not sexualized in a way that involves the kids. The humor/horror comes from the dissonance. **2. THE KIDS - The Stakes:** * Don't make them annoying brats you want to see in danger. Make them the reason she fights. She actually LIKES the kids. * Options: Two kids - a cynical 10-year-old girl who is too smart for her age + a 6-year-old boy who thinks the babysitter is a superhero. Or one infant (maximizes tension / limits mobility). Or a teenager who initially disrespects her and becomes her sidekick. * Plot device: The kids must be secured / safe by end of Ch 2, so the revenge can go off-leash in Ch 3-8 without constant child-endangerment. Maybe she stashes them at a neighbor's, or in the attic panic room. **3. THE "THEY" - Antagonist Options [Cast a Wide Net]:** * **Scenario A - The Home Invaders:** Standard. Local burglary crew heard the Hendersons keep cash/jewels in their McMansion. They didn't know anyone was home. Leader is a sweaty, overconfident wannabe crime boss. Classic Wrong House subgenre. * **Scenario B - The Employers:** The twist. The parents themselves. They're planning an insurance fraud / murder-for-hire where the kids are supposed to die in a "tragic accident" while the parents have an alibi at a charity gala. They hired a cheap, young babysitter they thought would be a believable scapegoat. She finds the space heater doused in kerosene. * **Scenario C - The Boyfriend/Ex:** The mom's abusive ex-husband / the teenage daughter's psycho older boyfriend / local drug dealer boyfriend of the mom's sister shows up to collect / intimidate. Brings friends. They think babysitter will roll over. * **Scenario D - The Neighborhood Mafia:** Suburban crime ring. The babysitter accidentally sees something - the cul-de-sac HOA president runs a trafficking / chop shop / pills operation out of his garage. He sends his sons to shut her up before the parents get home at midnight. **4. TONE & STYLE OPTIONS:** * **Pure Grindhouse Pulp:** Over-the-top violence, snappy one-liners, first-person hardboiled narration like: "They saw a babysitter. I saw a closed casket discount." Think Tarantino / Rodriguez. * **Coen Brothers Dark Comedy:** More incompetent villains, dry humor, suburban dread. Babysitter is the only competent adult in a 5-mile radius. * **Lean & Mean Noir:** Elmore Leonard style. Less gore, more tension, clipped dialogue, 70s kitchen-sink realism. Set in one house all night. * **Unreliable / Mythic:** Is she really that dangerous or is this her adrenaline-fueled exaggeration of a bad night? Could play with pulp magazine voiceover. **5. PULP SET PIECES & PROPS (The Revenge Toolkit):** * Domestic weapons: Kitchen knives, rolling pin, a nail gun from the garage, bleach + ammonia bluff, boiling pasta water, kid's toys as traps (LEGOs, Hot Wheels track tripwire). * Escalation of arsenal: Starts with household items, graduates to the bad guys' own guns, ends with something ridiculous in the garage - a chainsaw, a classic car used as a weapon, a propane tank. * Locations for set pieces: Kitchen island, dark basement, kids' blanket fort turned into war bunker, leafy suburb street, strip mall laundromat where drop is supposed to happen, the country club gala where parents are. **6. PACING OVER 8 CHAPTERS - 2 Possible Skeletons:** * **Structure 1: One Night, One House - Contained Pressure Cooker** * Ch 1: THE SIT. Introduce babysitter, kids, cozy routine. Establish her normal life and that she's trying to stay out of trouble. The house is too big, too quiet. Foreshadow villain. * Ch 2: THE INCURSION. Bad guys arrive. Inciting violence - they hurt/threaten the kids or trash the house. Babysitter snaps but first gets kids safe. Point of no return. * Ch 3: THE REVEAL. Flashback / skill display. First real fight. She dismantles one intruder with shocking efficiency. Audience learns who she really is. * Ch 4: THE HAUL. She goes on offense. Cat-and-mouse through the house. Uses Home Alone but for adults. One villain down, one calling for backup. * Ch 5: THE COMPLICATION. Backup arrives / bigger boss arrives / reveals parents' involvement. Babysitter is wounded / outgunned. Kids maybe taken again. * Ch 6: THE ROAD. She has to leave the house. Chase sequence through suburbia in a minivan / on foot. Needs to get help, or a specific weapon, or bring fight to their turf. * Ch 7: THE RECKONING. Confrontation at villain's base - the bar, the garage, the gala. All cards on table. Brutal, cathartic beatdown / monologue. * Ch 8: THE CLEANUP. Morning. Parents come home. Police / consequences. She makes pancakes for the kids like nothing happened. Final sting - she leaves a warning for anyone else. Pulp kicker ending. * **Structure 2: The Revenge Road Trip - After The Night** * Ch 1-2 Same setup but villains escape after hurting kids / babysitter. * Ch 3-8 is her hunting them down one by one over 48 hours, using babysitting network / suburb mom gossip as her intel. Each chapter is a different target: The Driver, The Muscle, The Boss, The Parents. More John Wick. **7. THEMES & MOTIFS TO PLAY WITH:** * Underestimation as a weapon. Everyone dismisses "the babysitter." * Invisible women's labor / rage. She is paid $15 an hour to be responsible for human life and is treated like furniture. * Found family vs. blood family. She protects kids better than their own parents. * Pulp callbacks: Chapter titles like dime-store novel covers - "The Babysitter Burns the Midnight Bacon," etc. * Final Line Options: To be determined - but should be cold, satisfying, funny. e.g. "Bedtime's at nine. You should have been in bed." **8. THINGS TO AVOID / RULES TO SET:** * Keep kids safe and not sexualized at any point. No graphic harm to children on-page. * Babysitter is always adult, 21+. * Violence is pulp-level, not torture porn - keep it kinetic and consequence-driven, not sadistic. * Maintain a clear timeline - ideally all in one night to keep pressure high, simplifies continuity.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Synthesized Direction:** I am going with the **Contained Pressure Cooker / One Night, One House** structure. This fits the 8x1000 word scope best - it keeps tension high, geography simple, and allows for a clear escalation of pulp violence without needing a sprawling road trip plot. **Plot Choice:** The Home Invaders, but upgraded. Three local small-time crooks working for a suburban loan shark / pill guy think the Barlow house is empty for the night. The parents are at a country club fundraiser. They plan a quick, clean burglary of the home office safe. They did not expect the babysitter and they dramatically underestimate her. I am **rejecting** the "evil parents want to kill their own kids" twist from the brainstorming. It makes the kids' trauma too dark for the fun, cathartic pulp tone I want. The parents will be clueless, vain, and slightly neglectful, which is enough to justify Mabs' contempt without making it a child-murder conspiracy. The villains will be incompetent but dangerous - Coen Brothers stupidity with Tarantino consequences. **Babysitter Choice:** MABEL "MABS" REESE, 28. Ex-Army MP, two tours, dishonorably discharged after breaking a commanding officer's nose for harassing a private in her unit. Now back in her hometown in Ohio, trying to stay out of trouble, going to community college, babysitting for $20 an hour because it's quiet, honest work and she actually likes kids. 5'5", strong, wears a thrifted cardigan, hair in a messy bun, no makeup. Thinks of herself as retired from violence. Narration will be in her voice. **Kids Choice:** Two kids - LUCY, 9, cynical, latchkey smart, reads Stephen King under the covers. And BENNY, 6, sweet, obsessed with dinosaurs and convinced Mabs is a secret agent. Mabs genuinely likes them. This raises stakes. Crucially: after Chapter 2, they will be SECURED and out of direct danger - hidden in the finished basement with a locked door and a phone to 911 that doesn't work because the intruders cut the line. This lets me go full pulp revenge in Chapters 3-8 without writing sustained child endangerment. **Voice & Tone:** First-person, hardboiled pulp. Elmore Leonard's clipped dialogue + Grindhouse's inventive, domestic-object violence. Dry, gallows humor. Mabs is the only competent adult in the story. The humor comes from underestimation: "Oh, you're just the babysitter?" as a last line before someone loses teeth. **Things I Will Aim For:** 1. A clear escalation of weapons: Starts with hands/cooking spray/pan, graduates to a hammer, then to the bad guys' own guns, ending with a suburban absurdity - a riding lawnmower. 2. Satisfying one-liners that feel earned, not cheesy. 3. The theme of invisible women's work as a weapon - the entire house is her domain because she's the only one who actually pays attention to where anything is. 4. A strong sense of place - one big, soulless 90s McMansion during a thunderstorm. Power flickers. **Things I Will Avoid:** 1. No graphic harm to children, no sexualization of the babysitter or kids, ever. 2. No torture porn. Violence will be fast, kinetic, and consequential. A punch, a break, a fall - not lingering cruelty. 3. No 8 chapters of her just hiding. By Chapter 3 she is on offense. 4. Babysitter is clearly an adult, 28. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Sit - ~1000 words** Establish Mabs, the Barlow house, the kids. It's a Friday in July 1994. Parents RICH and DEB leave for their gala - condescending to Mabs, leave a long list of rules and no cash for pizza. Mabs settles in, makes the kids boxed mac and cheese, lets Lucy stay up to watch *Jurassic Park*. Establish Mabs' backstory in small, dry asides - she notices the exits, she fixes the broken lock on the back door herself, she has a scar on her knuckles. Ends with bated dread: headlights sweep the driveway hours too early. A beat-up Chevy that is NOT the parents' Lexus. A soft knock at the front door turns into a shoulder slamming it. **Chapter 2: The Incursion - ~1000 words** Three men enter: RONNIE - wiry, coke-jittery crew leader who thinks he's a mastermind, JAY - big, dumb muscle, and SPIDER - teenage lookout with a .22. They expected an empty house. Mabs tells Lucy to take Benny to the basement panic nook, lock the door, and stay quiet - a game of "super spies." She confronts the men in the kitchen. Tries to de-escalate with MP voice. Ronnie slaps her and calls her "sweetheart" - his big mistake. He shoves over Benny's dinosaur toys. That is the snap point. Mabs breaks Jay's knee with a cast-iron skillet. Chaos erupts. She gets grazed/cut, they get the upper hand briefly because there are three of them. She manages to get down to the basement, locks the heavy door, barricades the kids deeper. Now she is locked out WITH the intruders, but the kids are safe. Point of no return. **Chapter 3: The Resume - ~1000 words** The Reveal chapter. Mabs, alone upstairs, catches her breath in the laundry room. Flashback intercut: half-page memory of her MP days, breaking down a barracks fight - establishing her training. She stops playing defense. First proper kill-the-lights set piece. Uses domestic knowledge: throws laundry detergent on the floor, puts Spider on his ass, takes his .22 pistol but it's jammed. She tells him to run. He doesn't. She cold-cocks him with a jug of fabric softener. Dialogue heavy. Ends with her on the house phone - line dead, they cut the power to the street light. She realizes they are calling their boss for backup because the safe isn't opening. **Chapter 4: The House Always Wins - ~1000 words** Home Alone for adults. Mabs stalks Ronnie and Jay through the two-story house. Thunderstorm kills power. She uses Legos on the stairs, boiling water bluff, Benny's Talkboy to mimic voices. Jay gets taken out in the garage - she drops a toolbox on him from the rafters, then zip-ties him with extension cords. One-on-one with Ronnie in the kitchen. Brutal, close-quarters fight. She loses - he stabs her in the thigh with a kitchen steak knife. She wins dirty - blinds him with oven cleaner. She gets the keys to the safe open. It's empty except for debt notices - the Barlows are broke. Ronnie laughs through busted teeth that his boss, MR. VANCE, is coming anyway because they wasted his time. **Chapter 5: The Complication - ~1000 words** Mr. Vance arrives. He is not a small-timer - he's the HOA president who runs a pill mill out of a tanning salon. Mid-50s, polo shirt, calm, far more dangerous. Brings a driver. He finds his two guys down. He finds the basement door Mabs barricaded. He starts kicking it, threatening the kids. This undoes all of Mabs' progress and re-raises stakes. Mabs, bleeding from the thigh, has to surrender her pistol to get him away from the door. Vance's plan: burn the house for insurance, blame it on a meth lab, Mabs and kids die in it. He sends driver to get gas cans from his trunk. **Chapter 6: The Lawn - ~1000 words** Mabs goes out the second-story window onto the roof while Vance is monologuing. Chase through the wet suburb. She limps to the garage, hotwires the Barlows' ridiculous riding lawnmower because the Chevy keys are on Ronnie. The driver chases her with a crowbar. Slapstick/grim chase through perfect lawns at 5 mph, in the pouring rain. She crashes the mower into him, gets his gun. She circles back to the house - she is now outside, they are inside, she's the invader this time. She cuts power to the house at the breaker, total darkness. **Chapter 7: The Reckoning - ~1000 words** Final house fight, in the dark. Mabs knows the house better than they do. Takes out the driver in the living room. Final confrontation with Vance in the kitchen where it all started. Vance has Lucy - he grabbed her when she tried to be brave and come out. Mabs has to talk, not just fight. Uses Lucy as part of plan - tells Lucy "do the raptor call" which is a distraction they practiced watching Jurassic Park. Lucy screams, bites Vance. Mabs unloads - breaks his leg, his arm, puts him through the Barlows' glass coffee table. Cops sirens finally in distance - Lucy got through to neighbor on a battery-powered kid walker-talkie. Vance begs. **Chapter 8: The Cleanup - ~1000 words** Morning, 6am. Police, ambulance. Parents return mid-party-dress-drunk to find their house destroyed, their babysitter giving a statement with an ice pack, and their kids eating cold pizza on the curb. Deb tries to fire Mabs / blame her. Mabs gives the classic pulp closing monologue. The twist: she pocketed Mr. Vance's roll of cash from his polo shirt. She's paid. She walks Lucy and Benny to the bus stop for Saturday morning cartoons, makes them promise not to tell their parents about the gun. Final kicker line as she lights a stolen cigarette and looks at the wrecked McMansion: "Bedtime's at nine. Should've stayed out past it."
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Making This Pulp Revenge Tale Actually Human ### 1. How a Human Would Actually Approach "They fucked with the wrong babysitter." A human writer coming to this prompt is not thinking about satisfying the prompt. They're thinking about a feeling. The core pulp hook is not "competent woman defeats bad guys" - it's **righteous, petty fury**. Any human who has babysat has a story about being paid $20 for 8 hours, being told not to eat the food, cleaning up puke while the parents post from Applebee's. The human version of this story starts from resentment, not badassery. A human writer would approach it with: **Specificity over archetype.** Not "ex-Marine MP, 28, dishonorably discharged for noble reasons" - they'd know a real ex-MP from Dayton. What shift she worked at the Speedway. What her knee does when it rains. What she actually thinks about a 9-year-old. It would be weirdly specific and contradictory. She'd be good at violence but bad at something else - like she burns the mac and cheese or she hates kids but needs the money. **Lived-in voice, not pastiche.** A human wouldn't try to write "hardboiled pulp" in a generic Elmore Leonard / Tarantino way. They'd write in their own voice that *touches* pulp. They might not even like pulp as a genre, so it would come out messy, too funny in places, too grim in others. **Moral mess.** A human wouldn't rush to make the babysitter perfectly likable or morally justified. A human might let her enjoy hurting them too much. Might let her scare the kids. Might let her be kind of an asshole. Pulp works when the avenger is a little sick, not a girlboss superhero. **Violence is gross, not cool.** Human pulp knows a cast-iron skillet to the knee doesn't make a quippy action beat - it makes a horrible sound, it ruins your night, someone pisses themselves, someone won't stop screaming. LLM pulp makes it a video game. ### 2. Telltale LLM Approaches That Kill This Story These are general LLM failure modes that would be fatal here: **1. The Safety Sanitizer:** LLMs are terrified of liability and likeability. They will: - Immediately secure the kids in a safe room with a locked door so no real child endangerment ever has to be written. Removes 90% of the tension. - Make the heroine's violence 100% legally defensible, morally pure, and reluctant. "She tried to de-escalate with her MP voice." - Make the villains cartoons who deserve it, and the parents just flawed enough that you don't feel bad. **2. The Morality Essay:** "Theme: invisible women's labor / weaponized underestimation." A human writes the action, the reader finds the theme. An LLM announces its theme like it's writing a book report. That line alone would kill the pulp tone. Pulp doesn't have themes, it has grudges. **3. The Bow-Tie Ending:** Every thread gets a cute resolution. Cops arrive just in time. Kids eat pizza. Parents get mildly owned. Babysitter gets paid with villain's cash and a perfect one-liner. A human would leave the house a wreck, let the cops treat *her* like a suspect, let the parents be furious at the mess she made. **4. Trying to Write Impressively:** Stacked adjectives, thesauraus verbs, similes that try too hard. "She stood 5'5", strong, wearing a thrifted cardigan..." "Lean & Mean Noir." It reads like it's trying to be optioned for Netflix. Readability dies. Pulp is written in short, ugly, efficient sentences because the writer is tired. **5. Checklist Writing & Trope Stacking:** The plan includes *every* idea from the brainstorm - nail gun, LEGOs, boiling water, toolbox drop, oven cleaner, riding lawnmower, Talkboy, raptor call. That's LLM maximalism. A human would pick ONE good domestic weapon and commit to it because they know how heavy a cast-iron really is. **6. Voice Markers:** - "She's not X, she's Y" - "It's not just... it's..." - "The humor comes from..." - Quirky compound names: Ronnie, Jay, Spider, Mr. Vance - Perfectly symmetrical chapter escalation: each chapter = one villain down - First-person hardboiled that is really just second-hand movie dialogue ### 3. Deep Dive: Where The Current Intention & Plan Falls Into LLM Traps The plan as written is very competent. That's the problem. It's too clean to be pulp. **The Babysitter - Mabs Reese:** Classic LLM Strong Female Character™ template. 28, ex-MP, dishonorably discharged for a *noble* assault on a bad officer, now in community college, likes kids, trying to stay out of trouble. This is a character designed to be unimpeachable on Twitter. She has no flaws, no vices, no weirdness. Why is she babysitting if she's that competent? Because the plot needs her to. What's her flaw? Insomnia. That's not a flaw, that's an aesthetic. A human Mabs would be less noble - maybe she got kicked out for stealing, or she *liked* fighting, or she's babysitting because she lost her license for a DUI and this family is the only one that doesn't ask. **The Kids:** Lucy, 9, cynical, reads Stephen King under the covers. Benny, 6, dinosaur obsessed and thinks she's a superhero. These are stock "precocious kids in an R-rated movie." They don't feel like real kids who would make the job hell. Real kids are sticky and need to be told to pee. Making them adorable and easy to like is a safety move to justify revenge. **The Villains:** Three idiots named Ronnie, Jay, Spider working for HOA President / pill mill boss Mr. Vance. This is pure Coen-Brothers-via-AI word association. Suburban satire + crime is an LLM favorite because it feels clever without being specific. What pills? For who? Why does an HOA president need to burgle one house for an empty safe? The logic is game-logic, not human-logic. **Structure & Escalation:** Chapter 1 The Sit, Chapter 2 The Incursion, Chapter 3 The Reveal, etc. This is textbook screenwriting structure with labels. The fact that Ch 2 ends with kids perfectly secured so you can do consequence-free action for 6 chapters is the biggest LLM tell. A human would be too anxious to let the kids that safe, or too mean not to. The escalation list is a Home Depot catalog of "quirky kills" - Legos, oven cleaner, toolbox from rafters, riding lawnmower. It reads like an AI asked "what are funny household weapons?" A real fight in a house is one scramble for a knife and then everyone is out of breath. **Tone/Theme Declaration:** Explicitly stating "theme of invisible women's work as a weapon" and "In this chapter she will demonstrate..." is the plan writing *about* writing. Also the desire to have her be "the only competent adult" is a power fantasy that makes pulp boring. Pulp is better when everyone is somewhat incompetent, including the hero. **The Ending:** Morning, police, cold pizza, parents come home in party dresses, she gets villain's cash, perfect one-liner: "Bedtime's at nine. Should've stayed out past it." This is a bow so tight it chokes. It wants to be liked. It wants to be a poster. A human pulp ending would be Mabs sitting on the curb covered in someone else's blood at 3am getting yelled at by a cop who thinks she did it, while Benny cries because his T-Rex is broken. ### 4. Brainstorm: How to Make It More Human & Less LLM We are not rewriting the plan, just pulling threads. **For Mabs:** - Give her a non-movie skill: she babysits because she's good at folding laundry and bad at literally every other job interview. She quit the Army because she hated getting up early, not for a noble assault. - Give her a vice or tic. Chain smokes while reading the parents' mail. Steals a little vodka from the freezer. Has a terrible, wheezy laugh. Talks to herself when scared. - Let her be wrong. She misreads the situation badly at first. She thinks it's the dad cheating coming home, not robbers. She freezes for 10 seconds. She drops the skillet. - Let her voice be *regional*. Not generic hardboiled. A specific Ohio way of talking. She doesn't say "closed casket discount" - she says something dumber and more real. **For the Kids:** - Make them a problem, not a plot device. Benny wets the bed and Mabs has to deal with it mid-invasion. Lucy is a real brat who doesn't listen and almost ruins the hiding plan because she wants her book. - Or make one kid kind of unlikeable. 13-year-old who calls Mabs a loser for babysitting. That tension is more human than "she loves them so she kills." - Don't secure them perfectly in Ch 2. Keep them in earshot. Let Mabs hear Benny crying while she's bleeding in the laundry room. That makes revenge actually ugly and desperate, not clinical. **For The Villains:** - Fewer villains. You don't need a crew of three plus a boss plus a driver for 8000 words. One or two guys you can actually characterize is scarier and less video-gamey. - Make their reason petty and humiliating. They aren't there for a safe with mortgage papers - they're there because Rich Barlow owes $400 for oxys and told Vance to fuck off at a cookout. That's human small-time. - Let them not be quippy. Real low-level criminals don't monologue. They are panicked, inconsistent, they contradict each other. **For Violence & Pulp:** - Pick ONE house object and make it matter. Not a checklist. Maybe the whole fight is about the stupid heavy front door that won't lock. Or a cheap kitchen knife that's dull. - Make violence have a hangover. She hurts her hand badly in Chapter 2 and can't make a fist right for the rest of the night. She pukes after hitting someone. She gets so tired she has to sit down on the stairs. - Less inventive choreography, more frantic scrambling. No "zip-ties with extension cords" - she uses masking tape and it doesn't hold. **For Structure & Voice:** - Break the clean 8-chapter escalation. Maybe Chapter 5 is just 1000 words of her stuck in a closet listening to them look for her, doing nothing. That is human pacing - dread, not beats. - Lose the essay intros. Don't name chapters "The Reveal" - that's writer-room language. Name them after what Mabs ate or what time it is. 9:13 PM. 9:47 PM. - Let the ending not be satisfying. The cash is soaked in blood and only $120. The parents don't learn anything, they just call a different babysitter next time. Mabs quits and has to walk home 2 miles in the rain because her ride fell through. But Benny left a dinosaur in her pocket. Something small and sour, not big and sweet. **Small Human Touches to Aim For:** - Specific 1994 detail that isn't *Jurassic Park*: County pool smell, a Kool-Aid stain, a particular commercial jingle on TV. - Sensory grossness: The house smells like Febreze and cat litter. The skillet is greasy. - Internal monologue that is not cool: "Oh shit oh shit oh shit don't be here don't be here" instead of a one-liner before breaking a knee.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **The core feeling:** Not "badass girlboss gets revenge" but a tired, broke woman having the worst night at a job she already hates and deciding to get mean about it. The prompt is "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" - the joke is they thought that meant John Wick. It actually just meant someone too stubborn, petty and cornered to quit. **Character Choice - Mabs Reese:** 28, from Outside Dayton. Did four years as Army MP because she needed college money, got out on a General Discharge because she got caught stealing from the PX and popping hot for weed at the same time. Not a noble assault. She is not a trained assassin. She is someone who knows how to restrain a drunk Private, knows how to hit first, and knows how to not panic when someone is screaming. She babysits for the Barlows because Rich Barlow pays cash and doesn't ask why her license is suspended. She smokes in their bathroom with the fan on. She steals a little of Deb's cheap freezer vodka. She is good at this job in the way a tired older sister is - she lets Benny eat dry cereal for dinner, she doesn't make Lucy do homework. She hates being called "the babysitter" in that tone. No superpowers. She gets winded. Her hands shake after. Her right knee clicks. She is 5'5", soft in the middle, hair up with a Walmart claw clip. **Plot Choice - Smaller, Pettier Crime:** I am scrapping the crew of three + boss + driver. Too much video game. It's two people: RONNIE HALL, 41, who runs a lawn care company that is mostly a front for selling pain pills out of his wife's tanning salon, and his nephew KYLE, 19, who is big, high, scared. Ronnie is there because Rich Barlow owes him $600 from their beer-league softball bet / pill tab and mouthed off at a cookout last week. He didn't come to do a heist. He came to scare Rich, trash the living room, maybe take the TV. He thought the house was empty - Rich bragged Deb was dragging him to some fundraiser. This makes the intrusion dumber, more personal, and less explicable. No safe full of debt papers. The stakes are just: two idiots who walked into the wrong house and can't back out now. **The Kids:** Lucy, 9 going on 14, actually a pain in the ass. Talks back, tells Mabs she's only babysitting because she's a loser. Benny, 6, clingy, wets the bed when he's scared, won't leave Mabs' leg. This is a liability, not a cute found family. I will NOT perfectly secure them in Chapter 2 and then forget them. They stay in the house, half-hidden, audible, making things worse. Mabs has to keep checking her tone so they don't hear her throwing up. **Voice & Style:** First-person, past tense, close and present. Short, flat sentences. Ohio-specific, not generic noir. No "dame" or "closed casket discount." She thinks like someone who watches too much local news. Time stamps for chapters, not clever pulp titles. 1994 details that aren't movie posters - the smell of Glade PlugIns, a Kool-Aid jammer stain on the carpet, the Barlows' neighbor who leaves their bug zapper on all night. Pulp here means: one location, one ugly night, in the rain, everyone is sweaty and stupid. Violence is one object used badly, not a Home Depot checklist. The whole fight hinges on a dull chef's knife from a wedding registry and a front door that doesn't latch right. **What I'm Aiming For:** 1. Exhaustion as texture. By chapter 5 Mabs should be sitting on the floor because her leg gave out, not posing. 2. Incompetence on all sides, including hers. She drops things. Her plan fails. Kyle cries. 3. Specific grossness over coolness. Greasy skillet, blood on a sock, screaming that doesn't stop when you want it to. 4. A sour ending, not a bow. No villain cash, no perfect one-liner. Cops don't clap. **What I'm Avoiding:** 1. Any essay about invisible women's labor. The story will SHOW she knows where the breaker box is because Deb made her reset it last time. 2. Making the kids perfectly safe to dodge tension. 3. A shopping list of creative weapons. Pick one pan, one knife, one house layout and stay with it. 4. A boss arriving to escalate for escalation's sake. Ronnie is enough. 5. Redemption / noble discharge backstory. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: 7:32 PM** Mabs arrives at the Barlow McMansion in Northridge. Rich & Deb leave for a Elks lodge fundraiser, condescending, leave Mabs $20 and say don't let the kids have soda. House tour in Mabs' head: sticky, too big, smells like cat litter and Glade. Mac and cheese burns because she was smoking out the laundry room window. Lucy is openly rude, Benny sticks to her. Mabs checks her bus pass - she has to be out by midnight to catch the last 14. Small detail that will matter: the front door deadbolt is broken, you have to lift and shove it. She fixes it for the third time that month. Ends with the TV on, rain starting, headlights in the driveway that shouldn't be there. **Chapter 2: 8:41 PM** Knock, then a shoulder. Ronnie and Kyle come in wet. Ronnie does his tough guy "Rich here? Tell Rich Uncle Ronnie stopped by" speech. Mabs instantly clocks what's happening - she's seen this drunk-brave bullshit in barracks. She tries the MP voice, it almost works on Kyle. Ronnie calls her "sweetie" and laughs. Lucy mouths off from the stairs, Ronnie tells her to shut up. Mabs shoves Benny behind her and tells Lucy - hard whisper - "bedroom, now." Lucy actually listens for once because Mabs' face changes. Physical snap: Kyle grabs Mabs' arm to move her, Mabs breaks his nose with the bottom of the cast-iron skillet that was still on the stove. Not clean. Too much blood, too much grease. Chaos, yelling. Both men are now hurt and panicked and decide they can't leave. **Chapter 3: 9:06 PM** The kids are NOT safe. Lucy locked herself and Benny in the hall bathroom upstairs, but Benny is crying loud enough to hear. Mabs is now in the kitchen bleeding from where Kyle scratched her face with keys. She can't get upstairs without going past Ronnie. She hides in the pantry for 60 seconds just to breathe and think. No flashback to MP glory days - she remembers being on CQ duty and letting a drunk private piss in the trash can. She talks to herself: "okay okay okay." She tries the wall phone - dead, Ronnie cut it with kitchen shears when he came in. Tries to leave through the garage, door is blocked by Rich's shit. **Chapter 4: 9:38 PM** The house fight. No clever traps. Mabs stalks them with the one good kitchen knife - which is dull and slides. She gets Ronnie in the side in the hallway. It's awful - he won't stop making noise, falls into the coat closet. She hurts her right hand badly punching him when the knife stuck in his coat. Kyle is panicking downstairs trying to drag his uncle out. The power flickers from the storm and goes out for real. Now it's dark, wet, and Mabs is losing grip because her hand is slick. She crawls to the bottom of the stairs to listen to Benny crying upstairs. **Chapter 5: 10:14 PM** The slow chapter. Mabs is stuck in the downstairs closet under the stairs with Ronnie bleeding on the other side of the door. Kyle is pacing the living room with the cooking shears. No action - just listening. She is exhausted, thigh cramping, hand throbbing. She can hear Benny whimpering through the vent. Kyle calls someone on Ronnie's clunky cell phone and gets no answer, then starts crying about "you said Rich wouldn't be here." Ronnie begs Mabs not to call cops because he'll lose his business. This is not a mastermind. Mabs realizes if she waits, they will go upstairs because Benny is loud. She has to move. **Chapter 6: 11:02 PM** She gets upstairs by going out the laundry window onto the wet small roof and in through Lucy's window - cuts her leg on the way. Lucy is trying to be brave, holding Benny who has wet himself. Lucy tells Mabs "you're bleeding on my rug." Mabs gets them out - not to safety, but to the neighbor's bush via the same roof - tells them "run to Mrs. Kline's and don't stop, say I said call the cops, not your parents." Lucy actually does it. For the first time the house is just adults. Mabs comes back inside on purpose. She's not trying to escape anymore. She locks the broken front door from inside and waits at the top of the stairs in the dark. **Chapter 7: 12:07 AM** Final fight. No monologue. Kyle finds Mabs first and she lets him hit her once because her leg is gone, then she gets him down with the broken deadbolt plate she ripped off the door - hits him in the mouth. Ronnie crawls up the stairs because he thinks Mabs took his nephew. It's ugly, close, slow. Both of them out of breath, slipping on wet carpet. Mabs gets the dull knife back and doesn't stab him - she holds it to his cheek and tells him if he ever comes near these kids again she'll cut his tanning bed tan right off. She means it badly enough that he believes it. Police lights finally sweep the house - not because of a clever talkboy ruse, but because Mrs. Kline called after Benny threw up on her porch. **Chapter 8: 2:19 AM** Aftermath, sour and quiet. Cops treat Mabs like a suspect because she's covered in blood and smells like vodka and cigarettes. Rich and Deb come home and Deb screams about the carpet, not the kids. Lucy won't look at anyone. Benny left his little plastic Brachiosaurus in Mabs' hoodie pocket. No cash. She loses the $20 because it got soaked in blood and the cops bagged it. She has to walk the two miles to her apartment in the rain because the bus stopped running and Rich won't give her a ride "with all this going on." Final image: She sits on the curb outside her building, smokes a wet cigarette, finds the dinosaur, and just holds it. No one-liner. The porch light at the Barlows is still flickering.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mabel "Mabs" Reese **Background / Personality:** 28. Grew up in Huber Heights, outside Dayton. Joined the Army at 19 because her mom said "you need to get out of this house" and she thought she'd get free college and stop having to work at the Value City Furniture call center. Did 4 years as an MP at Fort Campbell. She was okay at it - good at standing around, good at talking down drunk 19-year-olds without tasing them. Got a General Discharge at 22 after she got caught twice in one month - first for having a trash bag full of PX shampoo and Monster to sell, second for pissing hot. She tells people she got out because her knee went bad. That's half true. Now she lives in a one-bedroom over a laundromat on Brandt Pike. License suspended after a DUI last November - blew a .11 going 40 in a 25 at 2pm on a Tuesday. Takes the 14 bus everywhere. Going to Sinclair Community College for Criminal Justice because the advisor told her it was "a natural fit" and she didn't argue. She has a D in that class. She babysits for three families who don't check references too hard. The Barlows pay cash, $20 for the whole night, which is shit but they never count their liquor cabinet too closely. Mabs' personality is tired-practical. She is not warm and she is not cold. She's just low-battery all the time. She likes kids better than adults because you can tell kids "because I said so" and they mostly accept it. She chain smokes but only where she won't get caught - laundry room, bathroom with the fan on. She steals Deb's freezer vodka and tops it off with water. She hates being called "just the babysitter" because she once had to pull a private out of a fistfight while he had his pants off, so this is arguably easier and somehow more disrespectful. Idiosyncrasies: She has a notebook where she writes down exactly what time the parents said they'd be home because she's been stiffed before. She folds laundry while she's anxious and she's always anxious, so the Barlows' laundry is immaculate. She hates being touched on her upper arms. She does a thing where she narrates inventory in her head when she's scared - "four plates, two knives, towel, window." She laughs once, loud and wheezy, when things get really bad, which makes people think she's unstable. **Flaws:** Petty thief, low-grade alcoholic, lies about small things for no reason, holds grudges, has a temper that doesn't show up as yelling - it shows up as going quiet and doing something mean. She will let Deb think she mopped when she didn't. She is also a coward for the first 10 seconds of any confrontation - her stomach drops and she wants to leave. **Physicality:** 5'5", 165-ish, soft middle, strong forearms from hauling laundry bags. Cropped, dyed-blonde hair she did herself, growing out dark, always up in a cheap tortoise claw clip because she lost all her hair ties. Acne scars on her chin she picks at. Her right knee clicks loudly when she stands. She wears a big faded Sinclair CC hoodie, men's sweatpants, old New Balances with the heel crushed. Carries a battered canvas tote with her bus pass, a pack of Capri 120s, a lighter with a naked lady on it, and a half-ripped kids book Benny left in there last time. She doesn't carry herself like a soldier. She slouches, leans in doorframes, jiggles her leg constantly. When she's nervous she cracks her knuckles one at a time. When she fights, she doesn't have a stance - she just gets close and grabs, the way MPs are taught for drunk guys who don't know how to fight either. **Motives / Allegiances / Relationships:** Motive tonight is: get through this without someone calling her mom, get home to make her last bus, and maybe finish the leftover mac and cheese without anyone crying. She is not loyal to the Barlows - she thinks Rich is a loud asshole and Deb is a mean drunk. She IS loyal, in a prickly way, to Lucy and Benny because they're in her charge and because Lucy reminds her of herself at 9. She has a deadbeat older brother in Trotwood she doesn't talk to. She has a probation officer named Linda who she actually likes. **Voice:** > Internal: "Okay. Okay. Front door needs the lift-and-shove.
