Plates, knives, bleach under the sink, Benny crying, Lucy being a shit. Lift and shove." > Out loud to Ronnie, dead flat: "You need to step outside now. You are not supposed to be in this house." > After breaking Kyle's nose: "Oh, fuck -- okay, you grabbed me, you grabbed me first, okay?" # Lucy Barlow **Background / Personality:** 9 years old, fourth grade at Northmoor Elementary. The kind of kid who learned early that being smart is a way to be left alone. Her parents fight about money in the kitchen and then act fake-nice when she comes in, so she pretends she doesn't hear. She hates Mabs at the start of every sit because Mabs is an adult who doesn't pretend to be her friend. Then she mostly tolerates her because Mabs lets her drink coffee and watch what she wants. She reads adult paperbacks she steals from Deb's nightstand - mostly Dean Koontz because Deb thinks that's literature. She uses big words wrong on purpose to see if anyone will correct her. She tells Benny that babysitters are all failed criminals. She is mean in the specific way of kids whose parents are vaguely embarrassed of them. Trivial anchor: She has a chipped front tooth from falling off a scooter Rich bought and didn't tighten. She wears a too-small Lisa Frank t-shirt as pajamas. She keeps a cup of flat Coke by her bed that she sips like an adult with wine. Flaw: She is a snitch and a show-off. She will open the bathroom door just to prove she isn't scared. **Physicality:** Small, wiry, buzzing with energy. Brown hair cut in a straight bowl by Deb with kitchen scissors. Knees are always scraped. Sits with her whole body curled into a chair, knees to chest. Carries a Walkman that only has one working headphone. When she lies she looks dead at you. **Motives / Allegiances / Relationships:** Motives: Wants to be seen as grown, wants Benny to shut up, wants to not be scared. Semi-loyal to Benny in that older sister way where she'll hit him but kill anyone else who does. Doesn't trust her parents to show up. Actually trusts Mabs because Mabs once told her Deb was being a bitch (and then immediately said "don't repeat that"). **Voice:** > "You don't even have a car. That's why you have to babysit us." > (from inside the bathroom, trying not to cry) "Benny, shut up. We're playing Super Spies. Super Spies don't pee." # Benny Barlow **Background / Personality:** 6, first grade. Sweet and completely non-strategic. Still believes if he holds his breath long enough he can disappear. Has a stutter when he's excited that he hates. Wets the bed about twice a week, which is why Deb makes him sleep in pull-ups that he calls his "baby diapers" and hides under the bed. He loves Mabs with full, uncritical love because she doesn't make fun of his dinosaurs and lets him eat dry Lucky Charms out of her hand like she's feeding a horse. He carries one animal everywhere - currently a blue Brachiosaurus with bite marks on its tail. He is not precocious. He is a normal, sticky, loud 6-year-old who asks "why" until you want to scream. Tonight he is overtired because Rich promised to take him to a batting cage and didn't. Flaw: He cannot be quiet when it matters. If you tell him to whisper, he whispers louder. **Physicality:** Small even for 6, big head, hair always damp with sweat. Wears Lightning McQueen pajamas with the feet worn through. Always touching - hand on Mabs' leg, head on her arm. Smells like apple juice and dryer sheets. Runs everywhere even indoors. **Motives:** Stay next to Mabs. Don't get in trouble for peeing. Keep Brach safe. **Voice:** > "Mabs, is that the bad guy? Are you gonna get him like Jurassic Park?" > (muffled into her hoodie) "I didn't mean to." # Ronnie Hall **Background / Personality:** 41. Grew up in Vandalia. Owns "Hall's Total Lawn Care" which is a truck, a mower, and a nephew. In winter it makes no money, so his wife Carla lets him sell her leftover Lortabs from her tanning salon / nail place on Needmore. He is not a kingpin. He sells maybe 20 pills a week to other dads from softball. He thinks this makes him Tony Soprano. Rich Barlow owes him $600 - $200 from a softball league bet Ronnie invented after he lost, $400 from a "loan" that was really pills. Rich told him to fuck off in front of other guys at a cookout last Saturday. Ronnie has been stewing about it for six days because respect is the only thing he has that isn't financed. He didn't plan a burglary. He planned to kick Rich's door, take his big TV to "hold collateral," and scare Deb. He brought kitchen shears from his truck to cut the cable cord out of spite. He is the kind of guy who says "with all due respect" before saying something completely disrespectful. Idiosyncrasy: Wears a gold chain outside his t-shirt, even when mowing. Says "Mmmkay?" at the end of sentences. Chews gum loudly when nervous, which is always. Thinks himself very reasonable. Flaw: Needs an audience. Cannot stop talking. Cannot back down once he's said he'll do something because Carla is the only person who hears him say things at home. **Physicality:** 6'0", gone soft, gut over his belt, farmer tan. Thin mustache he keeps trimming too short. Polo shirt with Hall's Lawn Care logo, jean shorts even in October, white tennis shoes. Middle-aged guy trying to look hard. Moves like his back hurts, which it does. **Motives / Allegiances:** Wants to feel like a big man for one night. Allegiance is to himself and his reputation at the Moose Lodge. Brought Kyle because he wanted someone to drive and to see him be tough. Now stuck because leaving means admitting he fucked up. **Voice:** > "I ain't here for you, sweetie. This is between me and Rich. Rich knows what he done. Mmmkay?" > (holding his side, panicked, trying to bargain) "You don't wanna do this, you don't wanna - we was just fuckin' around, we was just gonna take the TV, that's it, that's it." # Kyle Hall **Background / Personality:** 19, Ronnie's wife's sister's kid. Lives in his mom's basement in Clayton. Graduated high school last year by the skin of his teeth, works at the car wash when he shows up. Smokes weed all day because he's anxious, then gets more anxious because he smoked weed. Looks up to Ronnie because Ronnie is the only adult male who doesn't call him a disappointment outright. He did not want to come. He is high as hell this whole night on a blunt he smoked trying to calm down in Ronnie's truck. When Mabs hits him, it's the first time anyone has hit him hard since middle school and he immediately wants his mom. Trivial anchor: Wears a brand new Starter Hornets jacket that is too big for him that he thinks makes him look like a drug dealer. Keeps checking his pager even though it has no batteries. Calls Ronnie "Unc" even though they're not blood. Flaw: Obedient to the worst person in the room. Freezes. Cries loud when scared - ugly, snotty cry that makes him feel even more embarrassed. **Physicality:** 6'2" but hunches to 5'11". Skinny-fat, acne on his back, carries weight in his face. Big hands he doesn't know what to do with. Moves awkwardly, like he's too aware of his knees. **Motives:** Doesn't want to go to jail. Doesn't want Ronnie to think he's a pussy. Wants to be home playing Mortal Kombat II on his Super Nintendo. **Voice:** > (already bleeding) "Unc, Unc, I wanna go, you said he wasn't gonna be home -" > (pacing with the shears, holding them like they're hot) "Stay back! I'll -- I'll cut the -- man, just stay back, okay? Okay?"

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 1: 7:32 PM** Rich Barlow opened the door like he was surprised to see me, even though Deb booked me on Tuesday. "Hey, Mabel," he said. He always calls me Mabel. My name is on my bus pass, clipped to my hoodie pocket. MABS. It's not hard. "Hey," I said. "Bus was late." It wasn't. I was early. I stood on their porch for ten minutes smoking so I wouldn't have to stand in their living room while they got ready. The house is one of those big new ones up on Ridgeview, the ones that went up in '91 and already look cheap. Vinyl siding that shakes when trucks go by. Big front with a little window that doesn't open. Deb came down the stairs in this red dress that was too tight and a perfume that smelled like bathroom spray. Febreze and cat litter, that's what their house actually smelled like. They had a cat I never saw. Just the box. "Okay, so bedtime is nine for Benny, nine-thirty for Lucy but not really, she can stay up," Deb said. She gives me this same list every time. She always writes it on a sticky note and then just says it all anyway. "No soda. No snacks after seven-thirty, Rich left cookies out but don't let them have them, it's for the thing tomorrow. And the front door, you have to -" "Lift and shove," I said. She blinked. "Right. Rich keeps saying he's going to fix it." Rich was by the mirror in the hall trying to get his tie right. He looked at me in the mirror. "You good with the lock?" "Yeah." "You sure? We had a thing last week, some kids -" "I got it," I said. They do this every time. Like if they say it enough, I'm going to rob them. What would I even take? Their ceramic geese? He handed me two tens. One was damp. "Midnight at the latest," Deb said, putting on earrings. "We might be a little after, gallery thing ran long last time but -" "I gotta catch the last 14," I said. "12:15. North Main." Rich made a face like I was being difficult. "We said midnight." "I know. I'm saying if you're after 12:15 I'm walking." I put the money in my pocket and took out my little notebook. I've got one that I take to every sit. I write down what time they said they'd be home because I've had two families just... not come back till three in the morning and then act like I'm the weird one for being pissed. Deb saw me writing. "What are you writing?" "Your note," I said. "Midnight." Lucy came down then and saved me. She had on that Lisa Frank shirt where the tiger looks like it's on acid, as pajamas. Her hair cut straight across like Deb did it drunk. "Hi," Lucy said to nobody. "This is Mabs, honey," Deb said. "I know who she is," Lucy said. Benny ran down after her. He's six and he's all head and damp hair. His pajamas have cars on them and the feet are worn through so they flap. He came straight to my leg and put his face on my thigh. His hair was sweaty. "Hey, Benny the Jet," I said. "Mabs, you smell like outside," he said. "Thank you." Rich clapped his hands once, like we were a team. "Okay! Be good! Listen to Mabs! Don't let her do anything wild, like -" "Like burn the house down?" Lucy said. Deb laughed too loud. "Okay, bye!" They left. Rich's Lexus backed out too fast, clipped the curb. The house went quiet in that way big houses do. Just the hum of the fridge and the zapper in the neighbor's yard. Big bug zapper they leave on all night, you can hear the pop. Lucy looked at me. "You gonna make us healthy dinner?" "Box mac and cheese," I said. "With cut-up hot dogs?" "No hot dogs. I didn't see hot dogs." She sighed like I'd ruined her life and flopped onto the couch. The couch was white and had a clear plastic thing on the armrests. No one sits on it except for pictures. Benny pulled my hoodie. "Can we have cereal?" "After mac and cheese." I went to the kitchen. The box was on the counter where Deb always leaves it like a test. Store brand. I got the pot, got the water going. I left the laundry room door open so I could see them on the couch, then I went in there and opened the little window and lit a smoke out of it. Capri 120s. I have to cup the smoke and blow it at the screen. My tote was on the dryer. Bus pass. Lighter with the naked lady. A paperback Lucy stole from Deb and gave me last time - Dean Koontz, sheep on the cover, half ripped. And Benny's blue dinosaur. Brachiosaurus. He leaves it at my place every time and then cries till I bring it back, so now I just keep it. I folded their laundry while I waited for water to boil. Anxiety habit. Their laundry is always half-done. Rich's t-shirts, Deb's work slacks. Kids' socks all stuck together with apple juice. If you fold it, Deb thinks you cleaned. "Mabs!" Benny yelled. "Water's sparking!" Shit. I came out. The water was at that furious boil where it spit. I dumped the noodles in and some went on the burner and burned and smoked. I turned the fan on and coughed and Lucy went, "Wow." By the time it was done I'd overdone it. The noodles were mush. The cheese stuff wouldn't dissolve because I used too much milk. Benny ate it anyway, standing up at the counter with his hand in the bowl. "Good?" I said. He nodded with his mouth full and cheese on his chin. Lucy took three bites and pushed it away. "Mom makes it better." "Your mom makes it the same way," I said. "No she doesn't." We watched Jurassic Park on their TV. They have the big one. Benny sat in my lap even though it's too hot for that. He smells like apple juice and pennies. Lucy sat too close and then too far. At the part where the raptors come in the kitchen, Benny buried his face in my hoodie. "Mabs, are you gonna get a real job?" Lucy said, not looking at me. "This is a real job," I said. "I mean after this." "I have a job. I go to school." "For what?" "Nothing. Criminal Justice." She looked at me then. "You failed cop school?" "Something like that." On screen the girl in the movie says "We have to do something." Benny whispered it with her. At 9:01 the power flickered. Rain started hitting the big windows. Ohio rain that comes down all at once. The neighbor's zapper kept buzzing, popping every time water hit it. Benny's eyes were half closed. Lucy had her Walkman going, one headphone busted. I stood up to go check the front door. I always check it. Rich never fixes it. You have to lift up on the handle and shove with your shoulder or it won't deadbolt. Last time I sat here some kid tried the handle at ten p.m. Just checking doors, probably. Rich blamed me for not locking it right. I walked to the entryway. The glass on their front door is that wavy kind so headlights look weird through it. There were headlights. Not the Lexus. Too high up. Old truck. It stopped in the driveway. Left the lights on. I stood there with Benny's cheese bowl in one hand. My notebook said midnight. It was 9:11. Someone came up to the porch and tried the handle. Just a jiggle. Testing. Then a knock. Polite, at first. Then a shoulder hit, hard, right on the spot where you have to lift and shove.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear authorial voice. Let me work through the key elements. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The first-person voice is immediately distinctive and consistent. Mabs reads as a real person rather than a type - her petty observations (the damp ten, the ceramic geese, the notebook she keeps because she's been stiffed before), her small thefts (smoking in the laundry room, the vodka implied but not yet shown), and her exhausted practicality all cohere into a believable character. The detail of her writing down the time in her notebook and Deb noticing it is a small, perfect character beat. The voice is flat and Ohio-specific without being performed - "Anxiety habit. Their laundry is always half-done" lands exactly right. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is notably good. It's functional and character-revealing without being on-the-nose. Rich calling her "Mabel," Deb's sticky-note speech she gives anyway, Lucy's "You gonna make us healthy dinner?" - these feel like real people talking past each other. The exchange about the lock is efficient and establishes the plot-critical detail without telegraphing it. The "failed cop school" exchange is the best moment - it's economical and tells us everything about both characters' dynamic. SETTING: The house is rendered through smell and texture rather than description - cat litter, Febreze, vinyl siding that shakes, the neighbor's bug zapper. This is showing rather than telling done well. The Glade PlugIns from the plan became Febreze, which is fine. The 1994 specificity is present without being a museum exhibit. PACING: The chapter does exactly what a first chapter should - establishes character, setting, stakes, and ends on a hook. The pacing is controlled. The mac and cheese burning, the Jurassic Park viewing, the door check - these feel like a real evening rather than setup mechanics. WEAKNESSES: There are a few minor issues. "His hair was sweaty" is slightly awkward - hair doesn't sweat, he sweats. "Benny's eyes were half closed" is a bit of a tell. The Jurassic Park detail (1993 film) is slightly anachronistic for a story with 1994 details, though it's plausible they'd have it on VHS. The ending is effective but the "polite, at first" line is the one moment where the voice slips slightly into thriller-genre signaling rather than staying in Mabs' flat register. METAPHOR/POETRY: The writing avoids forced poetry almost entirely. "The house went quiet in that way big houses do" is the closest thing to a poetic observation and it earns its place. No purple prose. No strained metaphors. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely - Rich and Deb leaving, the broken lock established, Lucy's rudeness, Benny's clinginess, the mac and cheese burning, the smoking in the laundry room, the bus pass detail, the headlights. The chapter ends exactly where planned. DARKNESS/LIGHTNESS: The lightness (Benny eating with cheese on his chin, Lucy's "wow" at the burned noodles) is genuinely earned and not cute-ified. The darkness at the end arrives naturally. Overall this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter that delivers on its plan without feeling mechanical. The voice is the real achievement - it's consistent, specific, and never tries too hard. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 2: 8:41 PM** The door didn't open on the first hit. Because it never opens right. Another hit. The whole frame shook. The little wavy glass rattled. Benny sat straight up. "Mom?" Lucy didn't move off the couch. She just turned her head. "You said midnight." I put the bowl down. My mouth went dry in that way it does before - you know you're about to have to deal with something and you already want to be somewhere else. "Benny, come here," I said. Rain was sheeting now. I could see the shape through the wavy glass. Two shapes. One big, one bigger but stooped. Truck running in the driveway with its lights on the curtains. The handle jiggled again and then a man's voice, trying to sound friendly. "Rich? Yo, Rich, open up. It's Ronnie!" I didn't know a Ronnie. Deb never said a Ronnie. Benny came and grabbed the back of my hoodie. His hand was sticky. Lucy finally stood up. "Mabs, who is that?" she said. "I don't know. Stay back." I went to the entryway. My knee clicked. I had four plates in the sink, a pot with cheese crusted to it, one good knife I couldn't find earlier. Inventory. That's what I do when I'm scared. Another shoulder hit. This time the top lock slipped. The deadbolt held because I'd lifted it. "Rich! Come on, man, it's raining out here!" Lucy was behind me now. I could feel her. "Go upstairs," I said, low. "Both of you." "Why?" Lucy said. "Because I said so. Go to your bathroom and lock it. Right now." Benny started breathing fast. That snuffly thing he does before he cries. The guy kicked the door at the bottom. "Rich knows I'm here!" I yelled through the door before I could think better. "Rich isn't here! Nobody's here supposed to be here but us! You need to leave!" Quiet for a second. Rain on the siding. Then a laugh. "Who's that? That Deb? Nah, that ain't Deb." The deadbolt finally gave. Not because he was strong. Because it's a shit deadbolt and the wood is soft. The door swung in six inches and stopped on the chain. The chain that Rich bragged he put in himself. A face in the gap. Mustache too short, gold chain, polo shirt soaked through. Hall's Total Lawn Care on the pocket. Behind him a kid, really a kid, in a Charlotte Hornets jacket three sizes too big, holding kitchen shears. Actual shears. Silver. The mustache guy saw me and did this thing where his whole face changes. Oh, you're not who I wanted. "Well, hey now," he said. "Where's Rich at, sweetie?" Don't call me sweetie. Linda the PO tells me I have to let stuff like that go. I was trying. "He's not here," I said. "You need to go." "Yeah, I can see he ain't here, sweetie. That's the problem. He owes me -" "I don't care what he owes you," I said. I tried to do the voice. The MP voice. Feet shoulder width, chin down, flat. "You need to step outside now. You are not supposed to be in this house." Behind me, Lucy said, loud, "He owes drug money. My dad sells drugs." Ronnie laughed, delighted. "See? Smart girl." He pushed on the door and the chain bent. That cheap brass chain. I had seen it. I had told Deb last month that thing won't hold a cat. "Ronnie, man," the kid whispered. "Maybe we should -" "Hold on, Kyle. I'm talking." My hand was on the door, pushing back. He was pushing in. Benny had his face buried in my leg and he was making that high whine. Ronnie looked down at Benny and made a face. "Aw, kid. It's fine. Just business." He said to me, "Rich told me to fuck off, mmmkay? In front of my wife? So I'm just gonna take his TV and we're square." Lucy from the stairs: "You're not taking our TV." Ronnie looked past me at her and his voice went hard so fast I felt sick. "You shut your mouth." That's when my stomach stopped dropping and my ears started ringing. That's what it feels like. Not brave. Just like someone turned the volume way up on a bad stereo. I said, "You don't talk to them." "Or what?" he said, smiling now. Kyle was still holding the shears like he didn't know why he had them. "Unc -" I let go of the door. The chain popped. The door came open and Ronnie stumbled in a step, off balance. He was right there, close enough I could smell the gum and the rain on him. He put his hand on my upper arm to move me out of the way. Squeezed. I didn't think. I just grabbed the handle of the cast iron skillet where I'd left it on the stove with the cheese still in it, ten inches away on the counter, and brought the bottom up. I didn't aim. I just swung. It hit Kyle first. No, - wait. It hit Ronnie? My memory did a jump. No. I hit Kyle. Because he was the one holding my arm. The kid. He'd grabbed my arm. The shears were cold. The bottom of that pan hit him flat across the face. The sound was - it was like dropping a bag of wet clothes. But louder. And then not loud at all. He went down so fast I thought he died right there. The shears clattered. Blood came out his nose instantly, thick and dark, and he made this noise that wasn't crying yet, just air coming out wrong. "OH! OH MY GOD!" Ronnie yelled. It was way too loud. Benny screamed. Actually screamed. Lucy screamed too but then stopped herself. Kyle was on the floor holding his face. Blood coming through his fingers and onto the white entryway tile. So much. Way more than that should be. His Hornets jacket had blood on it already. I was standing there holding that hot greasy pan with cheese hanging off the bottom, shaking so bad the pan rattled against the stove when I put it down. "I - you grabbed me, you grabbed me first," I said. My voice came out tiny. Ronnie looked at his nephew on the floor and then at me and his face went purple. "You stupid bitch!" he screamed, and he came at me not like a fighter, like a dad who found you smoking. Hands up to grab my head. I got low. Army shit. You get low when someone bigger comes. I drove into his gut and we both went into the hall table. The little ceramic geese Deb loves went everywhere and broke. He hit me in the side of the head with an open hand, hard, and I saw spots. He smelled like beer and lawn fertilizer. "Mabs!" Lucy yelled from halfway up the stairs. She had Benny by the wrist. Benny was crying full-on now, that stutter-cry. "Bedroom! Now!" I shouted. My mouth tasted like metal. Ronnie had me by the hoodie. He was trying to yank me off. Kyle was still on the floor making that air noise, rolling onto his side. Ronnie let go of my hoodie and shoved me back hard. I hit the wall. He spat on the floor. "Look what you did! Look what you did to my nephew! You stupid - we were just gonna take the TV!" He looked down at Kyle bleeding on his white tile and I could see it cross his face. The math. He can't leave now. He came to scare a guy about six hundred bucks and now there's blood on the floor and the babysitter saw his truck and knows his name is Ronnie. He kicked the front door shut. It stuck halfway because the chain was busted and hanging. He looked at me, breathing hard through his mustache. "Okay," he said, more to himself. "Okay. Okay, we gotta - you gotta sit down now, okay? You gotta sit your ass down." Outside, the rain kept hitting the windows.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's core ambitions: a messy, unglamorous home invasion that feels like it happened to real, tired people rather than action movie archetypes. Let me work through the key elements. VOICE AND STYLE: The first-person present-tense-feeling past tense narration is tight and controlled. Short declarative sentences do real work: "I didn't know a Ronnie. Deb never said a Ronnie." This is exactly the kind of flat, inventory-brain thinking the plan called for. The voice is consistent throughout - Mabs sounds like someone who has learned to process threat through cataloguing rather than feeling. The parenthetical self-correction ("It hit Kyle first. No, - wait. It hit Ronnie? My memory did a jump. No.") is a genuinely smart technique for conveying the chaos of violence without choreographing it cleanly. That moment earns its place. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Mabs is exactly as described - the stomach-drop cowardice for the first ten seconds, the MP voice attempt, the inventory habit, the hatred of being touched on the upper arms triggering the violence. The plan specified "she hates being touched on her upper arms" and the chapter delivers this as the precise trigger. Ronnie's "mmmkay" is present, his need for an audience, his inability to back down. Kyle's reluctance ("Unc, maybe we should -") and his immediate collapse into helplessness when hit are consistent with the profile. Lucy's mouthy intervention from the stairs is perfectly in character - the plan noted she'd mouth off and Ronnie would tell her to shut up, which is exactly what happens. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely excellent. Ronnie's "Rich knows what he done" energy comes through without being cartoonish. "You shut your mouth" landing hard enough to change Mabs' internal state is well-handled. The line "He owes drug money. My dad sells drugs" from Lucy is a nice piece of character work - she's showing off, being a snitch, and also being genuinely perceptive. The weakest dialogue moment is Ronnie's "You stupid bitch!" which is functional but the most generic line in the chapter. His post-violence "Okay, okay, okay, we gotta - you gotta sit down now, okay?" is much better - it captures his panicked improvisation. VIOLENCE: The pan strike is handled well. The plan called for "too much blood, too much grease" and the chapter delivers: "blood came out his nose instantly, thick and dark," "cheese hanging off the bottom." The confusion about who got hit first is a smart choice. The aftermath - Mabs' voice coming out tiny, the pan rattling when she puts it down - avoids the trap of making her cool. She's shaking. She's scared. She says "you grabbed me first" like she's already building a defense. PACING: The chapter moves well. The slow build through the door-knocking, the chain giving, the face in the gap, all feel earned. The moment where Mabs "let go of the door" is a clean pivot point. MINOR WEAKNESSES: "My stomach stopped dropping and my ears started ringing. That's what it feels like. Not brave. Just like someone turned the volume way up on a bad stereo" - this is the chapter's most self-conscious moment. The simile is trying a bit hard and the explanatory "That's what it feels like" is slightly over-written. The line "like a dad who found you smoking" for Ronnie's attack is a good specific image. The ceramic geese breaking is a nice detail. The ending - Ronnie's "Okay, okay, okay" math moment - is strong and sets up the next chapter well. FOLLOWED PLAN: The chapter hits all its marks: the MP voice almost working on Kyle, Ronnie calling her sweetie, Lucy mouthing off from the stairs, Ronnie telling her to shut up, Mabs shoving Benny behind her, the hard whisper to Lucy, Lucy listening because Mabs' face changes, Kyle grabbing Mabs' arm, the skillet, too much blood, too much grease, chaos, both men now stuck. The plan is followed faithfully without feeling mechanical. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled pulp fiction writing that earns its darkness through specificity rather than spectacle. The weaknesses are minor - one slightly over-explained emotional beat, one generic expletive. The strengths are substantial: consistent voice, earned violence, characters who behave like themselves under pressure, and a refusal to make any of it cool. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 3: 9:06 PM** Ronnie was kneeling by Kyle. I was on my ass against the wall by the busted geese. My head rang where he hit me. There was ceramic under my hand. One goose head, looking at me. Kyle wasn't dead. He was on his side holding his face making this wet keening noise that wouldn't stop. Blood was coming out between his fingers and going into the grout lines. So much blood from a nose. I thought noses weren't supposed to do that. "You're okay, you're okay, bud," Ronnie was saying, loud, like trying to convince someone in the room who wasn't there. He had his big hand on Kyle's shoulder and Kyle flinched away. "She just - she hit you. It's a nosebleed. You get nosebleeds." He looked up at me. Sweat had made his mustache go dark. "You see what you did?" he said. "We was just gonna talk!" Benny was screaming upstairs. Full lungs. That scared stutter-cry that means he can't breathe right. Lucy yelled, "Benny, shut UP!" That got me up. I got up off the floor using the wall. My knee did the click thing, loud. My cheek stung. Kyle hadn't scratched me with keys, it was the shears when he grabbed - he'd had them up by his hand and the point dragged me when I swung. Thin hot line from my cheekbone to my jaw. When I touched it my fingers came away red but not a lot. Not like Kyle's lot. Just enough to be scary. Ronnie stood up too. We looked at each other across three feet of blood and broken ceramic. "Don't," I said. My voice came out hoarse. "Don't go up there. Those are kids." "I didn't come here for kids!" he shouted. His face was all red. He had spit on his lip. "Where's Rich? Huh? Where's he at that he leaves his kids with - with you?" He took a step to the stairs. I stepped the other way, toward the kitchen. Away from them. I know that looks like cowardice. It was. The kitchen light was still on, sick yellow. Mac and cheese pot on the stove with cheese skin. My tote on the dryer where I'd left it. Phone on the wall, that gross yellow one with the long cord Deb never wipes. Ronnie followed me a step. "Hey! I'm talking to you!" Then Benny screamed louder and Ronnie turned his head to the sound upstairs. Kyle on the floor said something that was just vowels. I slipped into the pantry. It's not a big pantry. It's those skinny ones rich people put in to say they have a pantry. Wire shelves, cereal boxes, three kinds of ranch dressing, a stack of cat food for a cat that doesn't exist. Door that doesn't latch unless you push hard. I pulled it shut and held my hand over my mouth so he wouldn't hear me breathing. Dark. Smelled like potatoes going bad. Okay. Okay. Okay. Four ranch dressings. Two boxes of Apple Jacks. Cat food. Bleach. A mop. I did inventory because that's what you do when your brain is trying to run out of the room. Ronnie was yelling now out there. "Kyle, get up! Get UP, you look like a bitch!" Kyle crying, "My nose! My nose!" Benny still going upstairs. Lucy trying to shush him and failing. I was shaking so bad my teeth clicked. I put my knuckles in my mouth and bit down to stop it. That dumb MP training thing? There is no training for this. When I was at Campbell I sat CQ at a desk from midnight to 6am and watched infomercials. Once a private came in so drunk he peed in my trash can while I was sitting right there and I let him because I didn't want to do the paperwork. That's it. That's my whole military career. I had to get to the phone. Or the garage. The garage has a side door that goes outside. Rich's side door sticks but you can kick it. I pushed the pantry door a crack. Ronnie was in the entryway still, bent over Kyle. His back to me. Big white ass crack showing over his jean shorts. The front door was half-open still, rain blowing in onto the mat. His truck out there idling, lights on. Wasting gas. He'd cut the phone cord. I could see it from here. The yellow wall phone cord hanging down with the wires poking out, fresh cut. There were silver shears on the counter by the sink. He did it when I was in the pantry. Quick, like he'd thought to do it. "Mmmkay, mmmkay, we gotta think," he was saying to himself. He wiped his face. Gum chewing fast. I slipped out of the pantry low, stayed below the counter line. My hoodie sleeve dragged through something sticky. Garage door is off the kitchen, by the fridge. I turned that knob slow. Locked. Rich locks it from the other side because Deb is scared of raccoons. Okay. Okay. Back the other way - sliding door to the backyard, by the table. I could make it. Then to Mrs. Kline's. Kline is 80 and deaf but she has a phone. I was two steps from that slider when Kyle saw me. He was sitting up against the wall, blood all down the front of his Hornets jacket, holding toilet paper from somewhere to his nose that was already soaked red. Our eyes met. He didn't yell for Ronnie at first. He just stared at me like I was a bear. Then Ronnie turned and saw me too. He came fast for a big guy. Not fighting fast. Dad-angry fast. I grabbed the first thing on the counter and threw it. It was the box of Apple Jacks. It hit him in the chest and exploded and cereal went everywhere. He slipped on it - actually slipped on Apple Jacks - and went down hard on one knee and yelled "FUCK!" so loud Benny upstairs went quiet for half a second and then screamed even louder. I ran. Not to the slider. To the stairs. Because Benny went quiet and that was worse. Ronnie was up before I got there. He grabbed a handful of my hoodie and yanked me back. The claw clip in my hair broke and my hair fell down. He had my hoodie twisted in his fist. "You're not going up there!" he breathed in my face. Beer and gum. "You sit!" I kneed him. I tried. It hit his thigh and did nothing because I'm five-five and he is not. He shoved me and I went back into the kitchen island and hit my hip on the corner, hard enough my leg went dumb for a second. Kyle was crying again, louder. "Unc, there's cereal everywhere!" Ronnie looked at the mess - cereal across the tiles, blood, broken geese, mac and cheese - and for a second he just looked tired. Like a guy who came over to yell about six hundred bucks and now he's standing in a stranger's kitchen covered in a kid's blood. He let go of my hoodie. "Go sit in there," he said, pointing at the washroom. The little room where the washer dryer is. Where I smoke. "Both of you sit. We gotta figure this out." He thought he was still in charge. He thought this was a situation you figure out. Upstairs, Lucy slammed the bathroom door and yelled, "WE CALLED THE COPS! WE CALLED THEM ALREADY!" We both looked up at the ceiling. The bathroom vent rattles when you talk in it. You can hear everything. Ronnie's face went white under his tan. "You didn't call nobody," he said up to the ceiling, but quieter. Benny wailed, "Mabs!" I was on the floor by the island, hand on my hip, hair in my eyes, bleeding from my cheek onto my own hoodie, with Apple Jacks stuck to my socks. The wall phone cord swung a little from the wind coming in the front door.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's core promise: exhaustion as texture, incompetence on all sides, and specific grossness over coolness. Let me work through the metrics carefully. NUANCED CHARACTERS: Mabs is rendered with real specificity. Her cowardice in the first ten seconds ("I know that looks like cowardice. It was.") is exactly the self-aware, non-heroic voice the plan called for. The inventory habit under stress ("Four ranch dressings. Two boxes of Apple Jacks. Cat food. Bleach. A mop.") is character-as-behavior rather than character-as-description. Ronnie's moment of looking tired at the mess — "a guy who came over to yell about six hundred bucks and now he's standing in a stranger's kitchen covered in a kid's blood" — is genuinely good characterization, showing the gap between his self-image and reality. Kyle's bear-stare before yelling is well-observed. The characters feel like people rather than types. EMOTIONALLY ENGAGING: The chapter earns its tension through accumulation of small, specific details rather than manufactured stakes. Benny going quiet and that being worse than screaming is a genuinely effective beat. The Apple Jacks slip is both funny and chaotic in the right way. The ending image — Mabs on the floor with cereal on her socks, the phone cord swinging — lands with the right kind of sour exhaustion. COMPELLING PLOT: The chapter follows the plan closely: pantry hiding, phone cord already cut, garage blocked, failed escape attempt, Kyle spotting her, Apple Jacks chaos, Ronnie grabbing her, Lucy's fake cop call. The plot moves efficiently without feeling mechanical. The Apple Jacks as both weapon and pratfall is a nice piece of situational comedy that doesn't undercut the tension. COHERENT: The spatial logic of the house is maintained. The chapter tracks who is where, what Mabs can and can't see, and why each escape route fails. The timeline is clear. No continuity errors noticed. WEAK DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely excellent. Ronnie's "You see what you did? We was just gonna talk!" is perfect — aggrieved, self-justifying, absurd. "Kyle, there's cereal everywhere!" from Kyle is a genuinely funny line that also characterizes his arrested development. Lucy's "WE CALLED THE COPS! WE CALLED THEM ALREADY!" through the vent is well-placed. The dialogue doesn't overexplain and stays in character. Minor quibble: "You're not going up there! You sit!" from Ronnie is slightly generic compared to his other lines, but it's not a real problem. TELL-DON'T-SHOW: Almost entirely show. The one moment that edges toward tell is "He thought he was still in charge. He thought this was a situation you figure out." — this is Mabs' internal narration interpreting Ronnie, which is borderline, but it's in voice and earns its place as character observation rather than authorial intrusion. UNSURPRISING OR UNCREATIVE: The Apple Jacks as improvised weapon/pratfall is genuinely unexpected and earns its place. The phone cord already cut (Ronnie did it while she was in the pantry) is a nice reversal of expectation — she didn't see it happen, we piece it together with her. The "bear" simile for Kyle's stare is fresh. The CQ duty memory (private peeing in the trash can) is exactly the kind of anti-heroic military backstory the plan called for and it's funny and deflating in the right way. AMATEURISH: Very low amateurism. The prose is controlled, the pacing is deliberate, the voice is consistent. No clunky exposition, no over-explained emotions. The "okay okay okay" repetition is used sparingly and effectively. PURPLE PROSE: None. The prose is deliberately flat and specific, which is the right register for this story and this narrator. FORCED POETRY OR METAPHOR: Minimal. "Dad-angry fast" is a good compressed simile. "Beer and gum" as sensory shorthand for Ronnie's breath is efficient. The goose head looking at her is a nice recurring image. Nothing feels strained or decorative. WELL-EARNED LIGHTNESS OR DARKNESS: The Apple Jacks moment is the chapter's tonal risk — comedy in the middle of violence — and it works because it's grounded in the specific chaos of the scene rather than being a wink at the audience. The darkness of Benny going quiet is earned by the preceding noise. The ending is appropriately sour without being melodramatic. CHARACTERS CONSISTENT WITH PROFILE: Mabs' inventory habit, her cowardice in the first ten seconds, her knee clicking, her hair falling when the claw clip breaks — all consistent with the profile. Ronnie's "Mmmkay" appears, his need to be in charge, his back hurting (implied by how he moves). Kyle's crying, his obedience, his Hornets jacket getting ruined. Lucy's defiance through the vent. Benny's screaming. All consistent. FOLLOWED CHAPTER PLAN: The chapter hits every planned beat: kids not safe (Lucy and Benny in bathroom, audible), Mabs bleeding from cheek (shears, not keys — minor deviation from plan which said keys, but shears is actually more consistent since Ronnie had the shears), phone cord cut, garage blocked, pantry hiding, the 60-second breathing moment, the CQ memory instead of MP glory. The plan said "tries to leave through the garage, door is blocked by Rich's shit" — here it's locked from the other side, which is a slight deviation but works. The chapter ends with Mabs on the floor rather than at the bottom of the stairs listening to Benny, which is a minor deviation but the emotional beat is the same. FAITHFUL TO WRITING PROMPT: The chapter serves the larger story's premise well — this is the wrong babysitter in the sense of someone too stubborn and cornered to quit, not a trained killer. The chapter advances that premise effectively. Overall this is a very strong chapter. The voice is consistent, the craft is controlled, the characters are specific, and the tonal balance between grim and absurd is well-managed. The only real weaknesses are minor: one slightly generic line of Ronnie dialogue, one borderline tell moment, and a small deviation from the plan's detail about keys vs. shears (which is actually an improvement). This is professional-quality pulp fiction writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 4: 9:38 PM** Ronnie didn't hit me again. He just stood over me breathing hard and looking at the kitchen like the kitchen had done it. He said, "Fuckin' cereal," and kicked some Apple Jacks out of the way. They were already getting soggy in the blood. Upstairs the bathroom door was still shut. No screaming now. That was worse than screaming. Lucy does quiet when she's actually scared. Benny feeds off her. "Mabs?" Benny's little voice came through the vent. "Mabs, the toilet's -" "Shh," Lucy hissed, muffled. "Shut up about the toilet." Kyle was still on the floor in the entryway with toilet paper mashed to his face. His Hornets jacket was ruined. Blood had soaked down to the white stripes. He was trying to get up using the wall and his legs kept sliding in cereal. "Unc, I wanna go home," he said. His voice was all stuffed up. "My nose - I think it's broke." "No shit it's broke," Ronnie snapped. "You see what she did?" I was still on the floor by the island. My hip had gone from numb to this deep hot throb. If I pressed on it I wanted to puke. My cheek was still dripping. I put my hoodie sleeve up to it and it came away pink. Ronnie looked down at me. "You stay down there. You understand me? You stay." He said it like he was talking to a dog that kept getting on the couch. He turned toward the entryway to help Kyle. That was his mistake. When he turned I could see his whole back. His jean shorts. His polo riding up. The handle of the shears sticking out of his back pocket where he put them after cutting the phone line. No. Not the shears. The drawer. I army-crawled, which is really just dragging yourself like an idiot, to the silverware drawer by the stove. Deb has one of those wooden blocks with knives in it but they're all from a wedding set fifteen years ago and never got sharpened. I know because I tried to cut a tomato in this house once and ended up smashing it. I opened the drawer with my left hand. My right hand was still holding my face. Inside: spatulas, a can opener, another can opener, kid scissors, one big chef's knife. The big one. I grabbed it. It was lighter than it should be. The edge looked shiny but when you ran your thumb - I didn't run my thumb - you could tell. It wouldn't cut paper. "You hear me?" Ronnie said, not looking. He was trying to pull Kyle up. Kyle kept moaning and holding his nose with both hands now, paper gone. I got to my feet. My knee click was loud in my own ears. My sock caught on soggy cereal and I almost went down again. Inventory. I thought it without meaning to. Knife, one. Hand, bleeding. Hip, fucked. Kids upstairs, not safe. I said, loud enough that my own voice scared me, "You need to leave my house." Ronnie turned around fast. "Your house? Your -" He saw the knife and stopped talking. He put his hands up a little, not high, like whoa. Like calm down. Then he saw my hand was shaking so bad the knife tip was wobbling and his face did that thing again where it goes from scared to mean in half a second. "Oh, okay," he said. "Okay, you got a knife. Real big girl." He came at me. Not fast. He thought I wouldn't do it. I did it. I came in low like you're supposed to when a drunk private squares up, and I put that knife right in his side. Just above the belt, under the ribs where his polo shirt was already riding up. It was supposed to go in. That's what knives do in movies. You push and they go. This one didn't. The shirt bunched. The skin gave a little and then the knife skated sideways, sliced his side open maybe half an inch deep and maybe four inches long, not deep at all, just enough to open him. Like filleting. There was blood instantly but not a lot, just a bright line that started running down into his shorts. He made a noise I have never heard a person make. Not a scream. A yelp, like a dog who got its foot stepped on, but held. "AH! AH! You cut me! You cut me!" He fell back against the laundry room doors and slid down. Hit his ass hard on the tile and his legs went out. His hand went to his side and came away red and he stared at it like it was someone else's. "Oh, fuck! Fuck, I'm cut, I'm cut!" I was standing over him with that stupid dull knife. My hand was wet. I looked down - I'd cut my own palm when it slid. Not deep, but enough to be slick and stinging like hell. Blood on top of blood and I couldn't tell whose. "You cut me!" he kept yelling. He wasn't mad now. He was panicked, high pitched. "You crazy bitch you cut me!" Kyle started crying for real at that. Loud, ugly, snotty crying that you feel embarrassed for hearing. "Unc! Unc, you're bleeding!" "I'm bleeding! She's -" The lights flickered. Everybody looked up. That storm out there had been popping the zapper next door for an hour. The kitchen fluorescents buzzed, went out, came back, went out again. Then stayed out. Rain hammering. No fridge hum. Just breathing and crying and the rain on the windows. Dark, but not full dark. Streetlight coming through the wavy glass in the entry, orange and wet. The front door was still half open and rain was blowing in, making the cereal paste on the floor shine. Ronnie was trying to get up using the wall, hand still on his side. Blood was coming between his fingers but not fast. Just that slow jewelry-store spreading. I backed away to the stairs. I had to get between them and upstairs. I didn't have a plan past that. I put my foot on the first step and my hip went out. Just gave. That bone-on-tile hit earlier. I had to grab the banister with my cut hand and it slipped. I caught myself on the third step on my knees. Kyle saw me go down and he did something weird. He didn't come at me. He went to Ronnie and tried to pull him up. "Come on, Unc, come on, we gotta go, we gotta go," he kept saying, sobbing while he said it. Ronnie shoved him off weakly. "Don't - fuck - touch -" I crawled up two more steps. My palm left a red print on the white painted riser. I could hear upstairs now, clear. The vent in that bathroom, it goes right down to the kitchen. Lucy whispering, fast, "It's okay, it's okay, Mabs is gonna -" Benny whimpering, "She's bleeding, I saw, she's bleeding." He'd seen through the crack when I got hit. That's why he went quiet. Ronnie down there on the floor said, "We ain't done. You hear me? We ain't -" Then the power made a big pop outside, that transformer sound, and everything went truly black. The streetlight out front went out too. Black house. Rain like fists on the roof. I lay on the stairs with my face against the cool paint, that dull knife in my hand that wouldn't cut, my own blood making my hoodie stick to my hip, listening to Benny cry through the vent and Ronnie breathing heavy down in the kitchen and Kyle somewhere in between trying not to throw up from looking at his own nose. My hand wouldn't close right anymore.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's core aesthetic: exhaustion, incompetence, and specific grossness over coolness. Let me work through the metrics carefully. **Nuanced Characters:** Mabs is rendered with real texture here. Her inventory-narration reflex ("Knife, one. Hand, bleeding. Hip, fucked. Kids upstairs, not safe.") is consistent with her profile and feels earned rather than imposed. Her cowardice-then-action cycle is believable. Ronnie's shift from menacing to panicked after being cut is psychologically true - the bully who can't handle consequences. Kyle's ugly crying and his instinct to go to Ronnie rather than attack Mabs is a small, smart character choice. Lucy's whispered reassurance through the vent ("Mabs is gonna -") and Benny's "She's bleeding, I saw, she's bleeding" are efficient and emotionally precise. These are not cardboard figures. **Emotionally Engaging:** The chapter earns its tension through accumulation of small physical details rather than manufactured stakes. The soggy Apple Jacks in blood, the knife skating sideways instead of penetrating cleanly, the palm cut from her own weapon - these create a visceral, uncomfortable engagement. The moment Mabs hears Lucy trying to reassure Benny through the vent is genuinely affecting without being sentimental. **Compelling Plot:** The chapter delivers on its plan: the fight, the dull knife failing, the power going out, Mabs losing physical ground. The structure is tight. The knife not working as expected is the chapter's best plot beat - it subverts the expected violence while still delivering consequence. The ending with Mabs on the stairs, hand not closing right, is a strong cliffhanger that doesn't feel manufactured. **Coherent:** The spatial logic is clear throughout. The reader always knows where everyone is. The timeline is compressed and credible. No continuity errors. **Weak Dialogue:** The dialogue is largely excellent. "Fuckin' cereal" as Ronnie's first line is perfect - petty, displaced, character-revealing. "Your house? Your -" cut off by him seeing the knife is economical. "Real big girl" is exactly right for Ronnie. Kyle's "Unc, I wanna go home" is pathetic in the best way. The only slight weakness is Ronnie's repeated "You cut me!" which runs a beat too long, though it's defensible as realistic panic. Overall the dialogue is one of the chapter's strengths. **Tell-Don't-Show:** Almost entirely show. The hip pain is conveyed through action ("my hip went out. Just gave"). The knife's dullness is shown through the failed penetration. The darkness is shown through what Mabs can and can't hear. No expository intrusion. **Unsurprising or Uncreative:** The knife-that-doesn't-work is a genuinely creative subversion of the expected pulp beat. The palm cut from her own sliding weapon is a nice ironic detail. The power going out is a familiar trope but handled without fanfare. The chapter avoids most genre clichés. **Amateurish:** Very low amateurism. The prose is controlled. The pacing is deliberate. There are no rookie errors like over-explaining emotional states or undercutting tension with unnecessary humor. **Purple Prose:** Essentially none. The prose is spare and functional. "Like filleting" is the most stylized simile and it earns its place. **Forced Poetry or Metaphor:** "Blood on top of blood" is clean. "Rain like fists on the roof" is the one moment that edges toward cliché but doesn't quite tip over. "That slow jewelry-store spreading" for blood is the chapter's most distinctive metaphor and it lands - specific, unexpected, not overwrought. The "yelp, like a dog who got its foot stepped on, but held" is precise and original. The metaphors are sparse and mostly land. **Well-earned Lightness or Darkness:** The darkness is entirely earned. Nothing is gratuitous. The violence is ugly and consequence-laden. The moment of near-black comedy (Ronnie saying "Fuckin' cereal" while standing over blood and Apple Jacks) is perfectly calibrated - it's funny and horrible simultaneously, which is exactly the register the plan called for. **Characters Consistent with Profile:** Mabs' inventory reflex, her army-crawl, her cowardice-then-action, her self-recrimination ("which is really just dragging yourself like an idiot") - all consistent. Ronnie's bully-to-panic arc is consistent with his profile as someone who needs an audience and can't handle real consequences. Kyle's obedience and ugly crying are exactly as described. Lucy's quiet-when-scared and Benny's inability to be quiet are both present and consistent. **Followed Chapter Plan:** The plan called for: Mabs stalking with the dull knife, the knife sliding, Ronnie getting cut in the hallway (here it's the kitchen/laundry room area, a minor deviation), Ronnie falling into the coat closet (here he slides down the laundry room doors - slight deviation), Mabs hurting her hand (here it's a palm cut from the sliding knife rather than punching, which is actually more interesting), power going out, Mabs crawling to the stairs to hear Benny. The deviations are improvements or neutral changes, not failures. The plan is substantially followed. **Faithful to Writing Prompt:** The prompt is "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" and the plan's interpretation - not a badass, just someone too stubborn and cornered to quit - is fully realized here. The chapter is faithful to the pulp revenge tale genre while subverting its conventions intelligently. **Minor criticisms:** "Rain like fists on the roof" is the one moment of slightly lazy metaphor. Ronnie's repeated "You cut me!" could be trimmed by one iteration. The chapter is otherwise remarkably clean. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 5: 10:14 PM** I stayed on the stairs. For how long I don't know. The dark does that. Makes time stop working. Rain hammered the roof so hard I could feel it through the banister. No light anywhere. The streetlight that always shined through their wavy glass was dead. The zapper next door was dead. The whole block was out. Any other night they'd be freaking out about their freezer thawing. Rich brags about the steaks in there. Down at the bottom of the stairs I could hear Ronnie breathing. Open mouthed, wet. Every couple breaths he'd let out a little "hah" like it hurt going out. Kyle was closer. He'd sat down against the wall halfway down the hall. I could hear his Hornets jacket swishing every time he moved. And sniffing. Trying not to cry loud. My hip was on fire now. That corner of the island got me right on the bone. I had to sit on the second step with my legs out in front of me because if I bent it the pain shot up to my teeth. My hand wouldn't close. The cut across my palm from the knife was not deep but it was right on the fat pad under my thumb, where everything moves. Blood kept coming, slick. I held it with my other hand and it kept slipping. Benny had stopped crying. That was worse. Now it was just the vent breathing. I put my ear against the paint of the banister. I could hear faint through the floor vent that goes from downstairs up. Lucy was whispering nonstop, that fast scared whisper. " - and then Mabs is gonna get help and Mrs. Kline is gonna call and Dad's gonna be so mad, okay? So you have to be quiet, okay? You have to be quiet like hide and seek." Benny whispered back but I couldn't hear words. Just a little hitching breath. Ronnie heard it too. He laughed, but it came out as a wheeze. "They're still - they're still up there. You hear that? Your kids. Mmmkay?" He was talking to me but also to no one. "Kyle," he said. "Go get 'em." Kyle didn't move. Swish of jacket. "Kyle!" Kyle's voice, stuffed up because his nose was still bleeding into his throat. "Unc, I don't - I don't wanna go up there." "It's two kids! Go get - bring 'em down here so she -" "I don't wanna!" Ronnie tried to get up. I heard his palm slap wet tile. Slipped. He said "Ah! FUCK!" when his side pulled. "She cut me, Kyle. You see what she done? I'm cut!" He was bleeding. Not dying bleeding. The kind where you see a lot on your shirt and you think you're dying but you're not. I'd seen it at Campbell when a guy put his hand through a window drunk. The blood gets everywhere and looks big. "I called Carla," Kyle said suddenly. Quiet. Ronnie stopped moving. "What?" "I called Carla when you was - when the lights went. On your phone. I called the house." "You - what?" Ronnie's voice went up. "You called CARLA?" "She didn't answer. I left - I left a message. I said you was hurt." "Jesus CHRIST, Kyle!" That yell cost him. He made that hah noise again. Kyle started that ugly crying again. Not loud now, just snotty and ashamed. I should have moved while they were yelling. That's what a real MP would do, right? Get to an exit, get a phone, get help. But my hip wouldn't let me stand and my hand was full of blood and more than that - if I go downstairs I'm away from the kids. If I go down I'm past them. If I go up I'm trapped with Benny. So I stayed on the second step with my back against the wall and did inventory because my brain was trying to leave again. Two kids upstairs. Shitty door that pushes open if you lean. One dull knife I dropped somewhere on the stairs. Where? I didn't have it now. My hands were empty. Front door half open, rain coming in. Back slider maybe? Garage side door stuck. Phone dead, cord cut. No bus till morning. My tote in the laundry room with my smokes. I was laughing. Just once, that wheezy gasp I do. Ronnie heard it. "You think this is funny?" he yelled up the stairs, into the black where he thought I was. "Huh? You think this is funny, sweetie? You cut me! In this bitch's house!" "Unc, don't call her -" "Shut UP!" There was a crash down there. He'd tried to get up again and knocked something. Sounded like the hall table going all the way over this time. "Okay. Okay, mmmkay. We're gonna - we're gonna sit. Everybody's gonna sit for a minute, mmmkay? We're gonna figure this out." He said figure out again. Like this was a math test. "We were just gonna take the TV," he said, louder now, like he was explaining it to the judge already. "That's it! Rich said come get it! He said -" He didn't finish because upstairs Benny started crying again. Real loud this time. Not whimpering. Full on deep little-kid sobs where he can't get air. Lucy yelled, actual yelled this time, not whispering. "LEAVE US ALONE! WE TOLD YOU WE CALLED THE COPS!" Ronnie was quiet for a long second. Then I heard him dragging himself. Hand on wall, leaving a trail maybe. Moving toward the stairs. Toward me without knowing I was right here in the dark on the second step. I could smell him now. That lawn fertilizer smell mixed with blood and rain. "Kyle, come on. We're gonna - we're gonna go up. We're gonna talk to the little girls up there." "Lucy's not a -" I said without thinking. Then stopped. But he heard me. Right under me. He reached out in the dark and his hand found my ankle. It was warm and wet. His fingers closed. I didn't scream. I couldn't. Benny screaming did it for me.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a masterclass in sustained tension through restraint. The model has executed its plan with remarkable fidelity and craft, delivering exactly what it promised: exhaustion as texture, incompetence on all sides, and specific grossness over coolness. The opening is immediately effective. "The dark does that. Makes time stop working." This is not purple prose - it's a flat, accurate observation that fits Mabs' voice perfectly. The model avoids the trap of over-poeticizing darkness and instead uses it functionally, as a sensory and tactical problem. The inventory technique established in the character profiles is deployed here with precision: "Two kids upstairs. Shitty door that pushes open if you lean. One dull knife I dropped somewhere on the stairs. Where? I didn't have it now." This is showing character through behavior rather than telling us she's scared. The inventory is also narratively useful - it reminds the reader of the physical geography and the stakes simultaneously. Dialogue is a particular strength. Ronnie's voice is pitch-perfect: "She cut me, Kyle. You see what she done? I'm cut!" - the repetition, the self-pity, the audience-seeking. Kyle's "I called Carla" is a brilliant small moment that reveals his character completely - he's a frightened child who called the only adult he could think of, and the fallout (Ronnie's fury, Kyle's shame) is handled with economy and truth. "I left - I left a message. I said you was hurt." The stutter in that line is earned. The Lucy-through-the-vent moment is excellent: "and then Mabs is gonna get help and Mrs. Kline is gonna call and Dad's gonna be so mad, okay? So you have to be quiet, okay?" Lucy is doing what a scared 9-year-old would do - narrating a story where everything works out, trying to convince herself as much as Benny. This is consistent with her profile and emotionally resonant without being sentimental. Mabs' self-criticism - "I should have moved while they were yelling. That's what a real MP would do, right?" - is exactly the right note. It acknowledges her training without glorifying it, and the "right?" is a small, perfect touch of self-doubt. The laugh moment ("I was laughing. Just once, that wheezy gasp I do.") is well-placed and consistent with the character profile's note about her laughing when things get really bad. It doesn't feel forced. The ending is genuinely effective. "His fingers closed." followed by "I didn't scream. I couldn't. Benny screaming did it for me." - this is a clean, earned cliffhanger that doesn't rely on melodrama. The displacement of the scream onto Benny is psychologically true and structurally elegant. Minor criticisms: "That lawn fertilizer smell mixed with blood and rain" is slightly on-the-nose in its symbolism, though it works as sensory detail. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes - the slow chapter is meant to be slow, but a few of Ronnie's speeches could be trimmed without loss. The "figure out again. Like this was a math test" line is a small misstep - it's the one moment where Mabs' narration reaches for a simile that feels slightly beneath the prose's usual flatness. The chapter follows its plan almost exactly: Mabs stuck in the dark, listening, hand throbbing, hip cramping, Kyle crying about "you said Rich wouldn't be here," Ronnie trying to bargain/explain, the realization that she has to move because Benny is loud. The plan said "downstairs closet under the stairs" but the model places her on the stairs themselves - a minor deviation that actually works better dramatically, as it puts her in direct proximity to Ronnie's approach. Overall this is confident, controlled writing that trusts its own restraint. The voice is consistent, the characters behave according to their profiles, and the tension is built through accumulation of small, specific details rather than manufactured incident. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 6: 11:02 PM** His hand was on my ankle. Not grabbing yank. Just holding. Warm and wet. I could feel his thumb slip a little in blood or sweat. "Gotcha," he said, quiet, right under me in the dark. I could smell him. Gum gone soggy, rain, that copper penny smell of his side cut. My whole leg went stiff. I thought about kicking him in the face and then I thought about him pulling me down the stairs and my hip breaking all the way. Benny screamed again upstairs through the vent. That kind of scream where he's holding his breath between. Ronnie's fingers tightened. I did the only thing I could. I rolled. I just rolled off the second step sideways, onto the first step, then off the steps entirely onto the little rug runner that goes under the hall table. The rug that always bunches up. It bunched now and I went down on my bad hip and my vision went white for a second and I didn't make a noise. I bit my tongue to not make a noise. His hand lost my ankle when I rolled. He grabbed air. "Where - ?" he said. Then louder, panicked because he couldn't see. "KYLE! LIGHT! YOU GOT LIGHT?" Kyle's voice from the dark kitchen: "My phone - no battery - Unc, I can't see -" I was on the floor of the entry now. Cheek on the wet tile where rain blew in. Cereal paste under my face. The front door was right there, half open, black rain outside and the smell of wet grass. I could have crawled out. Felt it. The air out there was cold and real and I could just go into that bush and keep going and Mrs. Kline would call somebody and I wouldn't have to do this anymore. Then Benny wailed that wet diaper wail and I knew he had peed again. I turned away from the door. Dragged myself on my elbows toward the kitchen, toward the laundry room. The laundry room is off the kitchen. Tiny. Washer dryer stacked, shelf over it where Deb keeps Tide and wood cleaner she never uses. Window above the washer. Little crank window, maybe a foot by two feet, the one I smoke out. I pulled myself up using the dryer handle. My hip made me yell before I could stop it - just a short "ah!" that I swallowed. "She's in the kitchen!" Ronnie yelled. "Get her! Get her!" Kyle didn't move. I could hear him breathing fast in the dark living room. "I don't - where -" I got onto the washer. It rocked. Rich never leveled it. I cranked the little window handle. It stuck - paint stuck - then popped with a squeak. Cold rain came in sideways right in my face. Black outside. The little roof over the back patio is right there, flat tar, you can step out on it if you lean. I've done it before to smoke when Deb is home and I don't want her to see the window open. It's maybe a six foot drop to the ground and a slope. I got my leg out first. My good leg. My hoodie caught on the crank handle and ripped. My other leg, the bad hip leg, I had to drag and it screamed and my cut palm slipped on the wet sill and I almost went face first off the washer. Then I was out. Rain hammering my head flat. The tar roof was slick and pebbly and cold through my sweatpants. No light at all except the storm lighting everything grey for half a second at a time. The neighbor's big yard, the fence, the swing set that Rich never mowed around. The house has that second little window, Lucy's room. Right above this roof, two feet up. Her light is always those Christmas lights she taped up, but they were out with the power. I knelt on the tar and knocked on her window with my knuckle, quiet. "Lucy. Lucy it's me." Nothing. I knocked harder. My hand left a bloody print on the glass. A face appeared. Lucy, hair all frizz from humidity, eyes huge. She saw my face - must have seen blood still on my cheek - and she made a sound that wasn't words. She unlocked it. Old window, sticks. I had to shove from outside while she pulled. Rain came in between us. "What happened?" she whispered. It was the first time all night she wasn't being a shit. Benny was on her bed, curled around his dinosaur, in wet pull-ups. The room smelled like pee and kid sweat. The bathroom door in there was still shut but now I could see - they'd barricaded it with her little bookshelf. Smart girl. "Okay," I said, and water ran off my nose into her room. "Okay, listen. You have to go out this window. Onto the roof. Then down on the table by the fence and run to Mrs. Kline's. Don't stop." Lucy looked at the window, at the black rain. "Are you - that's - no." "You have to," I said. I was out of breath like I'd run a mile. "I can't get you both down the stairs. He's down there." Benny stared at me. "You're bleeding, Mabs." "I know. Take Brach with you." He held it tighter. Lucy grabbed my wrist. Her hand was small and cold. "Come with us." "I can't yet. I gotta - I gotta make sure he leaves. If you go to Kline's she'll call. You say the words: men in house, hurt babysitter, Ridgeview. You say Ridgeview, she'll know." "I don't wanna leave you," Benny whispered. It hit me sideways. Not brave. Just tired and pissed and wanting to sit in that wet with him and go. I pulled Benny up. He was heavier than he looked and wet and shaking. Lucy helped. We got him to the window, his bare feet on the sill. I went first back onto the tar to catch him. "On three, you drop your butt, okay? I got you. I got you, Benny the Jet. One -" I didn't do three. I just took his wrists and lowered him. He slipped in my bloody hands and fell the last foot onto the tar on his ass and didn't cry. Good kid. Lucy came out next, tougher, madder. She had our shoes? She had her shoes and my old New Balances. She threw them at me. "Put them on," she snapped, and I almost laughed that wheezy gasp again. We crouched there while lightning made the whole suburb white for a second. Every house dark. No one looking. I hugged them both, fast, wet hoodie against wet pajamas, and I smelled pee and apple juice and rain. "Run to the bush by the patio, then along the fence. Don't cut across Barlow's lawn, Kyle will see. Go to Kline's porch and bang. Bang loud. Don't stop." Lucy nodded, jaw set. That chipped tooth. Benny grabbed my hoodie string. "You coming after?" "Yeah. Two minutes. I'm right behind, okay? Run now." Lucy took his hand and they slid to the edge of the tar where the plastic patio table is. It was full of rainwater. She knocked it over - splash - and used it. Benny went first, then her, then they were down on the grass and running, hunched, Benny in just socks now holding his dinosaur over his head like rain would hurt it. I watched till they hit the side fence and disappeared toward the blue house where Kline's porch light should be but wasn't because power was out. Then I was alone on that roof. I turned around and crawled back to the laundry window. My leg was bleeding now, where I scraped it on the sill going in. Didn't matter. Inside is two men who thought they fucked with just the babysitter. I dropped back into the laundry room. Landed hard on the washer and then floor. My hip went out again and I had to bite my hoodie to not scream. In the kitchen, Ronnie yelled, "Did you hear that? On the roof! She's on the ROOF, Kyle!" I stood up in the dark kitchen, in wet clothes, holding the side of the dryer because my leg wouldn't take all of me yet. The front door was still half open, banging in the wind. Rain coming in, making the blood and cereal run. I went over and lifted and shoved it shut the way you're supposed to. Lift and shove. It stuck. It held. I locked it. With the shitty chain that was already busted I looped it through. Then I went back into the dark kitchen and stood at the bottom of the stairs, where they'd have to come up, and waited.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of pulp fiction writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The first-person present-tense-feeling past tense narration is tight and consistent. "Not grabbing yank. Just holding." - this kind of fragmented sentence structure works here because it mimics the way someone processes threat in real time. The Ohio specificity is present (the tar roof, the swing set Rich never mowed around, the plastic patio table full of rainwater). The voice stays in Mabs' register throughout - tired, practical, not heroic. WHAT WORKS WELL: The opening with Ronnie's hand on her ankle is genuinely tense. "Warm and wet. I could feel his thumb slip a little in blood or sweat." This is specific and physical without being overwrought. The detail earns its place. The moment where Mabs considers crawling out the front door into the rain and then hears Benny's "wet diaper wail" is the emotional core of the chapter and it lands. It's not sentimental - it's just a tired woman making a choice because she can't not. The plan called for this and the execution is clean. Lucy throwing the shoes is a perfect character beat. Small, mean, practical, and it almost makes Mabs laugh. Consistent with Lucy's profile (the snitch/show-off who is also competent when it matters). "Benny the Jet" - a small nickname that implies history without explaining it. Good. The ending is exactly right. She locks the broken door, loops the busted chain, and goes to wait at the bottom of the stairs. No monologue, no posing. The line "Inside is two men who thought they fucked with just the babysitter" is the one moment where the prose reaches for something more declarative, and it's borderline - it's the title prompt being invoked directly, which risks feeling like a chapter-title moment rather than a thought Mabs would actually have. It's the weakest line in the piece, slightly too self-aware. WHAT DOESN'T WORK AS WELL: The chapter plan called for Mabs to get the kids out via the roof and Lucy's window, which happens. It also called for Mabs to come back inside "on purpose" and lock the broken front door and wait at the top of the stairs. The chapter has her waiting at the bottom of the stairs, which is a small deviation but actually makes more tactical sense given the layout described. The plan said "cuts her leg on the way" - this is present but underplayed. The plan said Lucy "tells Mabs 'you're bleeding on my rug'" - this doesn't appear verbatim but the spirit is there with Benny saying "You're bleeding, Mabs." Minor. The emotional beat "It hit me sideways. Not brave. Just tired and pissed and wanting to sit in that wet with him and go." - this is good but slightly over-explained. The "not brave" qualifier is the author telling us how to read the moment rather than trusting the scene. The line "I went over and lifted and shoved it shut the way you're supposed to. Lift and shove. It stuck. It held." - the callback to the broken deadbolt detail from Chapter 1 is well-executed. This is the kind of structural payoff that elevates pulp. DIALOGUE: Sparse and functional. Ronnie's panicked yelling ("She's on the ROOF, Kyle!") is appropriately stupid and scared. Lucy's "Put them on" with the shoes is perfect. Benny's "You coming after?" is heartbreaking in a non-manipulative way. The dialogue is doing real work. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Largely absent. "Lightning made the whole suburb white for a second" is clean. No overwrought similes. The writing trusts its details. COHERENCE: The spatial logic of the house is maintained. The laundry room window, the tar roof, Lucy's window above it - this all tracks with what's been established. The power being out is used practically (no light, lightning for visibility). AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The "wet diaper wail" is slightly awkward as a compound noun. The final declarative line about "just the babysitter" is the most amateurish moment - it's the kind of line a writer puts in because they're proud of the title callback, not because Mabs would think it. OVERALL: This is a well-executed chapter that serves the story's larger architecture. It's not flashy but it's doing exactly what pulp fiction should do - move people through space under pressure while revealing character through action. The emotional beats are earned, the voice is consistent, and the plan is followed with intelligent small deviations. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 7: 12:07 AM** I locked the front door. That sounds stupid after everything. But it's what you do. Lift and shove. It caught. The busted chain I looped around the handle screw and through the deadbolt plate. It wouldn't hold anybody who really wanted in. It would hold ten seconds. That's enough when you know it's coming. The house was black black. Rain so loud on the roof it was like inside a drum. Water coming in somewhere - laundry window still open. I stood at the bottom of the stairs. I had nothing in my hands. That knife was somewhere upstairs on the carpet where I dropped it. My tote was in the laundry room but all that was in there was smokes and a lighter. "Unc?" Kyle's voice, from the living room. Whisper now. "Unc, I heard the door." Ronnie breathing. He'd moved to the couch, maybe. "You - you hear that? She went out?" I didn't answer.
