After the funeral she stopped eating, then stopped sleeping, then on a Tuesday in February walked down to the cove in her nightdress to go in. The groom found her ankle-deep, screaming about cold. Dr. Rowe said "hysteria, quiet, no excitement," and Sutcliffe - who had been with the family since Althea arrived and thinks quiet solves everything - moved her to the old nursery floor, where it's quiet indeed. She has not been downstairs for two years. Not because anyone locks the door every day - though Sutcliffe does lock it when there are visitors - but because everyone agreed downstairs was too much for her. She agreed too, for a while, because it was easier. **Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Blunt to the point of cruelty, because no one has contradicted her in years. Bored, not sad, most days - a deep, itchy boredom. She is not wise or ethereal. She is funny in a mean way. She keeps score. She hoards. Sugar lumps in a handkerchief, candle ends, the stubs of pencils. She will trade them. She smokes when she can get tobacco, which is rarely, so she chews the stem of her clay pipe like a worry stone. Trivial anchors: She hates washing her hair and wears a cap to hide how thin it is at the front. She picks at a scab on her left ankle that never heals. She can mend a sash with one hand while insulting you. When she is nervous she counts gulls. When she likes someone she gives them food. Core flaw: She frames her own confinement as other people's doing, which lets her not decide anything. She wants company but punishes it for arriving. She wanted to die once and now is ashamed she failed at that too. **Physicality:** 41, short and stout, 5'1", heavy bosomed, legs swollen at the ankle from sitting. Hair originally dark, now badly grey, thinned at the temples, kept in a thin plait under a greasy cap. Face red from indoor cold and too much tea - broken veins on cheeks, front teeth brown at edge. Hands surprisingly nimble, nails cut short. Moves slowly, with a waddle and a hand on furniture. Sits with knees wide. Smells of orris powder she puts on once a week, cold ash, and unwashed wool. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Motive: not to be left alone with the sound of the sea in the chimney. To have some say over something small - whether the window is open, whether she gets strong tea. She doesn't want to be cured; she wants to be consulted. Allegiances: To the house itself in a resentful way. To Sutcliffe in a tangled way - Sutcliffe kept her alive when she didn't want it, which she both hates her for and depends on. Her marriage was decent, not passionate. Her relationship with Thomas Ashcombe (her nephew by marriage? No, the boy Thomas is a distant cousin left with them) - she barely knows him because he wasn't allowed upstairs. With Eliza: Althea uses her at first (tea, news). Then she gets competitive - who can be bleaker? - then tender in a clumsy, invasive way. She wants to mother and insult Eliza at the same time. **Voice:** * Spoken (first meeting): "Oh. Another one. You shut that properly and the whole room screams. Heel of your hand. Like you mean it. Christ, you're frozen." * Spoken (affection as insult): "That glove. You call that darning? Give it here before you blind yourself with that needle." * Internal (not spoken aloud): "If she comes tonight I will have someone to talk at. If she doesn't I will have a reason to be angry, which is almost as good." # Mrs. Agatha Sutcliffe **Background:** Born Agatha Triggs, started at Ashcombe as scullery maid at 12 in 1816. Married the head groom Thomas Sutcliffe at 27, he died of a kick from a mare in 1859. No children that lived past a week - two girls, both buried. She has run the house since Mrs. Ashcombe the elder died in 1838. She can tell you which flagstone lifts in the hall, which chimney draws. When she was 16 she shinned up that same downpipe - then new cast iron - to meet a boy. She knows it sways. **Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws:** Practical to the point where kindness looks like cruelty. She does not believe in nervous complaints, she believes in cold, damp, idleness and lack of beef tea. She believes the house is a body and if she lets it get loose, it will die. She is not a snob - she despises everyone equally for not doing their job. Governesses are the worst because their job is ambiguous. She would respect Eliza more if Eliza scrubbed floors. Trivial anchors: She talks aloud when she thinks no one can hear - short sentences to the range, to the pump: "Come on, you bugger." She keeps a piece of hard ginger in her apron pocket and shaves bits off with her teeth. Her knees crack loudly and she does not apologize for it. She lines up shoes at night even if they are not hers. Core flaw: She equates care with containment. She thinks locking a door is protection. She kept Althea upstairs because she genuinely thought the sea air and visitors would kill her, and having kept her alive, she cannot admit it might now be killing her differently. She cannot say sorry, only "I told you." **Physicality:** 58, tall for a woman of her time, 5'7" and square. Big knuckled hands, red, wrists thick. Hair steel grey, scraped into a bun so tight it pulls her eyes. Missing a back molar - you see it when she shouts. Walks with a slight roll because knees are bad but never uses a stick - holds furniture instead when she thinks you aren't looking. Wears three keys always and you hear her before you see her. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Motive: keep Ashcombe Hall standing until her own death, no matter who has to be uncomfortable. Keep coal use low, keep the roof patched, keep Althea alive. Allegiance: to the Hall, not the family. The family comes and goes; she stays. She feels maternal but shows it by mending hems without comment. With Althea: Sutcliffe mothered her when Althea married in, and now mothers her like a difficult adult child - resentful, exhausted, loving in withholding. With Eliza: recognizes her instantly - another thin, sharp girl who thinks she's above the kitchen but isn't high enough for the parlour. Dislikes her because she is a reminder. Respects her only after she climbs badly and does it again anyway. **Voice:** * Spoken (tour): "Front door is not for you. Back door is bolted at eight. If you are out after, you are out. I am not getting up for governesses." * Spoken (finding footprints): "You think I don't know that mark? That's my boot from forty year ago, darling. Only smaller. That middle bracket will go. I have said it will go." * Internal / muttered to range: "Window open again. Bloody gulls will be in. And then who's cleaning it?" # Thomas Ashcombe (11) - Minor but load-bearing **Quick Sketch:** Nephew-cousin left with Ashcombe after his parents died in Quebec. Not wild - feral from neglect. Thin, knees always bruised, knows every rat run. Worships Sutcliffe in a way that makes him try to impress her by snitching, then feels guilty. He is the one who teaches Eliza the route because no adult ever told him not to tell a woman how to climb. Flaw: lies to be interesting. Physical: big ears, hair badly cut with kitchen shears, speaks too fast. Voice: "You have to push with your arse. No - like this. Watch. And don't breathe on the middle one, it rusts."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. I learned it because Mrs. Sutcliffe bolts the back door at eight. The agency letter said Ashcombe Hall, parish of St. Levan, coach from Penzance. It did not say the coach would drop me at the turnpike and not go further for the wet. I walked the last mile with my box banging my knee. Rain came in sideways off the sea. By the time I saw the house my boots were full of water and my one good dress was stuck to my legs. It is not a hall in the way they mean in books. It is granite, low and square, with a slate roof that shines. It has two windows boarded on the seaward side and a yard that is all clabber and hens. The paint on the front door had blistered. I stood there a minute with sea water dripping off my nose and then went and knocked at the front because the agency letter said "front entrance for interview" and I am literal. Mrs. Sutcliffe opened it six inches and looked at me. She is tall. Taller than me. Grey hair scraped back. A ring of keys at her waist that clicked when she walked. "We don't use that," she said. "Come round." I said my name was Eliza Finch, the new governess, and she was expecting me. She let the door go to. I had to go round to the back through the yard. The hens had got in a sack of meal and scattered it. I picked up my box and slipped. The kitchen is bigger than the whole cottage my aunt has in St. Austell. Range lit, a table scrubbed white, smell of boiled turnips. A girl of about fifteen stared at me and then looked away. Mrs. Sutcliffe wiped her hands on her apron and did the tour without pausing. "You won't be wanted in the front. You won't use the front door. The family rooms are not for governess. Your room is the small attic over the north side. The children are Thomas, eleven, and Alice, eight. Their parents died of the flux in Quebec. Mrs. Ashcombe is the mistress but she is indisposed. She does not receive. Master is dead. You will have the children from seven till four. You will have cold meat if there is any left. You will not be in the kitchen when we are taking dinner. Back door is bolted at eight sharp. I am not getting up to let governesses in. If you are out after, you are out. Do you understand me?" I said yes. I was biting the skin by my left thumbnail. It bled. She took me up. We went up two sets of stairs. The second set narrower. The stone was sweating even though it was cold. My room was a slant under the eaves with a cot, a basin, a jug with a chip, a copy of Foxe's Martyrs with no cover. No fire. One window that looked out not at the sea but at the scullery roof. The glass ran with water. "You'll start now," she said. The children were in what used to be the schoolroom but is mostly now a place to keep a table and two chairs and a heap of shells. Thomas has big ears and hair that has been cut with shears. Alice has a cough. I told them I was Miss Finch. Thomas said, "Last one left." Alice said, "She cried." I had no plan. I sat and made them spell. Thomas can spell but won't. Alice writes her a's backwards. They have chilblains like mine. Their socks are too big. At four I took them down as instructed and Mrs. Sutcliffe gave me a plate. Cold mutton, fat white and set, with a spoon of turnip that had gone watery. I ate it at the table in the schoolroom alone because the kitchen was for dinner and the dining room is not for me. The mutton had gristle in it that I had to chew and chew. After, I was meant to mend. Sutcliffe gave me a basket of stockings. I tried. My left wrist ached from carrying the box. The darning wool was coarse and the needle had rust at the eye. I pricked my finger and bled on a sock. I wanted to post a letter to the agency to say I had arrived so they would keep me on their books. I had written it on Sunday before I left Exeter. If you do not tell them you arrived they mark you as troublesome. The post is collected from the village at half past five. It was near five when I finished the socks. I put on my shawl and my boots, which were still damp inside. I told the children to stay. Sutcliffe was in the kitchen with the girl. "Where are you going?" "To the village to post a letter, Mrs. Sutcliffe." "Be back." The village is not a village, just four cottages and the public house. The rain had come on harder and the wind took my shawl. By the time I got to the box my stockings were soaked again. The box was locked for the night. The publican said collection had been at four that week because of the tide. I stood there holding my letter until the ink ran. It was dark coming back. I had no candle. The path has ruts that fill with water. I had to feel my way by the stone wall. My petticoat hem got heavy with mud and pulled. I could see Ashcombe, one light downstairs in the kitchen. The rest dark. The wind made the slates clatter. I tried the back door. It was bolted. I could hear Sutcliffe's keys and then her step going away. I knocked. Once, politely. Then again, harder, skin coming off my knuckle. No one came. The rain was now proper Cornish rain, which is like needles. My hair had come out of its pins. I picked up a pebble and threw it at the window but it is a small high window over the scullery and I missed. I went and stood under the washhouse lean-to because it had a roof. It is open on one side. There is a copper and a pile of sticks and the stink of wet ash. A cat that lives there stared at me. I sat on the upturned bucket and took my hands and put them between my thighs under my skirt to get them warm. It is unseemly but no one could see. I thought: I will have to beg in the morning. I will have to stand there with my wet dress and ask to be let in and thank her and she will tell me the rule again slowly. There was a light on above me. I had not seen it because the washhouse roof cuts the view. Third floor, at the back where my own little attic is but further over. Not my window. The old nursery, maybe. One lamp, swaying a little as if someone had set it down or the draught got it. I watched it for a long time. It did not go out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of opening chapter writing. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Style** The first-person past tense voice is precisely calibrated. Eliza's narration is clipped, literal, and bodily without ever becoming precious about its own austerity. "Rain came in sideways off the sea" is plain and accurate. "The mutton had gristle in it that I had to chew and chew" is exactly the kind of unglamorous physical specificity the plan called for. The sentence rhythms vary naturally - short declaratives for facts, occasional run-ons for anxiety ("I told them I was Miss Finch. Thomas said, 'Last one left.' Alice said, 'She cried.'"). The voice never tips into retrospective wit or self-conscious spareness. **Character Execution** Eliza is rendered with remarkable fidelity to her profile. The detail of biting her thumbnail until it bleeds appears naturally. Her literalness ("I knocked at the front because the agency letter said 'front entrance for interview' and I am literal") is shown rather than announced. Her mental ledger of costs is present in the way she tracks the sock, the needle, the rust, the pricked finger. She is not sympathetic in a conventional way - she tells the children to stay without warmth, she eats alone, she is practical about her own humiliation. Sutcliffe's introduction is excellent. The six-inch door opening, the keys clicking, the tour delivered "without pausing" - these are precise character details. Her voice ("I am not getting up to let governesses in") matches the profile exactly. Thomas and Alice's brief appearances are load-bearing: "Last one left" and "She cried" tell us everything about the house's history with governesses in four words each. **Plot and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: arrival in sleet, Sutcliffe's tour, the 8 o'clock rule established, the failed letter-posting, the locked door, the washhouse. The ending - the light on the third floor - is the correct hook. Nothing is rushed or skipped. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is right for this story. Every line of dialogue does work. Sutcliffe's tour speech is particularly good - it establishes hierarchy, rules, and character simultaneously without feeling like an info-dump because it's delivered in her voice, not the narrator's summary. **What Doesn't Quite Land** The chapter is almost uniformly excellent, which makes small imperfections more visible. "Proper Cornish rain, which is like needles" is the one moment that edges toward the kind of regional-color simile that could become a tic. It's not bad, but it's the most conventional image in the piece. The observation about the light "swaying a little as if someone had set it down or the draught got it" is nicely uncertain and specific. The ending is well-judged - it doesn't over-explain the significance of the light, just leaves Eliza watching it. This is the correct instinct. **Creativity and Surprise** The plan's core anti-romantic choices are already visible: the house is small and wet, not grand; the governess is not charming; the housekeeper is not a villain but a system. The decision to have Eliza sleep in the washhouse rather than be dramatically rescued is exactly right. The cat staring at her is a small, perfect detail. **Overall Assessment** This is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with unusual precision and discipline. It establishes character, setting, stakes, and tone without a wasted sentence. The prose is controlled without being cold. The darkness is earned through accumulation of specific, unglamorous detail rather than atmospheric gesturing. It is not a masterpiece - it is a very strong opening chapter that successfully resists every temptation the genre usually succumbs to. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I got back in at six in the morning. Sutcliffe unbolted the back door and found me under the washhouse lean-to asleep with my cheek on the copper edge. My dress had dried stiff where the mud was. She did not say anything at first. She just stood there with her keys. "Up," she said. "You will frighten the girl." I stood. My legs had gone dead. The bucket had left a ring on my stocking. "Eight means eight," she said. "Do you understand me now?" I said yes. I was holding my left thumb skin in my teeth so I did not cry. She let me in to change my stockings. No fire yet in my room, the jug rimed with ice inside. I wrung out my petticoat and left it to drip onto Foxe's Martyrs. My nose was running and I wiped it with my sleeve. That day I taught twice as loud so the children would not see my hands shaking. At dinner there was no mutton left. Sutcliffe said if I wanted to be fed at hours I should keep hours. I had a crust. At half past seven I went out to the privy and when I came back the back door was already bolted. I had been seven minutes. I knocked. I heard Thomas's voice inside, "That's Miss Finch," and then Sutcliffe saying, "Bedtime." I stood there with my hand on the wet wood. The kitchen light went out. Rage came up in my throat like hot water. Not tears. The sort of rage I used to get when my aunt would move my sewing to make room for bonnet straw and then say I was idle. I went back to the washhouse and sat on the bucket and swore under my breath. Then I got up and walked round the house in the dark. It is not high, Ashcombe, for a manor. Two floors proper and then the attics shoved under the roof. On the north side there are slates missing and a gutter that has come loose and drips. The windows downstairs are all shuttered and fastened. Upstairs, on the third, the windows are smaller, with thick glass. The one that had the light last night is at the back, over the scullery. From the ground you can just see it under the eaves. Under it, not ivy, a downpipe. Cast iron, once painted, now brown. It goes from the roof gutter all the way to a drain. It is held to the granite by three iron brackets. The middle one has pulled loose of the stone and the pipe wobbles when you touch it. I touched it. I had no plan except not to sleep in the washhouse again. My aunt in St Austell used to make me sleep in the outhouse when I talked back and the cat got in with me and I never got used to it. I took my boots off. My stockings were already wet through so I left them. I tucked my skirts up between my legs and knotted them. My drawers showed but there was no one to see. I tried. The first bracket is at head height. If you grip and put your foot on the lower stones, you can get a knee up to it. The iron was so cold it burned. It smelled of rust and rain. I got two feet up and the middle bracket gave a shriek and moved half an inch. I panicked and jumped. It is not far. Six feet. But I landed wrong on my left wrist with my whole weight. Something popped. Pain shot up my arm that was bright and sick. I sat in the mud and vomited a little water. My hem had torn where it caught on the drain cover. My left wrist swelled at once, thick and hot. I held it with my right hand and breathed through my mouth. Thomas said, from the dark, "You have to not push on the middle one." I nearly shouted. He was at the corner of the house in a nightshirt too big for him, feet bare, teeth chattering. "What are you doing out?" I said. "What are you doing?" "Nothing. Go inside." He came closer and looked at the pipe. "Sutcliffe locks it. So we go out the top." "What?" He showed me with his hands. "You sit on the sill, Alice does it backwards, and you push your arse out till your feet catch the pipe. Then you don't use the middle. You swing." He had done it. Often. "Does Mrs. Sutcliffe know?" I asked. My wrist was throbbing now so I had to jam it between my thighs to hold it. "She used to," he said. "She pretends she doesn't. She says if we break our necks she will use us for soup." He looked at my wrist. "Did you fall?" "No." He nodded. "You need to use your palms. Heel. Like this." He put his hands up as if lifting a window. Then he vanished round the corner, bare feet slapping. I waited till the pain dulled to a steady pulse and tried again. This time I did not look at the middle bracket. I put my right hand, then right foot, then left foot up high, so my weight was on the top bracket. The pipe did hold. It shivered but held. The window is old sash, wood swollen. One pane cracked from corner to corner with a bit of brown paper stuck over it. There is a lip of stone under it, three inches wide. I got my right knee onto the lip. My arms shook. My skirt had come undone and hung down over my face. I was breathing so hard I could taste iron. I got both palms under the sash and pushed. Heel of the hand, like Thomas said. It did not move. I pushed again, my bad wrist screaming. The glass rattled. The paper tore. On the third push it gave with a screech that I was sure would wake Sutcliffe in her grave, let alone her bed. Cold air fell out. Black inside. I dragged myself over the sill. My good knee hit the chamber pot and sent it skittering. I landed on rough boards on my side, skirts over my head, petticoat showing, boots that I had tied by their laces and clenched in my teeth gone soft with spit and fallen. I lay there panting. The smell hit me then. Not orris root. Cold ash, and old hair oil, and wee that has been left. It was freezing. Colder than outside because no wind. There was a grate with no fire, just paper and dust. A sewing table with a pair of shears rusted shut. A rocking chair. And a woman asleep in the rocking chair with a shawl round her. She had a cap on, grey hair thin at the front, mouth open. Fat ankles over the chair edge. A lamp on the floor guttering. She snored once. I had got in through the upper window. My wrist was the size of a turnip.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably disciplined piece of writing that executes its plan with unusual fidelity and craft. Let me work through it systematically. **Execution of Plan:** The chapter plan called for Eliza to be locked out a second time, attempt the pipe, fall and sprain her wrist, have Thomas reveal the route, and finally haul herself in to find Althea asleep in the chair. The chapter delivers all of this with precision. The plan's specific details—the chamber pot, the dead flies (omitted but replaced with equivalent sensory detail), the swollen sash, the palm-heel technique—are rendered faithfully. The chapter ends exactly where planned: Eliza inside, wrist swollen, Althea asleep. **Voice and Style:** The first-person voice is exceptionally controlled. The prose is plain, bodily, and specific without ever becoming precious. "Pain shot up my arm that was bright and sick" is exactly the kind of unshowy precision the plan demanded. The sentence rhythms vary naturally—short declaratives under stress, occasional run-ons when anxiety builds. "I had no plan except not to sleep in the washhouse again. My aunt in St Austell used to make me sleep in the outhouse when I talked back and the cat got in with me and I never got used to it." This is character revelation through accumulation, not declaration. **Avoidance of Gothic Cliché:** The plan explicitly warned against "Thesaurus Gothic" and the chapter honors this completely. The manor is cold, smells of wee, has a rusted chamber pot. Althea's first appearance is: fat ankles, thin grey hair, mouth open, snoring. This is a genuine achievement—the anti-romantic is sustained without becoming self-consciously gritty. **Physical Specificity:** The climbing sequence is the chapter's centerpiece and it earns its place. The detail is granular and credible: the three-inch stone lip, the swollen sash, the boots clenched in teeth going soft with spit. The fall is rendered with appropriate violence—"Something popped. Pain shot up my arm that was bright and sick. I sat in the mud and vomited a little water." This is not melodrama; it's accurate. **Dialogue:** Thomas's dialogue is excellent—practical, childlike, slightly ghoulish ("She says if we break our necks she will use us for soup"). It reveals character and advances plot simultaneously. Sutcliffe's two lines ("Up" and "Eight means eight") are perfectly economical. The dialogue is sparse but every line works. **Character Consistency:** Eliza's rage is rendered as physical sensation rather than emotional declaration ("Rage came up in my throat like hot water. Not tears."). Her pride—"Did you fall?" / "No."—is exactly right. Her snooping instinct manifests in the inventory of the room she's broken into. Thomas is exactly as sketched: practical, feral, slightly showing off. **Emotional Engagement:** The chapter builds genuine tension through accumulation of small humiliations—the crust instead of dinner, the seven minutes, the kitchen light going out. The rage is earned. The fall is visceral. The final image—Eliza on the floor, wrist swollen, Althea snoring—lands with dark comedy that is entirely earned. **Minor Criticisms:** The opening section (the first lockout aftermath) moves very quickly through what was the previous chapter's events. The line "I had got in through the upper window" at the end is slightly too on-the-nose as a callback to the prompt—it reads as a self-conscious wink. The chapter is also slightly front-loaded with the aftermath of the first lockout before getting to the main action, though this is brief. The plan mentioned "dead flies in the track" as a specific sensory detail that was dropped, though the replacement details are equally good. **Overall Assessment:** This is genuinely accomplished literary fiction. It avoids every trap the plan identified and executes the physical and emotional beats with precision. The prose is clean, the character voice is consistent and distinctive, and the chapter advances the plot while building character through action rather than exposition. The anti-romantic rendering of the manor and its occupants is sustained without becoming a pose. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
She woke when I knocked the pot. Not all at once. First her head tipped further and she snored louder. Then she opened her eyes straight at me lying on the floor with my skirt over my knees and my boots on the boards where I'd dropped them. She did not scream. She looked at me, then at the window, then at me again. Her eyes were watery and very pale blue. "Oh," she said. Her voice was hoarse, like she had not used it. "Another one. Shut it, you're letting the sea in." I scrambled up. My left wrist made me clap my teeth together. I said, "I am sorry - I - the back door -" "Yes," she said. "She does that." She put her hands on the arms of the chair and pushed up. She is short. Shorter than me and twice as wide. She had a thick shawl pinned with a big rusted brooch, and stockings that had fallen down around her fat ankles. There was a scab on the left one she had scratched. A cap, greasy at the edge, over hair that was thin at the front so you could see scalp. "The heel," she said. "You have to do it with the heel of your hand. Like you mean it. Otherwise it screams." I had my right hand on the sash still. I pushed down. It screamed anyway and stuck half-shut. "Leave it," she said. "Once it's open you can't shut it proper without tallow. I have none." This was the mistress. I knew it now because of the chair and the way she looked at the room as if it owed her. "Mrs. Ashcombe?" I said. "Mrs. Althea Ashcombe," she said. "And you are the new girl. Miss Finch. Thomas said you had a Birmingham accent which you don't. Sutcliffe said you were stuck up." She sat back down heavily. The chair creaked. My wrist was beating. I tucked it under my other arm. She saw. "What have you done?" "Fell." "Off the pipe. Yes. Everyone falls off that pipe. I told them it wanted fixing before my husband died and he said the price of iron." She squinted. "Let me see." I did not want to. She held out a hand. Her nails were short and yellow. She smelled of old powder and cold. I showed her. It was fat and turning blue around the little finger side. She prodded it with one finger. I made a noise. "Not broke," she said. "Sprained. I did both trying to get to the cove. You need it bound." She got up again, went to a chest that had a broken lock, and rummaged. She is one of those people who hoards inside drawers. I could see: lumps of sugar in paper, a bit of candle, a clay pipe stem chewed flat. She tore a strip off what looked like an old petticoat. It was not clean but nothing up here is. "Hold out." She bound it too tight at first, then loosened it. Her hands were warm. She tied the knot with her teeth. "There," she said. "Don't use it for a week or it will stay weak and then what use are you." I thanked her. My voice was small. She sat again. "Are you going to tell Sutcliffe you were here?" "No," I said quickly. "She would dismiss me." "Yes." She looked satisfied. "She will anyway. She always does with the governesses. They are either weepers or they have ideas." She picked up the lamp. Its flame jumped. "I am not supposed to have tea after four," she said, as if continuing a conversation we had been having for months. "Sutcliffe says it keeps me awake and then I am cross. But I am cross anyway and I am awake anyway. I have not had strong tea since February." I understood then. She was not mad in the way they say in novels. She was shut up. And bored. So bored she had counted the flies in the window track. There were fourteen, dead. I asked, still stupid with pain, "Do you live up here?" "I sleep up here," she said. "I live wherever I like. Which is here, because downstairs they all look at me and count how many spoonfuls I have. Sutcliffe and Dr Rowe said quiet. Quiet is this. Three rooms that smell of sick." She had two other doors off this nursery. One open into a dark bedroom with a big bed made up and not slept in. The other shut. "Why is the window unlatched?" I asked. "Because if it is latched I can't breathe," she said. "Also Thomas comes in that way when he is meant to be having his Latin. He brings me things if I ask nice, which I don't. I ask as his mistress should." We looked at each other. I was cold right through now, my stockings wet, my muddy hem dripping onto her bare boards. I must have looked as bad as I felt. She said, "You can come in that way if you want. I am tired of the gulls being my only visitors." I said, "Mrs. Sutcliffe bolts the back door at eight. If I am out after prayers - if I post a letter - I am locked out." "Yes," she said again. "She has done that since she was a girl and got locked out herself and her mother beat her. So now she does it to everyone. Christian justice." She pulled her shawl tighter. "I will leave it open an inch if I want company. You will know because I will put the rag in. That old flannel. See?" She pointed to a grey piece wedged on the sill. "If the rag is in the track, it's open for you. If it's out, I am bad and you are not to come. And you will bring me things." "What things?" "Tea. Not the dust she brings me. The real tea from the caddy. And the newspaper if there is one and she has done with it. And a proper chop, not boiled till it is a rag. And tallow for that sash. And you will talk to me while I eat it and not speak of your soul." It was not unkind, the way she said it. Just practical. I said, "Yes." "And you will not tell Sutcliffe I am still using this floor," she added. "She thinks I am in the bedroom. Which I am, in the day. But at night I am here because the bedroom smells of my husband's pomade still and I cannot get it out." I nodded. My wrist throbbed inside the bandage. "What's your glove?" she said suddenly and grabbed my right hand before I could pull it back. My glove, the good one, has the forefinger worn through and I have my ink stain showing, black like I have been tattooed. "Bad darning," she said. "Give it here tomorrow. I can do better than that with my eyes shut, which is nearly how I do it." She let go. She looked at the window again where the sea wind was coming hard. "Go now," she said, "or she will hear you come downstairs. Use the inside stairs, not the pipe at night, you stupid girl. Only come up the pipe after eight from outside." She had two staircases up here - the main one that goes down to the locked door Sutcliffe keeps, and the servants' back ladder that is nailed shut at the bottom. I went down the main one on tiptoe, one hand on the wall because my wrist couldn't hold the rail. The door at the bottom of the third floor was locked on the outside, but the key was in it on the inside, left there. I turned it. It was stiff. Thomas must oil it. I came out on the second floor landing where no one goes and slipped into my own attic without being seen. I took off my wet things and hid the bandaged wrist under my sleeve. At seven, when Thomas came for lessons, he saw the bandage. He said nothing, just looked at me and then away. Up in the nursery, the grey rag was tucked back into the window track.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes the plan with considerable skill and discipline. The meeting between Eliza and Althea is handled with exactly the anti-romantic, transactional energy the plan promised, and the writing largely delivers on its stated intentions. **Voice and Style:** The first-person voice is tight and bodily throughout. "My wrist was beating" is a good example of the plain, physical register the plan called for. The prose avoids the thesaurus Gothic the plan explicitly warned against. Sentences are short and declarative when Eliza is observing, and the occasional run-on ("She had two other doors off this nursery. One open into a dark bedroom with a big bed made up and not slept in. The other shut.") creates rhythm without calling attention to itself. The detail work is strong: the fourteen dead flies in the window track, the sugar lumps in paper, the clay pipe stem chewed flat, the scab on the ankle. These are earned specifics, not mood-board decoration. **Character Consistency:** Althea arrives exactly as profiled - blunt, hoarding, bored rather than ethereal, funny in a mean way. "I am cross anyway and I am awake anyway. I have not had strong tea since February" is a perfect line for her. The detail about the bedroom smelling of her husband's pomade is quietly devastating and consistent with her profile's note that her marriage was decent but not passionate. Eliza's voice is also consistent - she notices the hoarding, counts the flies, understands the situation quickly and practically. The moment she tucks her wrist under her arm before Althea sees it, then shows it anyway, is good character work. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It does not explain itself. "She does that" in response to the locked door is perfect - it establishes Althea's knowledge of the house, her resignation, and her dry humor in three words. The negotiation of terms ("You will bring me things") is genuinely transactional and avoids sentimentality. The glove moment at the end is well-placed - it's the first gesture of something warmer, but it's framed as criticism ("Bad darning") which keeps it honest. **Plot and Structure:** The chapter follows its plan closely. The bargain is established, the rag-in-the-window signal is introduced, and the chapter ends with the rag back in the track - a clean, earned image that doesn't overreach. The revelation that Thomas already uses the route is handled economically. The detail about the key being left in the lock on the inside, and Thomas presumably oiling it, is a nice piece of world-building that doesn't announce itself. **Weaknesses:** There are a few moments where the writing slightly over-explains. "I understood then. She was not mad in the way they say in novels. She was shut up. And bored. So bored she had counted the flies in the window track." The first two sentences are unnecessary - the reader has already understood this from Althea's dialogue. The fly-counting detail is strong on its own; the interpretive sentences before it are mild tell-don't-show violations. Similarly, "It was not unkind, the way she said it. Just practical." is slightly redundant given that the dialogue has already established this. The plan called for Eliza to be "still stupid with pain" when she asks "Do you live up here?" - this is a slightly clunky question that the chapter acknowledges but doesn't quite redeem. The chapter is also slightly shorter than the ~1000 word target implied by the plan, though it doesn't feel truncated - it ends at the right moment. **Overall:** This is disciplined, specific, anti-sentimental writing that earns its small moments of warmth. The characters are consistent with their profiles, the chapter follows its plan faithfully, and the prose avoids the pitfalls the plan identified. The weaknesses are minor and mostly involve brief lapses into telling what the scene has already shown. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
It blew all day and then at four the rain came sideways and didn't stop. We could hear it in the schoolroom, beating on the scullery roof. Thomas said it was a westerly and would take slates. Alice cried because her chilblains itched. At six Mrs. Sutcliffe told me not to go out. She said it as if I would: "You will not go out in this, Miss Finch, and get yourself drowned and then who's fetching you." I said I would not. I meant it. Then at supper there was a plate again and after she gave me the caddy key to get tea for the morning because the girl has a gumboil and cannot be trusted. The caddy is tin, with real Hyson in it, not the sweepings she gives us and Althea. I stood there with my bad wrist aching inside the binding and looked at it. I took three pinches and put them in a paper packet from my own pocket. My hand shook because it was stealing and it was also so easy. I had got tallow too. From the kitchen, a stub on a plate by the range. I wrapped it in more paper and put it up my sleeve. It melted a little and made a grease mark. At eight the back door was bolted. I waited. My attic window has no view of the nursery window, but I could hear the sea. I sat on my cot with my boots in my lap and waited till the house went quiet. Sutcliffe's keys, then her heavy step to her room, then the girl coughing downstairs, then nothing but the wind in the chimney and the gutter banging. I went out the back way through the downstairs passage even though it was bolted. You can unbolt it from inside, slip out, and pull it to. I did that. The rain hit me so hard it took my breath. I left my boots off. I carry them by the laces in my teeth now. It is better than round my neck. Thomas was right about not breathing on the middle bracket — it rusts and the rust makes it slick. I knew it now. Right hand to the top bracket, right foot high, ignore the middle, left foot to the sill lip, arse out. My skirt over my knees again. The wind tried to peel me off. The rag was in. Grey flannel wedged in the track. I knocked the way she told me to: two taps on the glass so she knows it isn't Thomas. The lamp was lit. Althea was awake, in the chair, with a blanket round her that was too thin. The room was so cold you could see your breath. She looked up and said, "Well. You're wet." She got up and shut the door to the bedroom so the draught would not take the lamp out. "You brought it?" she said. I put the boots down inside the sill and unwrapped my hands. My wrist throbbed. Then I got the paper packet out of my pocket. It had gone damp where my palm sweated. She took it and sniffed it. "That's proper," she said. She went to the table where she had a cracked cup and a little kettle that she heats over a spirit lamp when Sutcliffe isn't looking. We had a practice now. She measures with her fingers. She made two cups, one thin for show in case anyone came, one strong, black, with sugar she had saved in her handkerchief. She gave me the strong one. It burned my tongue and I drank it too fast because it was hot. "You didn't bring the paper," she said. "The wind took the Penzance one off the line and Sutcliffe threw it on the fire." "She would." She sat. She looked at my wrist. "Take that off." The binding had got wet. She unwound it. The swelling was down but still blue. She prodded again. I did not make a noise this time. She had got lard somewhere, not tallow, from somewhere. She rubbed it in with a hard thumb. It hurt but warm after. "You should keep it up," she said. "But you won't." Then she saw my glove. I had taken them off to climb. The right one with the forefinger worn through. The ink stain. My aunt said the ink would never come out because I dip too deep. "Give me that," she said, and before I could she had it. She fetched the sewing box that had the rusted shears. She got a needle, not a good one, thick like a sack needle, and black wool. "This is too thick," she said, and did it anyway. Big, clumsy stitches over the hole, pulling tight so the finger puckered. "It's worse," I said without thinking. "Yes," she said. "But now it will hold and your finger won't show and you won't be ashamed." She gave it back. It was worse. It was also warmer. Then she showed me the sash. "You do it like this," she said. "Not up. Out and up. Because the wood is swollen you have to give it slack." She took my good hand and put it on the sash. Her hand over mine. Both cold at first. "Heel here. Now pull to you a little - yes - now lift." The tallow I had brought had gone soft. She took it from my sleeve where I had put it and smeared it along the runners with her thumb. It smelled of mutton. We tried again together. The sash lifted without screaming this time. Just a sigh. "There," she said, pleased. "You have to know how." The rain came in for a second and wet us both. We shut it again. We sat and she talked. Not about feelings. About the house, when it was built, which stones let water, how her husband used to try and fix the roof himself and made it worse. How Sutcliffe had once locked her in the stillroom by mistake and left her there for three hours and she had eaten all the raisins. I told her about my aunt's bonnets and about Thomas chewing his cuff. At some point I was reading. She asked me to read the bit of old Telegraph she had kept from September, about a wreck off Zennor. I read and kept losing my place because the lamp smoked and my eyes hurt from cold. She corrected my pronunciation of "lugger." Then she stopped talking and put her head back in the chair as if she had dropped off but her eyes were open. Outside it had got worse. The gutter was clanging like someone hitting it with a stick. Water came under the paper on the cracked pane. I should have gone then. I said, "I should go." She said, "Yes." Neither of us moved. Then she said, "Your hands are still cold," and took both of them between hers. Not like a lady takes a hand, light, but like you hold bread under your armpit to warm it. Hard, both hands, squeezing. Her hands were rough and warm from the shawl. Mine were red and cracked. No one has held my hands like that since my father died. In Falmouth the old lady had me rub her feet. My aunt held my wrist to hit it. I sat there and let her. After a minute she let go and said, "If you stay all night you will be late for your cold meat." I got my boots. In the morning they would be wet inside again and I would have to put my stockings on wet. I lifted the sash the right way this time, heel, out and up, and sat on the sill with my legs out into rain. She did not say come again. She just took the rag out of the track, then put it back in, an inch of air showing. I went down. The middle bracket held because I did not look at it. At the bottom I stood in mud up to my ankles and realized my petticoat had come unknotted and was showing again. I pushed it down and went round to unbolt the back door from inside and bolt it again behind me. I was in bed by near five, shivering, gloves clutched. The right finger all stitched thick and ugly and holding.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of intimate realism. The model has executed its own plan with unusual fidelity and discipline, and the result is something genuinely accomplished. The chapter follows the plan almost exactly: a storm night, neither sleeping, Eliza bringing stolen tea, Althea binding the wrist, re-stitching the glove badly, teaching the sash technique with tallow, reading aloud from the old newspaper, and the climactic moment of hand-holding that is explicitly not romantic in the conventional sense but is the most intimate thing Eliza has experienced since her father's death. The final image of the rag replaced in the track is precisely the "You left the window unlatched for me" ending the plan promised. Character consistency is excellent. Eliza's voice is exactly as designed: bodily, precise, anti-sentimental, with a mental ledger running ("It was stealing and it was also so easy"). Her self-awareness is calibrated correctly — she notices things without editorializing them into meaning. The detail of carrying boots by the laces in her teeth, the shaking hand over the stolen tea, the wet petticoat at the end — these are all consistent with the character profile's emphasis on physical specificity and class humiliation in the body. Althea is rendered with equal fidelity: blunt, invasive, giving food as affection, the clumsy thick stitches that are "worse" but "will hold." The line "Yes. But now it will hold and your finger won't show and you won't be ashamed" is one of the best in the chapter — it captures Althea's particular mode of care, which is practical and slightly condescending and genuinely tender all at once, without any of those words being used. The dialogue is notably strong. It is spare, functional, and character-revealing without being writerly. "Well. You're wet." is a perfect opening. The correction of "lugger" pronunciation is a small, precise detail that does real work — it establishes Althea's local knowledge, her need to assert authority, and the texture of their relationship. The exchange "I should go." / "Yes." / [neither moves] is a classic technique but executed without sentimentality. The plan called for avoiding "Thesaurus Gothic" and the chapter delivers. There is no "frisson," no "palpable sorrow," no atmospheric overreach. The cold is rendered through wet stockings, a smoking lamp, breath visible in the air, water under the cracked pane. The intimacy is rendered through lard rubbed in with a hard thumb, bread held under an armpit. The hand-holding moment is the emotional climax and it is well-earned. The comparison — "Not like a lady takes a hand, light, but like you hold bread under your armpit to warm it" — is the chapter's best metaphor, and it works because it is specific, class-inflected, and physically accurate. It does not reach for poetry; it reaches for the right comparison from Eliza's world. The following sentence — "No one has held my hands like that since my father died. In Falmouth the old lady had me rub her feet. My aunt held my wrist to hit it." — is devastating in its compression and earns its darkness completely. The chapter's weaknesses are minor. The opening weather-setting is slightly functional — "It blew all day and then at four the rain came sideways and didn't stop" is competent but not exceptional. The section where Althea talks about the house's history and Sutcliffe locking her in the stillroom is slightly summary-ish, though it serves the plan's purpose of showing intimacy accruing through shared mundane complaint. The line "I was in bed by near five, shivering, gloves clutched" is a touch abrupt as a final sentence, though the image of the stitched finger "holding" is the right note to end on. The plan's instruction to use the worn-through glove as the single recurring object is honored: the glove appears, is re-stitched badly, and the final image returns to it. This is disciplined symbolic economy. One small concern: the chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth in its execution. The plan called for Eliza to do things that are "not admirable" — here she steals tea and tallow, which is mild. The chapter is warmer than the plan's stated intention of keeping things "lopsided" and costly. But this is a minor deviation; the plan also specified Chapter 4 as the intimacy chapter, and the warmth is appropriate to that function. Overall this is a chapter of genuine literary quality: specific, unsentimental, emotionally precise, with strong dialogue and no significant failures of craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By mid-November the rag was in every night. I stopped getting locked out because I stopped being late. I came in at seven fifty-five and sat on my cot and listened to Sutcliffe bolt it, and then waited an hour and went out through it again from inside. Up the pipe and in. It was faster than asking to be let out. My wrist got better enough to hold a needle but it clicks now when I bend it. I think it will always click. We had a use. In the mornings I taught Thomas his Latin which he hates and Alice her letters which she writes backwards still. I was not good at it. In the first week I tried. By the second I was tired because I was up half the night, and I let them run. Thomas ran to the cove and came back with his boots full of sand. Alice bit the girl. Sutcliffe blamed me for that. Sutcliffe looks at me when I come into the kitchen for meat as if she is waiting for me to apologise for something. I never know what. My meat is smaller now. I eat it in the schoolroom and Alice watches my mouth. At night I climbed. It got easy. Not easy like not scary, easy like a chore you know. Right hand to top bracket, don't touch the middle, right foot high on the granite where there is a toe-hole Thomas's father must have cut, left foot to sill, knees out. I learned to knot my skirts higher so they didn't catch. I learned which night the wind would come off the sea and make the sill slick with salt and then I went slower and cursed steadily all the way up. I learned to carry things in my bodice because my pockets get turned out. A chop wrapped in paper leaks grease under your stays but stays warm. The middle bracket held. I stopped looking at it. What I brought: tea pinched from the caddy every two days, then every day when Sutcliffe stopped counting. A bit of paper. A meat chop on Thursdays when there is roast and Sutcliffe is mellow with fat. Once a bit of tobacco I found in the yard that Althea said was not tobacco but dried seaweed and smoked anyway and made her cough. What she gave: she mended my petticoat hem where I tore it, big uneven stitches that held. She showed me how to make a fire out of paper and one stick that would burn for an hour without smoke so Sutcliffe wouldn't smell it. She re-did the bandage on my wrist till I told her it was done. And talk. She talks and talks when she has someone. Not about how she feels. About who took what spoon in 1852. About how to keep rats out of linen - peppermint oil, which we don't have. About Thomas when he was four and ate her button. One night I asked her about walking to the cove. She said, "I was wet. That's all." Then she said, "The sea was very loud. I thought if I went in it would be quiet." We left it. In December it was black at four. The house leaked everywhere. My attic basin was ice in the morning. I broke it with my hairbrush. I had one chilblain burst on my right foot and bled into my stocking and had no other stockings dry. I wore it. The candle allowance ran out because I burned one up there every night. I told Sutcliffe the draught took it and she gave me another and marked it in her book with her hard pencil. That week my bleeding came. It always comes heavy the first day and I get a pain low down like someone pinching from inside. The rags I have are thin and my aunt taught me to wash them cold so they don't stain. It was that first day, and raining, and I was already late because Alice had cried till her nose bled. I should not have gone up. I went up. I sat on my cot till the house was quiet and then my belly cramped so bad I had to stop halfway up the pipe with my forehead against the iron, rain running into my mouth, boots clenched in teeth, glove hand holding. The rust under my palm was rough and cut a little. I got in and was so cross I could hardly speak. Althea had her kettle on. She looked at me. "You're cross," she said. "I have the curse," I said, because I was too tired to say it nice. My mother used to call it that. She did not tut or look away like my aunt. She just nodded and pushed the chair closer to the little fire. She said, "Sit. Knees open. It's easier." I did. I sat with my skirts up a bit and my hands between my thighs, which I know is unseemly. She got out a thing she had hoarded - a scrap of flannel bigger than the one in the window, warmed it by the spirit lamp, gave it me to hold against my belly. We didn't read. She didn't ask for tea. She talked about her own bleedings, how they stopped last year and she was glad. The wind got under the cracked pane and lifted the paper. We both watched it. After a bit she said, "You don't have to come up anymore. Sutcliffe hasn't locked you out in weeks." I said, "I know." She said, "Then why do you?" I had the warm flannel dripping heat into me and her tallow on the runners and my gloves with the thick stitches. I could hear Sutcliffe's pump downstairs even up here if I listened hard. Four beats then a pause. I tried to say because I like the room where no one tells me to be smaller, but it sounded foolish in my head. I said, "I don't know." She said, "Yes you do," in that mean fond way she has, and let me keep the flannel. I climbed down after, slow, cramping still, blood already leaking despite the rags because climbing pulls. At the bottom I stood in mud and thought I would have to wash everything in cold water come morning and it would stain anyway. In January Thomas asked me, "Do you love Mrs. Ashcombe?" We were in the schoolroom and Alice was asleep with her head on the table, mouth open. I said, "Don't be daft." He said, "Sutcliffe says you are up there." I looked at his big ears, red with cold. I said, "Sutcliffe says a lot." He said, "Are you coming down the pipe tonight? It's going to freeze." He was right. It did freeze. The pipe next night was white and my hands stuck to it. I spat to get them loose. But I did not stop.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of sustained, disciplined prose. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Style** The first-person voice is exceptionally well-maintained. Eliza's narration is clipped, bodily, and unsentimental in exactly the way the plan demanded. "My meat is smaller now. I eat it in the schoolroom and Alice watches my mouth." This is devastating in its compression - class humiliation rendered through a child's gaze at a hungry woman. The sentences are short and declarative when Eliza is in control, and the occasional run-on ("I learned to knot my skirts higher so they didn't catch. I learned which night the wind would come off the sea and make the sill slick with salt and then I went slower and cursed steadily all the way up") earns its length by mimicking the accumulation of learned habit. The plan called for "plain, with occasional run-ons when she's anxious or cold" and this is precisely what's delivered. There's no thesaurus Gothic here. No "frisson." The word "unseemly" appears once, used by Eliza herself in self-awareness, which is exactly right. **The Climbing as Chore** The plan's central ambition - "make the climbing a repeatable, shitty, specific chore that slowly becomes chosen" - is fully realized. The passage about the pipe becoming "easy like a chore you know" is excellent. The specificity is earned: "don't touch the middle," "the toe-hole Thomas's father must have cut," "I learned to carry things in my bodice because my pockets get turned out." This is not romanticized. A chop wrapped in paper leaks grease under your stays. The rust cuts her palm. These are not decorative details; they are the texture of a life. **The Menstruation Scene** This is the chapter's centerpiece and it's handled with extraordinary care. The plan called for Eliza to climb "while cramping, furious" and the execution is better than the plan: "my belly cramped so bad I had to stop halfway up the pipe with my forehead against the iron, rain running into my mouth, boots clenched in teeth, glove hand holding." The physical specificity here is not gratuitous - it earns the intimacy that follows. Althea's response ("Sit. Knees open. It's easier.") is perfect: practical, unsentimental, and more tender than any declaration could be. The warmed flannel is the chapter's emotional climax, and it lands because it has been prepared for by all the preceding physical discomfort. **The Central Exchange** "You don't have to come up anymore. Sutcliffe hasn't locked you out in weeks." / "I know." / "Then why do you?" This is the chapter's thematic core, and the plan specified that Althea would ask this question point-blank and Eliza cannot answer. The execution is faithful and effective. "I tried to say because I like the room where no one tells me to be smaller, but it sounded foolish in my head" - this is excellent interiority. The answer she gives ("I don't know") and Althea's response ("Yes you do") in "that mean fond way she has" is exactly the relationship dynamic the profiles established. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is right for this story. "I was wet. That's all." / "The sea was very loud. I thought if I went in it would be quiet." This is the chapter's most emotionally loaded exchange, and it's handled with restraint. The two women "left it" - no processing, no comfort, just acknowledgment. This is mature writing. Thomas's intrusion at the end - "Do you love Mrs. Ashcombe?" - is well-placed. His voice is consistent with his profile (practical, slightly snitching, speaks too fast in implication). "Sutcliffe says you are up there" is a good threat-that-isn't-quite-a-threat. **Weaknesses** The chapter is almost uniformly excellent, which makes the few weak moments stand out. "She talks and talks when she has someone. Not about how she feels. About who took what spoon in 1852." The 1852 detail is good, but "she talks and talks" is slightly tell-don't-show - we're told she talks rather than shown a sample. The plan called for time compression and this is achieved, but the compression occasionally flattens what could be more textured. The line "I said, 'I don't know'" followed by "She said, 'Yes you do,' in that mean fond way she has" - "that mean fond way she has" is a slight intrusion of retrospective characterization that edges toward telling. It's minor but noticeable. The ending - "But I did not stop" - is clean and right. The frozen pipe, the spitting to get hands loose, the refusal to stop: this is the chapter's final image and it earns its plainness. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: time compression from November to January, the climbing becoming automatic, the private economy between the women, the heavy period and the climb, Althea's question. The plan mentioned Eliza telling Althea "real gossip about the house" and Althea telling her "how to make Sutcliffe leave her alone" - these are gestured at but not fully rendered, which is a minor gap. The plan also mentioned Eliza being "tired all the time" and "using too many candles" - both present. The candle lie to Sutcliffe is a nice specific detail. **Overall Assessment** This is genuinely accomplished literary fiction. The writing is controlled, specific, and emotionally intelligent. It avoids every trap the plan identified - no symbolic overload, no likeability filtering, no thesaurus Gothic. The physical world is rendered with precision and the emotional world emerges from it rather than being imposed upon it. The chapter does exactly what it sets out to do and does it with considerable skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
It froze hard at the end of January. I woke and the basin was solid, and the chamber pot in my room had frozen over with a skin. I broke it with the brush. My breath stayed in the attic till noon. Thomas said the cove was white. Alice's cough got worse and Mrs. Sutcliffe gave her honey that was mostly wax. All that week the pipe was white too, and I had to spit on my palms to make them stick and unstick. On Thursday night I went up late because Alice had been sick on the floor and I had to clean it with cold water. My right glove, the one Althea stitched, had come undone again and the wool stuck out like a tongue. Althea had been cross that week. She said her ankles hurt and she couldn't get warm. She said the rag was letting in too much draught now, did I think she should take it out. I said no. On Friday it thawed a little at midday and then froze again at night, which makes stone slick. Thomas told me. He said, "Don't go up." He said it in the schoolroom in his fast way, looking at my wrist. I said I would not. I meant it for a while. At supper Mrs. Sutcliffe watched me eat. She gives me less now because there is less. She said, "That cupboard tea is going quick." I said, "Is it?" She said, "You tell me, Miss Finch." I went up to my cot and sat till the house went quiet. The keys, the step, the girl coughing. Then the silence that means Sutcliffe is in bed but not asleep, listening for rats. I told myself not to. Then I told myself I would just go and tell her I wasn't coming tomorrow because the pipe was bad. I put on my stockings that were still damp and my boots round my neck instead of teeth because my gums bled from cold. I went out the back door. I unbolted it from inside. The yard was ice. The moon was up and made everything sharp. The pipe had ice inside it. You could hear it creak. The rag was in. I got up half way. Right hand to top bracket, don't touch middle, foot to toe-hole. It held. My skirts had frozen stiff and wouldn't knot, so they hung and caught wind. I was at the sill, had my palm heel to the sash, remembering out and up, when the lamp came on below me. Not in the nursery. In the scullery. Yellow light spilling out onto the ice. And Mrs. Sutcliffe's voice, loud: "Come down off there, Miss Finch." I froze with one knee on the stone lip and one foot dangling over nothing. My heart went so fast I thought I would be sick off the side. She was standing in the yard in her big shawl over her night things, boots on bare feet, holding the lamp up. Her hair was down and grey and wild. She looks bigger without it scraped back. "Come down. Now. Not up. Down." I could not go up. Althea must have heard. The sash was half up. I saw her cap inside. I had to go down the way I came, which is harder than up because you cannot see the brackets and you have to trust your feet. I went slow. My frozen skirt tangled. The middle bracket gave out a loud shriek when my foot brushed it. The whole pipe shuddered. Sutcliffe made a noise. I got down the last bit by jumping. My good wrist took it. Pain up to elbow. We stood there. Me in mud and ice, skirts showing my drawers, boot laces still round my neck, glove finger flopping, breathing like a dog. Her with lamp. "You think I don't know that mark?" she said, and she wasn't shouting now. She was breathing hard too. She pointed to the sill where my muddy knee had smeared. "That's my boot from forty year ago, darling. Only smaller. That middle bracket will go. I have said it will go since '62 when my Tom said he'd fix it. He did not fix it. Now look." She grabbed the pipe and shook it. It did move. Ice fell off it. "Come in," she said. "You will get death." She took me round to kitchen, not through back door but side door to scullery. She pushed me onto stool by range where it was warm. The girl was there half asleep. She set the lamp down and looked at me properly. I stank of iron and cold and grease from the tallow on my hands. "How long?" she said. Since November. Since you locked me out the first time. I said, "A month." She snorted. "Liar." She put kettle on. Her hands shook a little. I saw. "Do you know what you are doing?" she said finally, to the range. "That up there is not a sweetheart. Mrs. Althea is not right. Since Mr. Thomas died she went wrong. She walked down to the cove in February in her shift to drown. Groom found her up to her knees talking about the noise. Dr Rowe said quiet. No excitement. No stairs, no visitors. She said keep her above. So I kept her above. Not locked, kept. So she lives." She slapped the spoon down. "And you go and fill her with strong tea and gossip and get her worked up so she doesn't eat and paces all day. She has been worse since you started." I thought of Althea counting gulls, Althea giving me her flannel, Althea saying the sea was too loud. "She is not worse," I said, and my voice cracked. "She is bored to death. You have left her there like jam in a pantry." Sutcliffe looked at me, really looked, and for a second I thought she would hit me. She has big hands. "Heel of hand," she said, pointing at my hand, which was still black with iron. "Who taught you that?" She knew. I said nothing. She said, "I did that climb to meet my Tom. When this was new iron. I was sixteen and thought my mother would kill me if she knew. My mother did know and beat me raw." She shook her head. "You girls never look down." She poured water. "You will finish the month. You will not go up again. That pipe comes down tomorrow. I will have Will nail that window shut for draughts. You will have a character that says you kept time and were clean enough and no more. Do you hear me?" She meant no character. No governess gets taken with "clean enough." My eyes filled. I did not want them to. I put my hands between my thighs without thinking. She said, softer, "You can use the front door if you want to leave early. It is not locked." Then she made me climb down again. Not up the pipe, but she made me show her how I got up, so she could see. She stood in the ice yard with lamp and shouted, "Arse out! Arse out like you mean it! Like you are sitting on the sill!" I did it, skirt rucked, petticoat showing, drawers muddy. The girl watched from window. Mid-way, foot on the loose middle bracket, it went. Not broke - pulled clean out of granite with a puff of dust and ice. The pipe swung. I was high enough that I fell onto the scullery roof edge, then slithered off into mud. The pipe clanged down beside me like a bell. I lay there, winded, left wrist again sprained, hair in mud. Sutcliffe came over and stood over me and said, "There. I told you it would go." Althea's window above was dark. The rag was gone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of sustained craft. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Style** The first-person voice is rigorously maintained throughout. The prose is plain, bodily, and specific in exactly the way the plan demanded. "I broke it with the brush." "My breath stayed in the attic till noon." These are not decorative sentences; they are functional and accumulative. The rhythm is short and declarative, with occasional run-ons that feel earned rather than accidental. The chapter avoids the thesaurus Gothic the plan explicitly warned against. There is no "frisson," no "palpable sorrow." When Eliza's eyes fill, the narration notes it with self-disgust: "I did not want them to." **Character Consistency** Eliza is exactly as profiled: she lies to Sutcliffe ("A month"), she puts her hands between her thighs without thinking and then registers it as unseemly, she cannot ask for help until caught. The detail of the boot laces around her neck rather than in her teeth because her gums bleed from cold is a small, perfect specificity that rewards close reading. Sutcliffe is rendered with full complexity - the shaking hands she tries to hide, the revelation about her own climb at sixteen, the cruelty of "clean enough" as a character reference, and then the strange, almost tender instruction to demonstrate the climb again. The line "Arse out! Arse out like you mean it! Like you are sitting on the sill!" is one of the best lines in the chapter - it is simultaneously humiliating, practical, and reveals Sutcliffe's own embodied knowledge of the climb. Althea is present only as a cap glimpsed through a window and a rag removed, which is exactly right for this chapter. **The Plan's Execution** The chapter follows its plan closely. Sutcliffe finds the muddy footprints (though here it's a muddy knee smear on the sill rather than footprints, a small deviation that actually works better). The confrontation in the yard rather than the nursery is a smart choice - it's more exposed, more humiliating, more physical. The revelation about Althea walking into the sea is delivered as planned. The dismissal without a real character is there. The pipe coming down is there, and it's handled brilliantly - not as a dramatic climax but as a kind of grim punctuation: "There. I told you it would go." The plan called for Eliza to climb down "with Sutcliffe watching, humiliated, skirt rucked, not romantic at all," and the chapter delivers this with the added indignity of Sutcliffe making her demonstrate the climb again, which is crueler and more interesting than simply watching her descend once. **Dialogue** The dialogue is excellent throughout. "You tell me, Miss Finch" is a perfect line - it contains accusation, knowledge, and a kind of weary patience. "That's my boot from forty year ago, darling. Only smaller." The word "darling" here is devastating in context - it's not affectionate, it's the condescension of someone who has seen this before and is tired of it. "You girls never look down" is a line that works on multiple levels without straining for it. The exchange about Althea - "She is not worse. She is bored to death. You have left her there like jam in a pantry" - is the one moment where the metaphor is slightly more literary than Eliza's established voice, but it's not egregious, and "jam in a pantry" has the right domestic specificity to feel like something Eliza would actually say. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter earns its emotional weight through accumulation of physical detail rather than declaration. The moment where Sutcliffe's hands shake and Eliza notices is quietly devastating. The revelation about Althea's attempted drowning recontextualizes everything without melodrama. The final image - Eliza lying in mud, winded, the pipe clanged down beside her "like a bell," Althea's window dark, the rag gone - is genuinely affecting. The simile "like a bell" is the one moment of slight poeticism, and it works because it's unexpected and precise: a bell is both loud and ceremonial, and the pipe's fall has the quality of an announcement. **Weaknesses** The chapter is very strong, but there are minor issues. The line "like jam in a pantry" is slightly more writerly than Eliza's voice typically allows. The transition from Sutcliffe's revelation about Althea to her practical dismissal of Eliza is handled efficiently but could have breathed a little more - the emotional weight of what Sutcliffe has just disclosed (that she essentially imprisoned Althea out of protective love) is somewhat rushed past. The girl watching from the window is mentioned twice but never quite lands as a presence - she's there for witness but feels slightly underdeveloped as a detail. These are small complaints against a chapter that is doing almost everything right. **Originality** The chapter is genuinely surprising in its choices. The decision to have Sutcliffe make Eliza demonstrate the climb again - not to punish her but seemingly to confirm her own knowledge of the pipe's danger - is an unexpected and psychologically rich choice. The pipe actually falling is handled with perfect anti-climax. The chapter consistently refuses the romantic or the redemptive. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The pipe was gone in the morning. Will the farm boy had taken it away in two pieces on a barrow. There were four holes in the granite where the brackets had been. They looked like mouths. The ice in them melted and ran down. I stayed. Mrs. Sutcliffe said two weeks, as she always does with governesses, to be proper. I would finish the month and go at the end of January. She said it in the kitchen in front of the girl so I would not argue. My left wrist swelled again from the fall. It clicked when I made a fist. She saw me wince and said nothing. She locked the third floor door. Not with a small key, with the big house key she wears that opens everything. She did it at ten in the morning while I was teaching Thomas how to do sums, and I heard it go. A clunk that went down through the house. "That is for draughts," she said when she came past the schoolroom later. "Will is nailing the nursery sash shut as well for draughts." Thomas looked up at me over his slate. He had ink on his chin. After dinner, which was now just bread and lard for me at the schoolroom table, he asked, "Where do you go at night now?" "Nowhere," I said. He said, "Mrs. Althea paces." I could hear it too. Up through the boards. Her heavy step, back and forth, back and forth in the nursery to the bedroom and back. Not quick. Slow. Like she was counting. Before, when I was up there, she sat mostly. Now she paced. I tried to be good. I kept time. I was in at seven forty. I was out of the kitchen when they were taking dinner. I darned the stockings neat so Sutcliffe could not fault them. I made Alice write her a's the right way round six times. She cried and I told her to stop snivelling and she cried more and Sutcliffe took her away and said I was too sharp. I tried to use the front door. On Sunday Will was told to open it to air the hall because of damp. It is a big door with a black handle. It has swollen shut. Will had to kick the bottom. When it came open cold air with sea in it blew in and lifted the rush mat. Sutcliffe said to me, "If you want to go to service you may go out that way, Miss Finch, as you like front doors." So I did. I put on my one dress and my shawl and my boots, both dry for once, and I walked out through the front door with Thomas and Alice behind me like I was a lady. Outside the step was green where no one treads. The sea was grey and flat. The wind got in my teeth. It felt stupid. My skirts caught on the big threshold and I had to hitch them. Thomas laughed and I wanted to hit him. At the church they stared at us. Mrs. Ashcombe's pew is big and empty except for us three now. I sat and the vicar preached on obedience and Alice fell asleep and drooled on my sleeve. We came back the same way. Sutcliffe had the front door shut again and bolted from inside. We had to go round to the back. My boots were full again. On Monday I wrote a letter. I wrote it at night on my bed with the candle Sutcliffe counts. It was to Althea, but I could not get it to her. I said: *Mrs. Ashcombe - I am sorry I got caught. The middle bracket went as you said and I fell. Mrs. Sutcliffe says you are worse since I came. I do not think you are worse. I think you were bored before and you are bored now but you had tea. I kept your flannel. It is stained. I will wash it when I have hot water. Eliza Finch.* I read it through. It was true and it was also not enough. I tore it into bits and put the bits into the pot because there is no fire to burn them. On Tuesday Althea got sick. No one told me. I found out because the girl took a basin up and came down quick and said Mrs. Althea won't take broth. I was scrubbing out Alice's shoes and I stopped with the brush in hand.
